paper_title: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz
paper_excerpt: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li ( lixiang.62770689@bytedance.com ) ByteDance Inc https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8572-1875 Zhe Li ByteDance Inc Ji Chen Peking University https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1603-1963 Article Keywords: Posted Date: May 31st, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-1689060/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The paper claims that the neural network results for the homogeneous electron gas (HEG) "outperform many traditional ab initio methods" and achieve "error of less than 1%" using BF-DMC as reference. However, subsequent independent analysis in the literature demonstrates that the DeepSolid method (referred to as "LiNet") does not actually improve upon backflow DMC (BF-DMC) results across the full density regime for N=54 electrons, undermining the claimed superiority of the method for this benchmark system. "Overall, our neural network performs very well, with an error of less than 1% in a wide range of density (see Supplementary Table 11)." This claim presents the correlation error relative to BF-DMC, but the framing suggests the neural network method is achieving higher accuracy than traditional methods. A subsequent study by Pescia et al. (2024) in Physical Review B directly compared DeepSolid (labeled "LiNet") against BF-DMC for N=54 electrons and found that "This is in stark contrast to the (FermiNet-based) architecture of [27] (dubbed LiNet in the following), which does not improve upon BF-DMC energies over the whole density regime" [[1]](#citation-1). This directly contradicts the paper's implication of superior performance. "the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches" The abstract makes a broad claim of outperforming "many traditional ab initio methods." However, for HEG specifically, the neural network VMC results remain at or slightly above BF-DMC energies due to the inherent variational bias. The paper's HEG Table 11 in the supplementary material shows energies that are systematically higher (less accurate) than BF-DMC at most densities, which is expected for a variational method but contradicts claims of "outperforming" state-of-the-art approaches. "The state-of-the-art results, namely VMC with backflow correlation (BF) [22] and distinguishable cluster with double excitations (DCD) [23] are also plotted for comparison, and the most accurate BF-DMC result is used as the reference energy of correlation error." By using BF-DMC as the reference and only comparing against BF-VMC and DCD, the paper obscures the fact that the neural network VMC cannot provide energies lower than BF-DMC due to the variational principle. The message-passing NQS study [[1]](#citation-1) shows that more recent neural network approaches can achieve lower energies than DeepSolid while using fewer parameters, suggesting the DeepSolid architecture is not optimal for this system. References: [1] Pescia, G., Nys, J., Kim, J., Lovato, A. & Carleo, G. Message-Passing Neural Quantum States for the Homogeneous Electron Gas. [Phys. Rev. B 110, 035108 (2024)](https://journals.aps.org/prb/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevB.110.035108); arXiv:2305.07240.
annotator_comments: While claims are actually fine, I marked it as not correct as the paper they use to support these claims appeared two years after the presented one. The field was evolving very fast and in 2022 the presented results could be considered as good. The only thing one could move on with is the claims of improving upon the state-of-the-art. The auhtors should clarify what exactly they mean with it.

Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The paper presents the comparison of graphene cohesive energy against "the experimental value [21]" from Ref. [21] (Dappe et al., 2006). However, Ref. [21] is a DFT study using the "Local-orbital occupancy formulation of density functional theory," not an experimental measurement. This misattribution undermines the paper's validation claims for graphene. "The calculated results are plotted in Fig. 2d along with the experimental value [21], and it shows that our neural network can deal with graphene very well, producing a cohesive energy of graphene within 0.1 eV/atom to the experimental reference" The paper explicitly states that Reference [21] provides an "experimental value" for graphene cohesive energy. However, examining the reference citation shows it is: "Yanick Dappe, Rafal Oswalcowski, P. Pou, José Ortega, R. Pérez, and Fernando Flores. Local-orbital occupancy formulation of density functional theory: Application to si, c, and graphene. Phys. Rev. B, 73, 06 2006." This is clearly a theoretical DFT paper, not an experimental study. Actual experimental determination of graphene cohesive energy involves complex measurements of graphite exfoliation energies and layer binding, not direct cohesive energy measurements. "Experiment cohesive energy is from Ref. [21]." This caption in Figure 2 explicitly labels the reference as "Experiment cohesive energy," which is factually incorrect since Ref. [21] presents DFT calculations. Using DFT-calculated values as "experimental" references for validating a method that aims to exceed DFT accuracy is methodologically problematic and could inflate the apparent agreement with experiment.
annotator_comments: The valid point is that authors refer to ref. 21 as providing the experimental value. However, ref. 21 takes the corresponding reference value from a different work. This could be requested to correct. But, in general, the comment/critique by this reviewer is rather pointless.

paper_title: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz
paper_excerpt: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li ( lixiang.62770689@bytedance.com ) ByteDance Inc https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8572-1875 Zhe Li ByteDance Inc Ji Chen Peking University https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1603-1963 Article Keywords: Posted Date: May 31st, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-1689060/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: For the LiH crystal, the paper uses a HF-based finite-size correction from a 2×2×2 to extrapolate thermodynamic properties, but performs the larger 3×3×3 calculation at only a single geometry (equilibrium lattice constant) and at the Γ-point only, without twist averaging. Standard practice in QMC studies of solids typically employs twist-averaged boundary conditions (TABC) for 3D systems to reduce finite-size errors, as the authors themselves use for graphene. "For further validation, we have also computed directly the 3 × 3 × 3 supercell of LiH at its equilibrium length 4.061Å, which contains 108 electrons." The 3×3×3 calculation is performed at only a single lattice constant, making it impossible to assess how the method performs across the equation of state for this larger system. The comparison "also very close to the experimental value −4.759 eV" relies heavily on the accuracy of the HF-based finite-size correction rather than demonstrating convergence with system size through twist averaging as done for graphene. "The calculations are performed on a 2 × 2 × 2 supercell of graphene using twist average boundary condition (TABC) [25] in conjunction with structure factor S(k) correction [26] (see Supplementary Fig. 3) to reduce the finite-size error." The paper applies TABC for graphene but not for LiH, despite LiH being a 3D system where finite-size errors are typically more severe. A recent transferable neural wavefunction study [[2]](#citation-2) demonstrated that for LiH specifically, joint optimization across multiple twists and system sizes significantly reduces computational cost while improving accuracy. The absence of TABC for LiH in this work represents an inconsistency in methodology across systems. "Using our neural network, we first simulate the equation of state of LiH on a 2 × 2 × 2 supercell, as shown in Fig. 2f. In addition, we employ a standard finite-size correction based on Hartree-Fock calculations of a large supercell" The finite-size correction methodology relies on HF calculations rather than using neural network calculations at multiple supercell sizes to extrapolate to the thermodynamic limit. While this is computationally pragmatic, it introduces uncertainty about how much of the reported cohesive energy accuracy is attributable to the neural network versus the HF correction. Standard QMC practice often involves calculating multiple system sizes to directly quantify and extrapolate finite-size effects [[3]](#citation-3). References: [2] Gerard, L., Scherbela, M., Sutterud, H., Foulkes, W. M. C. & Grohs, P. Transferable neural wavefunctions for solids. [Nat. Comput. Sci. 5, 1147–1157 (2025)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43588-025-00872-z). [3] Chiesa, S., Ceperley, D. M., Martin, R. M. & Holzmann, M. Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions. [Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404 (2006)](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404).
annotator_comments: Overall, I agree with this comment. However, I would not claim that what the authors do is completely wrong. One may simply ask for doing that additional set of experiments.

Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The paper does not provide clear information about the computational resources required (GPU hours, wall-clock time) for the calculations presented, making it difficult to assess the practical applicability of the method and compare with other approaches. Given that subsequent work has noted the high computational cost of the approach, this omission is significant for reproducibility and assessment of the method's practical value. "Concrete code of this work is developed on Github at https://github.com/bytedance/DeepSolid." While code availability is commendable, the paper lacks quantitative information about computational resources needed for each system studied. The acknowledgments thank "ByteDance AML team specially for their technical and computing support," suggesting substantial computational resources were used, but these are not quantified in the paper or supplementary materials. * Quote from Supplementary Table 2: "Homogeneous electron gas: (256, 32), 3 layers, 1 determinant, Batch size 4096, Training steps 3e5" While hyperparameters are provided, the actual wall-clock time and hardware used are not specified. A subsequent study [[2]](#citation-2) noted that "DeepSolid required over 80,000 GPU hours to study a single system," highlighting the significant computational burden that is not explicitly communicated in the original paper. This information is critical for readers to assess the practical utility of the method. "All the expectation values for distribution |Ψ|² are evaluated via the Monte Carlo approach, and then the energy and wavefunction is optimized using the modified KFAC optimizer" The KFAC optimizer and 200,000-400,000 training steps combined with batch sizes of 4096-8192 imply substantial computational requirements. Without explicit reporting of GPU hours or comparison to training costs of alternative methods, readers cannot properly evaluate the cost-accuracy tradeoff of this approach relative to alternatives such as traditional QMC or other neural network architectures. References: [2] Gerard, L., Scherbela, M., Sutterud, H., Foulkes, W. M. C. & Grohs, P. Transferable neural wavefunctions for solids. [Nat. Comput. Sci. 5, 1147–1157 (2025)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43588-025-00872-z).

paper_title: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz
paper_excerpt: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li ( lixiang.62770689@bytedance.com ) ByteDance Inc https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8572-1875 Zhe Li ByteDance Inc Ji Chen Peking University https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1603-1963 Article Keywords: Posted Date: May 31st, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-1689060/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The paper reports statistical uncertainties for energy values using parenthetical notation (e.g., "-0.551677(1)"), but does not discuss the variance of the local energy, which is an important metric for assessing wavefunction quality in variational Monte Carlo. High variance can indicate poor optimization or wavefunction quality issues that are not captured by the mean energy alone. From Supplementary Table 3: "-0.551677(1)" for H10 chain energy The statistical uncertainties reported are very small (on the order of 10⁻⁶ Hartree), but without reporting the variance of the local energy, it is unclear whether these small error bars reflect a truly converged high-quality wavefunction or simply long sampling runs. The zero-variance principle states that the exact ground state wavefunction has zero variance in the local energy; reporting variance would provide important information about wavefunction quality. "Besides energy minimization, stochastic reconfiguration optimization [37] has also been widely adopted and proved to be much more efficient" While stochastic reconfiguration is mentioned as the optimization method, there is no discussion of convergence metrics beyond energy. In neural network VMC studies, reporting both the mean local energy and its variance (or the ratio of variance reduction compared to Hartree-Fock) is standard practice to demonstrate wavefunction quality [[4]](#citation-4), [[5]](#citation-5). The absence of variance reporting makes it difficult to assess whether the wavefunction has reached a high-quality local minimum. "Training curves of the 2 × 2 × 2 LiH crystal is plotted in Supplementary Fig. 4." The supplementary figures show training curves for energy convergence but do not include variance plots. Standard practice in neural network QMC papers typically includes both energy and variance convergence plots to demonstrate wavefunction quality. For instance, the FermiNet paper [[4]](#citation-4) explicitly discusses variance reduction as a measure of wavefunction quality. This omission makes it harder to fully assess the quality of the converged wavefunctions. References: [4] Pfau, D., Spencer, J. S., Matthews, A. G. D. G. & Foulkes, W. M. C. Ab initio solution of the many-electron Schrödinger equation with deep neural networks. [Phys. Rev. Res. 2, 033429 (2020)](https://journals.aps.org/prresearch/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevResearch.2.033429). [5] Hermann, J., Schätzle, Z. & Noé, F. Deep-neural-network solution of the electronic Schrödinger equation. [Nat. Chem. 12, 891–897 (2020)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41557-020-0544-y).

paper_title: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz
paper_excerpt: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li ( lixiang.62770689@bytedance.com ) ByteDance Inc https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8572-1875 Zhe Li ByteDance Inc Ji Chen Peking University https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1603-1963 Article Keywords: Posted Date: May 31st, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-1689060/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The manuscript’s headline accuracy claims for *cohesive energies* (graphene and LiH) are not fully supported because the key isolated-atom reference energies are imported from an external neural-QMC work rather than computed within the paper’s own method/code, which undermines attribution of the reported agreement to the proposed periodic ansatz and weakens reproducibility of the cohesive-energy results. “Graphene is arguably the most famous two-dimensional system (Fig. 2c) receiving broad attention in the past two decades for its mechanical, electronic, and chemical applications [24]. Here we carry out simulations to estimate its cohesive energy, which measures the strength of C-C chemical bonding and long-range dispersion interactions. The calculations are performed on a \( 2 \times 2 \) supercell of graphene using twist average boundary condition (TABC) [25] in conjunction with structure factor S(k) correction [26] (see Supplementary Fig. 3) to reduce the finite-size error. The calculated results are plotted in Fig. 2d along with the experimental value [21], and it shows that our neural network can deal with graphene very well, producing a cohesive energy of graphene within 0.1 eV/atom to the experimental reference (see Supplementary Table 6). We also plotted the results with PBC, namely the Γ point only result, which deviates from the experiment data by 1.25 eV/atom.” This is a strong quantitative conclusion (“within 0.1 eV/atom”) that depends on how the cohesive energy is constructed, not just on the bulk/supercell total energy. Cohesive energies are differences between large total energies, so the atomic reference must be consistent and clearly attributable to the proposed method to justify the claim. Here, the paper presents the cohesive-energy agreement as evidence that “our neural network can deal with graphene very well,” which implicitly attributes the result to the proposed periodic ansatz. If the atomic reference energy is taken from a different method/pipeline, the claim no longer isolates the contribution of the proposed approach. The reader cannot tell whether the agreement is driven primarily by the graphene calculation, the external atomic reference, or cancellation between them. This weakens the inferential link from the graphene cohesive-energy number to the paper’s core methodological contribution. “The final results are listed in Supplementary Table 6. The energy of an isolated carbon atom is taken from Ref. [2], \( E = -37.84471 \) Hartree.” This sentence explicitly confirms that a crucial input to the graphene cohesive energy (the isolated-atom energy) is not produced by the present work’s workflow, but copied from another paper. While using an external benchmark value can be reasonable for contextual comparison, it materially changes what is being validated: the reported cohesive energy is no longer an end-to-end result of the proposed periodic neural network ansatz. Because the paper’s conclusion is phrased as the method “producing” an accurate cohesive energy, the computational provenance of all terms in the energy difference matters. In particular, any mismatch in Hamiltonian conventions, numerical settings, or physical corrections between the external atom calculation and the present periodic calculation would directly propagate into the cohesive energy. The manuscript does not provide a justification that such mismatches are negligible at the claimed 0.1 eV/atom level. “The energy of \(2 \times 2 \times 2\) LiH is listed in Supplementary Table 8. The energy of an isolated lithium atom is taken from Ref. [2], \(E = -7.47798\) Hartree.” The same pattern occurs for LiH: a cohesive-energy-related reference (the isolated Li atom) is imported from an external work rather than computed with the proposed approach. This makes it difficult to assess the method’s capability on an equal footing, because the cohesive energy is sensitive to the accuracy of *both* the bulk and atomic calculations. Importing atomic energies also impedes reproducibility: a reader re-running the provided DeepSolid configurations would not reproduce the reported cohesive energies without separately sourcing the same atomic numbers from elsewhere. More importantly, the manuscript uses the LiH cohesive-energy agreement as a validation highlight (“very close to the experimental value”), but that agreement is partly contingent on a value computed outside the presented methodology. The paper should either compute these atomic references with the same code path or clearly reframe the claim as “bulk energies + external atomic benchmarks,” not as an end-to-end cohesive-energy prediction. “C -37.84471(5)” [[1]](#ref1) This entry from FermiNet’s Table I matches the carbon atomic energy reused by the reviewed manuscript for its graphene cohesive-energy calculation. It demonstrates that a key term in the reported cohesive energy comes from an external neural-network VMC workflow rather than from the reviewed paper’s periodic-solid method. That matters because it changes what is being validated: the cohesive-energy agreement partially inherits accuracy from a separate implementation with its own training, sampling, and numerical choices. Even if the external atomic energy is highly accurate, the resulting cohesive energy is not an end-to-end prediction attributable solely to the proposed periodic ansatz. In addition, any mismatch in physical conventions or numerical corrections between the two workflows would directly enter the energy difference. Therefore, the cohesive-energy benchmark provides weaker evidence for the proposed method than implied by the manuscript’s interpretation. “The determination of the cohesive energy is obtained as being the difference between the energy of the isolated nickel and that of the bul.k per unit atom.” [[4]](#ref4) This sentence states the standard definition of cohesive energy as a difference between an isolated-atom energy and a bulk per-atom energy, highlighting that the atomic reference is an essential part of the reported quantity. Because cohesive energies are differences between large numbers, careful control of consistency between the atomic and bulk computations is needed to avoid spurious agreement. In the reviewed manuscript, the isolated-atom terms are not computed within the same reported workflow, so the cohesive energies are not fully “closed” results of the proposed method. The citation also underscores why importing atomic energies is not a minor bookkeeping choice: it can dominate the error budget for the derived quantity. Therefore, presenting cohesive-energy agreement as a primary accuracy demonstration requires more methodological closure than the paper currently provides. This gap materially weakens the strength of the paper’s validation evidence. References: [1] D. Pfau, J. S. Spencer, A. G. D. G. Matthews, and W. M. C. Foulkes. *Ab-Initio Solution of the Many-Electron Schrödinger Equation with Deep Neural Networks* (arXiv:1909.02487v3). [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf) [2] C. Lin, F. H. Zong, and D. M. Ceperley. *Twist-averaged boundary conditions in continuum quantum Monte Carlo algorithms* (Phys. Rev. E 64, 016702). [https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf](https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf) [4] E. H. Megchiche, C. Mijoule, M. Amarouche, and J.-C. Barthelat. *First-principles calculations of the vacancy formation energy in nickel* (Algerian Journal of Advanced Materials, 2006). [https://hal.science/hal-04289125/document](https://hal.science/hal-04289125/document)

paper_title: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz
paper_excerpt: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li ( lixiang.62770689@bytedance.com ) ByteDance Inc https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8572-1875 Zhe Li ByteDance Inc Ji Chen Peking University https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1603-1963 Article Keywords: Posted Date: May 31st, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-1689060/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The finite-size control for key solid benchmarks (graphene and LiH) is not validated via systematic convergence studies (supercell size and twist/k-point sampling), yet the manuscript draws strong conclusions about near-experimental accuracy; this is a major methodological gap because residual finite-size errors in Coulomb systems can be comparable to (or larger than) the claimed error bars. “The calculations are performed on a \( 2 \times 2 \) supercell of graphene using twist average boundary condition (TABC) [25] in conjunction with structure factor S(k) correction [26] (see Supplementary Fig. 3) to reduce the finite-size error.” This sentence shows that the graphene cohesive-energy claim is based on a single, small supercell (2×2) augmented by correction schemes. For graphene-like systems, finite-size effects can be subtle because the system is not a simple gapped insulator and long-range Coulomb correlations remain important. Merely stating that TABC and an S(k) correction are used does not establish that the residual finite-size bias is below 0.1 eV/atom. The manuscript does not report a convergence check with respect to supercell size (e.g., 2×2 vs 3×3 vs 4×4) or twist-mesh density. Without such a study, the magnitude of the remaining finite-size error is unknown, making the subsequent accuracy conclusion fragile. Given that the main comparative statement is “within 0.1 eV/atom,” the absence of a demonstrated finite-size error bound is a significant methodological omission. “A 3×3 Monkhorst-Pack mesh in the first Brillouin zone of the supercell reciprocal space with Γ point centered is used to approximate the twist average integral, which reads” This indicates that twist averaging is approximated with a modest 3×3 mesh for the supercell. For high-accuracy claims, the adequacy of the twist mesh should be justified by explicit convergence tests, because twist integration error is a controlled numerical approximation. The supplement gives a specific quadrature rule, but does not report how the result changes with a denser mesh (e.g., 4×4 or 6×6) or with different symmetry handling. In addition, for quantities like cohesive energy, the twist-integration error propagates into an energy difference, which may amplify small biases. Without reporting the mesh-convergence behavior, it is not possible to know whether the reported “finite-size error corrected” cohesive energy is stable at the claimed accuracy. This undermines the credibility of the graphene validation as presented. “Using our neural network, we first simulate the equation of state of LiH on a \( 2 \times 2 \times 2 \) supercell, as shown in Fig. 2f. In addition, we employ a standard finite-size correction based on Hartree-Fock calculations of a large supercell (see Supplementary Fig. 5).” This sentence shows that LiH results are primarily based on a 2×2×2 QMC supercell, with an a posteriori Hartree–Fock-based correction rather than an explicit many-body size extrapolation. In Coulomb systems, the leading finite-size effects include both single-particle shell effects and many-body long-range correlation contributions; HF corrections address only part of the problem. The paper does not show QMC energies for multiple LiH supercell sizes (beyond a single 3×3×3 point at one lattice constant) to empirically demonstrate the corrected equation-of-state curve is converged. Because the Birch–Murnaghan fit and derived properties (bulk modulus, cohesive energy) are sensitive to small systematic shifts, the lack of a multi-size convergence assessment is a substantial gap. As a result, the reported agreement with experimental thermodynamic quantities cannot be robustly attributed to the method rather than finite-size modeling choices. “Twist averaging serves to make the decay more regular but does not reduce its overall magnitude that is determined by a charge interacting with the correlation hole of its own image in the nearby supercell.” [[2]](#ref2) This statement from the foundational TABC literature emphasizes that twist averaging primarily regularizes shell effects but does not, by itself, eliminate the dominant long-range Coulomb finite-size contributions. This is directly relevant because the reviewed manuscript leans on TABC (graphene) and HF-based corrections (LiH) to claim near-experimental accuracy from small supercells. The quote highlights that additional many-body finite-size corrections (beyond twist averaging) are typically required to control the Coulomb-image/correlation-hole contributions. While the manuscript mentions an S(k) correction for graphene, it does not provide a quantitative residual-error analysis or show that the correction is sufficient for the claimed accuracy. Therefore, the literature suggests that the paper’s finite-size treatment may be incomplete without further evidence. This undermines the strength of the conclusions drawn from the finite supercell calculations. “Finite-size corrections, both on kinetic and potential energy, can be calculated within a single simulation.” [[3]](#ref3) This quote reflects a well-established viewpoint in QMC finite-size analysis: both kinetic and potential energy contributions can require explicit many-body finite-size corrections grounded in long-wavelength physics. The reviewed manuscript does include some correction ideas (TABC, structure factor correction, HF empirical correction), but it does not demonstrate that these corrections collectively bound residual finite-size error below the levels needed for its strongest claims. In particular, for LiH the manuscript emphasizes HF-based correction, which is not the same as a many-body correction of both kinetic and potential contributions. For graphene, the small twist grid and single supercell size leave open whether both classes of finite-size errors are controlled. The quoted literature makes clear that robust finite-size handling is a central methodological requirement, not an optional detail. Without convergence evidence, the manuscript’s accuracy claims remain under-justified. References: [2] C. Lin, F. H. Zong, and D. M. Ceperley. *Twist-averaged boundary conditions in continuum quantum Monte Carlo algorithms* (Phys. Rev. E 64, 016702). [https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf](https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf) [3] S. Chiesa, D. M. Ceperley, R. M. Martin, and M. Holzmann. *Finite-Size Error in Many-Body Simulations with Long-Range Interactions* (Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404). [https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404)

paper_title: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz
paper_excerpt: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li ( lixiang.62770689@bytedance.com ) ByteDance Inc https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8572-1875 Zhe Li ByteDance Inc Ji Chen Peking University https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1603-1963 Article Keywords: Posted Date: May 31st, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-1689060/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The manuscript reports many high-precision quantitative outcomes (e.g., “within 0.1 eV/atom,” “error of less than 1%”) without clearly defining statistical uncertainties, their estimators, or how uncertainties propagate through derived metrics like cohesive energies and “correlation error,” which weakens the reliability of the conclusions. “Supplementary Figure 2 | \(2 \times 2\) Graphene training curve. For clarity, at each iteration number, we show the median energy per primitive cell over the last 10% of iteration.” Reporting a “median … over the last 10%” is a visualization choice that does not, by itself, provide a statistically interpretable uncertainty estimate for the final energies. Medians suppress outliers but do not quantify sampling variance, autocorrelation effects, or residual optimization drift, all of which are central in VMC-style estimations. Because the paper’s main claims hinge on small energy differences (sub-eV per atom) and on “outperforming” baselines, the uncertainty model needs to be explicit and standard. The paper does not accompany these median curves with defined error bars or confidence intervals. Without an explicit estimator (e.g., mean ± standard error with reblocking), a reader cannot assess whether apparent improvements exceed stochastic noise. This directly weakens confidence in the reported accuracy margins. ```html -76.15588(6) -76.24949(5) -76.26314(5) ``` The parenthetical notation strongly suggests an uncertainty is being reported, but the manuscript does not define what “(6)” or “(5)” means (e.g., one standard error of the mean, one standard deviation, or an error from fitting). In QMC practice, different conventions exist, and the distinction matters when comparing to claimed sub-0.1 eV/atom accuracy. Moreover, twist averaging and structure-factor corrections introduce additional sources of uncertainty and bias beyond the raw Monte Carlo sampling noise. The table as presented also does not state whether these uncertainties are computed after equilibration, reblocking, or accounting for autocorrelation. Without an explicit definition, the reported precision cannot be reliably interpreted. This is a significant issue because the paper repeatedly uses precise numerical comparisons to support strong conclusions. “Overall, our neural network performs very well, with an error of less than 1% in a wide range of density (see Supplementary Table 11).” This claim depends on a “correlation error” metric defined using multiple energies (HF, a DMC reference, and the network’s estimate). Such derived ratios can be sensitive to small denominators (e.g., at high density where correlation energies are small) and to uncertainties in each contributing term. The manuscript does not provide a propagation-of-uncertainty analysis for this percentage, nor does it state whether the “<1%” bound holds within statistical confidence. If the underlying energies have correlated Monte Carlo noise (common in reusing walkers, or in shared correction schemes), naive error reporting can be misleading. In addition, finite-size and fixed-node biases can dominate over sampling error for some regimes, but these are not separated. As a result, the “<1%” statement is hard to interpret as a robust quantitative guarantee. This weakens the reliability of the conclusions drawn from the HEG comparison. (excerpt below) [[1]](#ref1) ``` After network optimization, we run O(105) MCMC steps and calculate the mean local energy every 10 steps. The energy and associated standard error are estimated using a standard approach to account for correlations. 60 ``` This quote exemplifies common practice in neural-network VMC papers: energies are reported with a clearly stated “standard error” estimate that accounts for correlated samples. The reviewed manuscript does not provide a comparable definition of its parenthetical uncertainties, nor does it state how autocorrelation is handled in its estimates. Because QMC sampling is correlated by construction, omission of correlation-aware error estimation can materially understate uncertainty. The quote is not presented to require identical methods, but to illustrate the field’s baseline expectation for uncertainty reporting when making strong quantitative claims. Without similarly explicit uncertainty definitions, the reviewed paper’s precision claims are less credible. This affects both interpretability and reproducibility. References: [1] D. Pfau, J. S. Spencer, A. G. D. G. Matthews, and W. M. C. Foulkes. *Ab-Initio Solution of the Many-Electron Schrödinger Equation with Deep Neural Networks* (arXiv:1909.02487v3). [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf)

paper_title: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz
paper_excerpt: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li ( lixiang.62770689@bytedance.com ) ByteDance Inc https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8572-1875 Zhe Li ByteDance Inc Ji Chen Peking University https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1603-1963 Article Keywords: Posted Date: May 31st, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-1689060/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The manuscript’s formal description of the solid wavefunction ansatz is internally inconsistent between the main-text expression and the provided algorithm, making it difficult to verify correctness (e.g., spin structure and determinant factorization) and undermining reproducibility of the central methodological contribution. “\(\Psi(\mathbf{r}) = \mathrm{Det}_1[e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{r}_1}u_{mol}^\uparrow(d)]\mathrm{Det}_1[e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{r}_1}u_{mol}^\downarrow(d)] .\)” This equation indicates a product of (at least) two determinants associated with spin-up and spin-down channels, aligning with standard QMC antisymmetry enforcement. However, the notation is ambiguous (both are labeled “Det\_1”), and it does not clearly specify whether the determinants are block-diagonalized by spin or full determinants over all electrons. In periodic systems with complex-valued orbitals, these details matter for both the correctness of antisymmetry constraints and the implementation of phase factors. Because the method’s novelty is in extending molecular neural wavefunctions to periodic boundary conditions, the wavefunction definition must be unambiguous. Any inconsistency in determinant structure directly affects the interpretation of how fermionic antisymmetry is ensured. This ambiguity is magnified because subsequent algorithmic details are presented as a concrete implementation. “28: \( \Psi = \mathrm{Det}[\phi^\dagger] \mathrm{Det}[\phi^\dagger] \)” The algorithmic line shown here appears to form the wavefunction by multiplying two identical determinants, without specifying distinct spin channels. Taken literally, this differs from the main-text description of separate spin-up and spin-down determinants and would be conceptually incorrect for a typical spin-separated ansatz. Even if this is intended as shorthand, the manuscript does not clarify how spin is represented in \(\phi\) or why the product involves two identical terms. Since Algorithm 1 is presented as the “overall sketch of the neural network,” readers should be able to map it directly to an implementable procedure. Internal inconsistencies in the method specification impede verification by other groups and weaken confidence that the written method matches what was executed. Given that the wavefunction definition is the core of the paper, this is a significant reproducibility and correctness concern. ```python def logdet_matmul(xs: Sequence[jnp.ndarray], w: Optional[jnp.ndarray] = None) -> jnp.ndarray: """Combines determinants and takes dot product with weights in log-domain. Args: xs: FermiNet orbitals in each determinant. Either of length 1 with shape (ndet, nelectron, nelectron) (full_det=True) or length 2 with shapes (ndet, nalpha, nalpha) and (ndet, nbeta, nbeta) (full_det=False, determinants are factorised into block-diagonals for each spin channel). Returns: sum_i w_i D_i in the log domain, where w_i is the weight of D_i, the i-th determinant (or product of the i-th determinant in each spin channel, if full_det is not used). """ ``` The released implementation explicitly distinguishes two cases: a single full determinant over all electrons or a product of spin-channel determinants when factorized. This illustrates that the determinant factorization and spin handling are subtle and explicitly parameterized in code. When contrasted with the manuscript’s Algorithm 1 line that multiplies two identical determinants, it reinforces that the paper’s written description does not clearly convey the actual determinant structure needed for correctness. Moreover, because the paper positions its architecture as an extension of FermiNet to periodic systems, readers expect consistency between described mathematical form and code-level semantics. If the manuscript does not precisely describe whether it uses “full_det” or spin-block determinants in each experiment, reproducing and comparing results becomes harder. This is not merely a presentation issue: determinant structure can change expressivity, optimization behavior, and even numerical stability. The mismatch therefore undermines the clarity and verifiability of the central methodological claim. (excerpt below) [[1]](#ref1) ``` Eq. 7 uses the fact that the full determinant det[ Φ] = det [ φi(xj; {x/j}) ] may be replaced by a product of spin- up and spin-down terms if we choose φi(xj; {x/j}) = 0 if i∈1 ...n ↑and j ∈n↑+ 1,...,n or i∈n↑+ 1,...,n and j ∈1,...,n ↑. ``` This quote from FermiNet (which the reviewed manuscript cites as a base architecture) provides a clear, standard statement of the spin-factorized determinant product used to enforce fermionic antisymmetry. Because the reviewed paper claims to “represent the molecular networks with FermiNet” and extend them to periodic systems, consistency with the baseline formulation is important for interpretability. The contrast between this explicit spin-factorized form and the reviewed paper’s ambiguous/duplicated determinant notation highlights a real specification problem. Readers cannot confidently determine whether the paper’s ansatz is a direct periodic analogue of the FermiNet factorization or something else. This matters when assessing novelty, correctness, and comparability to baselines. The paper should resolve this inconsistency by clearly defining the determinant blocks and spin treatment in the main text. References: [1] D. Pfau, J. S. Spencer, A. G. D. G. Matthews, and W. M. C. Foulkes. *Ab-Initio Solution of the Many-Electron Schrödinger Equation with Deep Neural Networks* (arXiv:1909.02487v3). [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf)
annotator_comments: I find it quite impressive that this reviewer actually looks at the code to understand whether some parts of the paper are not properly discussed. I am personally not doing it on a regular basis as it would consume a substantial amount of time.

paper_title: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz
paper_excerpt: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li ( lixiang.62770689@bytedance.com ) ByteDance Inc https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8572-1875 Zhe Li ByteDance Inc Ji Chen Peking University https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1603-1963 Article Keywords: Posted Date: May 31st, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-1689060/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The manuscript’s computational claims (notably multi-node GPU parallelization and “highly efficient” training) are not reproducible from the provided code as released, because the code explicitly lacks multi-node support and hard-pins an outdated JAX stack without an executable, versioned environment specification, making independent replication of the reported large-scale results impractical. “Calculations are also ensured by efficient and massive parallelization on multiple nodes of high-performance GPUs.” This sentence frames multi-node, large-scale GPU parallelization as a key enabler of the method’s reported results and scale (e.g., 108-electron LiH). When such computational infrastructure is central to feasibility, reproducibility requires that the released code supports the same execution mode or that the paper provides a faithful reproduction recipe (container, scripts, and system requirements). Otherwise, readers cannot distinguish between an algorithmic advance and an implementation/hardware advantage that cannot be replicated. The paper also uses strong scale-related language (“highly efficient,” “massive parallelization”), which invites scrutiny of whether the software artifact matches those capabilities. If the released code does not support multi-node, reproducing the reported wall-clock feasibility and scaling claims becomes difficult. This is a material reproducibility gap because it affects whether other groups can validate and extend the approach. “Present released code doesn't support multi-node training.” This directly contradicts the multi-node parallelization claim in the paper’s narrative and indicates that a key part of the computational setup used in the reported experiments is not available to replicators. Even if the algorithm itself is correct, lack of multi-node support can prevent reproducing the flagship calculations within reasonable resource limits, especially for larger electron counts. The manuscript does not provide an alternative route (e.g., smaller-scale scripts that reproduce at least one reported figure/table) demonstrating that the method can be validated without the unavailable infrastructure. As a result, the computational evidence in the paper becomes partially non-falsifiable for typical academic settings. This is not a minor engineering detail: for QMC with neural wavefunctions, compute scale can be the dominant feasibility constraint. Therefore, the mismatch undermines reproducibility and the strength of empirical validation. ```python REQUIRED_PACKAGES = ( "absl-py", 'attrs', "dataclasses", "networkx", "scipy==1.9.3", "numpy", "ordered-set", "typing", "chex==0.1.5", "jax==0.2.26", "jaxlib==0.1.75", "pandas", "ml_collections", "pyscf", "tables", "h5py==3.2.1", "optax==0.0.9", "opt_einsum==3.3.0", ) ``` This pinned dependency set hard-codes very old versions of core numerical libraries (notably JAX/JAXLIB), which is a common source of “bit-rot” and install failure in modern environments. The paper does not provide a container image, lockfile, or validated environment spec beyond this setup.py, so even obtaining a runnable installation can be difficult for reviewers and practitioners. This is especially problematic because the paper’s central claims depend on complex-number support, automatic differentiation, and performance-critical primitives whose APIs can change across versions. The pinned versions also diverge from the paper’s narrative of easy integration and practical adoption, because users must match an old stack precisely. A reproducible artifact typically includes an environment file and a tested installation path that is robust over time. Without that, the empirical claims are harder to verify independently. “Our experiments were carried out with `jax==0.2.26` and `jaxlib==0.1.75.`” This reinforces that the released code is tied to a narrow and dated software configuration, yet the manuscript does not provide a full specification of the broader environment (Python version, CUDA/cuDNN versions, compiler/toolchain) needed to reproduce GPU results. In high-performance neural QMC, such details often determine whether results and runtimes can be replicated. The absence of an end-to-end environment specification also makes it difficult to assess whether the reported improvements are robust or contingent on a specific, potentially non-portable stack. Moreover, the paper’s claims about being “easily integrated” into other neural structures are in tension with the rigidity of the pinned environment. Even if the algorithm is conceptually general, the artifact as released is not immediately reusable without significant engineering effort. This constitutes a substantive reproducibility concern for a methods paper.

Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The paper compares the properties of Lithium Hydride (LiH) derived from a small $2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell directly to experimental bulk values, ignoring significant finite-size errors. "where $E_0, V_0, B_0, B'_0$ are fitted quantities, their results and corresponding experiment data [4] are listed in Supplementary Table 9." The authors fit the equation of state and compare the resulting bulk modulus ($B_0$) and cohesive energy ($E_{coh}$) directly to experimental values. However, the data used for this fit comes from the $2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell calculations (Supplementary Table 8), as indicated by the context of Supplementary Note 4.3 and 4.4. "Energy of the $3 \times 3 \times 3$ LiH crystal... -8.16020(2)" (Supplementary Table 10) The energy per primitive cell for the $3 \times 3 \times 3$ supercell at $L=4.061$ Å is -8.16020 Ha, whereas the $2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell energy at $L=4.0$ Å is -8.15096 Ha (Supplementary Table 8). This difference of approximately 10 mHa indicates a substantial finite-size error that has not been converged or extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit. "Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions" [3] As established in the literature, finite-size errors in many-body calculations of solids, especially for potential energy, decay slowly ($1/L$ or $1/N$). Comparing results from a small supercell directly to bulk experiment without proper finite-size scaling or extrapolation leads to invalid conclusions about the method's accuracy relative to experiment. References: [3] S. Chiesa et al., "Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions", Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404 (2006). https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404 [4] P. López Ríos et al., "Inhomogeneous backflow transformations in quantum Monte Carlo calculations", Phys. Rev. E 74, 066701 (2006). https://journals.aps.org/pre/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevE.74.066701
annotator_comments: The authors write explicitly that they employ finite-size correction.

Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: Contrary to the claim that the method outperforms many traditional ab initio methods and reaches the level of the most accurate approaches, the reported results for the Homogeneous Electron Gas (HEG) and Hydrogen chain are consistently worse than or merely comparable to established Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) methods from over a decade ago. "the obtained results... outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches." (Abstract) This claim is contradicted by the paper's own data when compared to the cited benchmarks. "Supplementary Table 11 | Energy per electron of HEG... r_s=1 Net: 0.530019(1), BF-DMC: 0.52989(4)" For the Homogeneous Electron Gas, the proposed method ("Net") produces higher (worse) variational energies than the Backflow Diffusion Monte Carlo (BF-DMC) results from 2006 [4] for all reported densities ($r_s = 0.5$ to $20$). Since BF-DMC is a "traditional" QMC method, the claim of outperforming such methods is factually incorrect for this system. "Supplementary Table 3 | Energy of H_{10} chain... Bond length 1.8... Net: -0.572922(1), LR-DMC(LDA): -0.57304(1)" For the Hydrogen chain ($H_{10}$), the method's energy is slightly higher (worse) than the Lattice Regularized DMC (LR-DMC) result from the benchmark paper [1]. While the results are close, they do not "outperform" the established accurate approach. References: [1] M. Motta et al., "Towards the solution of the many-electron problem in real materials: Equation of state of the hydrogen chain with state-of-the-art many-body methods", Phys. Rev. X 7, 031059 (2017). https://journals.aps.org/prx/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevX.7.031059 [4] P. López Ríos et al., "Inhomogeneous backflow transformations in quantum Monte Carlo calculations", Phys. Rev. E 74, 066701 (2006). https://journals.aps.org/pre/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevE.74.066701
annotator_comments: The point is that the goal may be to get closer to this accuracy and not to outperform it but at a reduced computational cost. The real critique here is that the authors don't clearly demonstrate how more and whether their approach is more computationally efficient.

paper_excerpt: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li ( lixiang.62770689@bytedance.com ) ByteDance Inc https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8572-1875 Zhe Li ByteDance Inc Ji Chen Peking University https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1603-1963 Article Keywords: Posted Date: May 31st, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-1689060/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The use of a single $2 \times 2$ supercell for graphene is insufficient to capture long-range correlations and reach the thermodynamic limit, rendering the claim of "unprecedented accuracy" for this system unsupported. "The size of supercell is $2 \times 2$." (Supplementary Note 3.1) A $2 \times 2$ supercell contains only 8 carbon atoms. For a gapless semi-metal like graphene, finite-size effects are particularly severe. Relying on such a small system size without performing a finite-size extrapolation (as was done for the Hydrogen chain) fails to demonstrate that the method can accurately describe the bulk properties of graphene. "Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions" [3] Accurate calculation of 2D materials requires careful extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit. The use of Twist Averaged Boundary Conditions (TABC) helps but does not eliminate the need for larger supercells to capture long-range correlation effects, which are crucial for the claim of "ab initio calculation of real solids". References: [3] S. Chiesa et al., "Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions", Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404 (2006). https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404
annotator_comments: I would also willing to see how their method performs with a larger cell.

paper_title: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz
paper_excerpt: Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li ( lixiang.62770689@bytedance.com ) ByteDance Inc https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8572-1875 Zhe Li ByteDance Inc Ji Chen Peking University https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1603-1963 Article Keywords: Posted Date: May 31st, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-1689060/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The paper claims the method is "highly efficient" but provides no computational cost analysis (e.g., wall-clock time, memory usage, total FLOPs), while the reported hyperparameters suggest the method is computationally expensive. "based on which we develop a highly efficient QMC method" (Abstract) The term "highly efficient" implies a competitive computational cost relative to other methods. However, the paper provides no data to support this. Neural network ansatzes in QMC are generally known to be orders of magnitude more expensive than conventional Slater-Jastrow QMC or DFT. "Training steps 2e5... Batch size 4096" (Supplementary Table 1) The requirement of 200,000 training steps with a batch size of 4096 samples implies nearly a billion evaluations of the neural network. For a solid-state system with all-electron calculations (like LiH), this represents a massive computational burden. Without a cost comparison, the claim of "high efficiency" is misleading and likely incorrect compared to the "traditional methods" the paper claims to outperform.

Moreover,
review_item: To my knowledge, Bloch functions are for one-electron states not for correlated many-body wavefunctions. Besides, the FermiNet employs the Generalized Slater determinants and its 'molecular orbitals' actually take into account electron correlations. The authors should prove that the application of the Bloch function form is not an approximation (if it is an approximation, please explicitly mention that). Unless this is a very trivial matter, the authors should state the proof in the Appendix or Supplementary Information.

Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The manuscript's claim that the method outperforms other ab initio methods is not supported by the evidence shown. For the H10 and the UEG, the result is better stated as similar performance with the state-of-the-art DMC results (rather than a claim of being better than common approaches). In the case of the UEG in particular, it is known that the DMC results are not free from error at high densities due to fixed node error. Instead, as highlighted by a recent paper (Ref 31), the most accurate approaches come from FCIQMC which are available for N=14. Similar FCIQMC results are also available for N=54, which could have been used for comparison as they show clear improvement on BF-DMC.

Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The manuscript's claim of improvement over the existing FermiNet papers is not supported by the evidence shown. The changes made include a different choice of PBCs and NN optimizer. To validate the effectiveness of the optimizer, we would need to see efficiency data. For the PBCs choice, we would need to see well-analyzed side by side comparisons. Instead, the manuscript does not reach a direct comparison when treating the UEG, choosing different systems than Ref 31. It is possible to infer from the data presented that this algorithm captures DMC-quality energies, while Ref 31 goes beyond DMC to FCIQMC level accuracy. Thus, this manuscript's choice of PBCs seems less accurate than FermiNet not more.

Read Full License Ab initio calculation of real solids via neural network ansatz Xiang Li1*, Zhe Li1, and Ji Chen2 1ByteDance Inc, Zhonghang Plaza, No. 43, North 3rd Ring West Road, Haidian District, Beijing. 2School of Physics, Interdisciplinary Institute of Light-Element Quantum Materials, Frontiers Science Center for Nano-Optoelectronics, Peking University, Beijing 100871, People’s Republic of China Abstract Neural networks have been applied to tackle many-body electron correlations for small molecules and physical models in recent years. Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: The manuscript's claim of investigating long-range effectiveness in real systems is weakened by the size and electronic structure of the systems chosen. In practice, the long-range components of the energy are being added after the fact with finite size corrections (Ref. 26). The calculations themselves are being performed on relatively small system sizes. Evidence is not presented for the convergence of long-range effects (which would, for example, require analysis of the energy with system size). Moreover, the systems shown do not include a real metal (where long-range effects really come into play) and the system sizes shown have significant gaps at the one-particle/HF level of theory. So, while the systems proposed are ab initio real solids, they are not actually treated in the same way as the state of the art, and do not go beyond what can already be done.

Here we propose a new architecture that extends molecular neural networks with the inclusion of periodic boundary conditions to enable ab initio calculation of real solids. The accuracy of our approach is demonstrated in four different types of systems, namely the one-dimensional periodic hydrogen chain, the two-dimensional graphene, the three-dimensional lithium hydride crystal, and the homogeneous electron gas, where the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches. Moreover,
review_item: Comparing this paper to what is currently the state of the art in the field, there are a number of high impact publications. The best known paper is by Troyer in Science (Ref 9) and the original FermiNet paper was published in Phys Rev Research (Ref 10). The best case scenario of the technical development appears to be a more efficient version of FermiNet for solids and I would put this on a par with the original FermiNet development in terms of its specialism. In my view, this paper is better suited to a more specialized journal which would also allow the claims of the novelty of the network and any speed up to be better considered and reviewed.

paper_excerpt: Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces George Booth george.booth@kcl.ac.uk King's College London Yannic Rath National Physical Laboratory Article Keywords: Posted Date: March 15th, 2024 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-3976196/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.
