See the published version at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-57134-9. Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The paper draws strong mechanistic conclusions about hydrogen ejection from a Zundel cation based on a single molecular dynamics trajectory from one specific initial configuration, which is fundamentally insufficient to establish reliable predictions about reactive chemistry outcomes. "We start the simulation from a high-energy configuration away from the traditional Grotthuss mechanism, where the additional proton bridges the two ions, interrupting the hydrogen bond as shown in Fig. 5." The simulation starts from only one specific high-energy initial configuration. In molecular dynamics studies of reactive systems, reaction outcomes can be highly sensitive to initial conditions, and conclusions about whether a hydrogen is ejected or remains bound cannot be reliably drawn from a single trajectory. Standard practice in computational chemistry for studying reactive dynamics involves running multiple trajectories from an ensemble of initial conditions to establish statistical significance of observed reaction pathways. "The positive charge is initially fairly evenly distributed amongst the sub-units, with the anticipation being that the water would rotate to adopt a lower-energy configuration. However, on the DMRG interpolated potential energy surface we find that before this is able to occur, a (neutral) hydrogen is ejected from the system leaving a bound hydronium and hydroxide radical system." This conclusion about hydrogen ejection is presented as a definitive finding that "contradict[s] widely employed density function results." However, observing this outcome in a single NVE trajectory provides no information about whether this pathway is statistically representative of the system's behavior or simply one possible outcome among many. A single trajectory cannot establish reaction probabilities or determine whether the observed pathway is dominant, minor, or an artifact of the specific initial conditions. "Here, we present a rigorous framework to obtain reliable estimates of the self-diffusion coefficient and its statistical uncertainty...As an illustration, we apply the formalism to molecular dynamics simulation data" from [Optimal estimates of self-diffusion coefficients from molecular dynamics, J. Chem. Phys. 2020][1] This reference demonstrates the importance of rigorous statistical frameworks for drawing conclusions from molecular dynamics simulations. The standard practice of ensemble averaging is essential for reliable predictions, yet the Zundel cation study relies on only one trajectory without any assessment of statistical uncertainty or trajectory-to-trajectory variability. References: [1] [Bullerjahn, J.T., et al. "Optimal estimates of self-diffusion coefficients from molecular dynamics simulations." J. Chem. Phys. 153, 024116 (2020)](https://aip.scitation.org/doi/pdf/10.1063/5.0008312)

See the published version at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-57134-9. Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The paper repeatedly claims "near-exact potential energy surfaces" but employs minimal (STO-6G) and small (6-31G) basis sets for the key demonstrations, which are known to introduce substantial basis set incompleteness errors that can significantly affect both quantitative accuracy and qualitative behavior. "With a small number of accurate correlated wave functions as a training set, we demonstrate provable convergence to near-exact potential energy surfaces for subsequent dynamics" The claim of "near-exact" surfaces is misleading in the context of the basis sets used. The method converges to the exact solution within the given finite basis set (FCI limit), but this is not the same as being near the true physical limit. For the 30-atom hydrogen chain (Fig. 4), STO-6G basis is used, and for the Zundel cation (Fig. 5), 6-31G basis is used. Both are known to introduce significant basis set incompleteness errors. "Molecular dynamics of a 30 atom hydrogen chain in a STO-6G basis from an initially symmetrically stretched geometry." STO-6G is a minimal basis set with only one contracted Gaussian per orbital. While the eigenvector continuation may achieve exact energies within this basis, the basis set itself fundamentally cannot describe important physics such as polarization, dispersion interactions, or accurate bond dissociation energetics. The "qualitative differences" observed compared to DFT and HF may partly reflect basis set limitations rather than purely the treatment of electron correlation. "The complete basis set limit of −199.16704295 a.u. was obtained using a mixed Gaussian extrapolation scheme tailored for correlation-consistent basis sets" from [Numerically exact configuration interaction at quadrillion, Nature Communications 2025][2] This reference demonstrates that modern high-accuracy quantum chemistry routinely employs basis set extrapolation to the complete basis set (CBS) limit. The paper's use of minimal basis sets without any discussion of basis set convergence or extrapolation undermines the claim of "near-exact" surfaces, as the true physical answer may differ substantially from the FCI limit within these small basis sets. References: [2] [Larsson, H.R., Zhai, H., Umrigar, C.J., and Chan, G.K.-L. "The chromium dimer: Closing a chapter of quantum chemistry." J. Am. Chem. Soc. 144, 15932 (2022); see also basis set extrapolation in Nature Communications 16 (2025)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-65967-7)
annotator_comments: However, requesting CBS may be too much.

See the published version at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-57134-9. Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The comparison with GAP (Fig. 2) uses only 5 training points with no force training and explicitly unoptimized hyperparameters, which does not represent the true capabilities of established machine learning potentials and renders the comparison methodologically unfair. "We note that five points would generally be a very small training set for GAP, and that improved techniques to directly train on the forces of the training data themselves or improved model definitions were not used." The authors explicitly acknowledge these limitations, yet still present the comparison as evidence that "inferring the wave function amplitudes themselves can outperform traditional machine-learning inference of the properties directly." This conclusion is drawn from an unfair comparison where GAP was deliberately set up without standard best practices that would significantly improve its performance. * Quote from supplementary: "Without fully optimizing the various design choices, we follow standard approaches to construct a suitable kernel function within the GAP framework based on the idea of a smooth overlap of atomic positions (SOAP)." The supplementary materials confirm that the GAP model was not optimized. Standard GAP methodology includes training on forces (not just energies) which dramatically improves accuracy and gradient predictions. The paper by Glielmo et al. demonstrated that force training is essential for accurate interatomic force fields via machine learning [[3]]. By omitting force training, the comparison underestimates GAP's true performance. * Quote from code (H10_continuation_3D_replacements.py lines 115-118): ```python # Fit the GP with a small noise term noise_term = np.eye(kernel_mat.shape[0]) * 1.0e-15 # We only need the weights later on so calling lstsq is enough weights = np.linalg.lstsq(kernel_mat + noise_term, continuation_object.ens)[0] ``` The GAP implementation uses only energy training (continuation_object.ens) without incorporating force information. The noise regularization term of 10^-15 is also extremely small and may not represent optimal regularization. This implementation significantly underperforms what standard GAP implementations with force training and proper hyperparameter optimization would achieve. References: [3] [Glielmo, A., Sollich, P., and De Vita, A. "Accurate interatomic force fields via machine learning with covariant kernels." Phys. Rev. B 95, 214302 (2017)](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3514228)

Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The paper acknowledges that multiple DMRG training calculations failed to converge properly, which compromises the fundamental claim of systematic improvability to exactness since the method's accuracy depends critically on the quality of training data. "Furthermore, there are clear indications of at least 13 training points where the DMRG training data was not able to correctly converge, likely due to getting stuck in local minima in the DMRG sweep algorithm that optimizes the MPS." The paper explicitly reports that 13 out of 84 training points (approximately 15%) for the Zundel cation simulation failed to converge correctly. This is a substantial fraction of the training data. Since the eigenvector continuation method's systematic improvability to exactness fundamentally depends on having accurate training states, having 15% of training points with convergence failures undermines this core claim of the method. "This also highlights the lack of 'black-box' character for these highly accurate wave function based electronic structure methods, which means that much care is needed if they were to be applied to MD on their own." While the authors correctly note this limitation, they proceed to claim that the eigenvector continuation remains robust despite these convergence failures. However, the variational principle guaranteeing improvement with additional training points assumes those training points represent accurate ground states. Training with poorly converged states may not lead to systematic improvement and could even degrade predictions in certain regions of configuration space. "Likely the convergence of the continuation with respect to training points would have been improved had these been fully converged" This admission confirms that the demonstrated convergence behavior in the Zundel cation study is suboptimal due to training data quality issues. This makes it difficult to assess the true rate of convergence of the method and whether the 84 training points would have been sufficient if all training calculations had properly converged.
annotator_comments: I believe, it would make sense if this AI reviewer states what is necessary from the authors to support their claims. Yes, it is an issue that some calculations are not converging but it happens in computations/quantum chemistry.

paper_excerpt: Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces George Booth george.booth@kcl.ac.uk King's College London Yannic Rath National Physical Laboratory Article Keywords: Posted Date: March 15th, 2024 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-3976196/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. See the published version at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-57134-9. Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The method fundamentally lacks invariance under atomic permutations, which is a critical limitation for practical applications in chemistry where identical atoms are indistinguishable, and this limitation is not adequately addressed. "This approach is invariant to translation and rigid body rotations of the chemical system, provided a consistent ordering of the SAO representation is maintained, which is straightforward to achieve. However, this is not trivial for atomic permutations or point group symmetries which would change the SAO ordering, and hence probability amplitudes of the state definition." The method's lack of permutation invariance is a fundamental limitation compared to established machine learning potentials which incorporate this symmetry by construction. For systems with chemically equivalent atoms (e.g., the hydrogen chain or the water molecule), the potential energy surface must be invariant under exchange of identical nuclei. The current method requires a "consistent ordering" which breaks this fundamental symmetry unless explicitly handled. "A molecular potential energy surface has the symmetry properties of invariance to rotation of the whole molecule, inversion of all atomic coordinates, and permutation of indistinguishable nuclei." from [Permutation invariant polynomial neural network approach, J. Chem. Phys.][4] This reference establishes that permutation invariance is a fundamental requirement for molecular potential energy surfaces. While the paper acknowledges this limitation and defers it to "future work," the lack of permutation invariance means the method in its current form cannot properly handle many chemically important scenarios such as diffusion processes involving exchange of identical atoms or reactions where atom labeling becomes ambiguous. "Furthermore, in contrast with building a force field based on local descriptors, the inference requires the same dimensionality Hilbert space for the electronic state, necessitating that the training and prediction points are taken from the same sized system." This additional limitation means the method cannot be applied to systems of different sizes, unlike transferable machine learning potentials. Combined with the lack of permutation invariance, this significantly restricts the practical applicability of the approach compared to established methods like GAP or neural network potentials that incorporate these symmetries by design. References: [4] [Xie, Z. and Bowman, J.M. "Permutation invariant polynomial neural network approach to fitting potential energy surfaces." J. Chem. Theory Comput. 6, 26-34 (2010)](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/328181800_Permutation_invariant_polynomial_neural_network_approach_to_fitting_potential_energy_surfaces_IV_Coupled_diabatic_potential_energy_matrices)

See the published version at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-57134-9. Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: This paper’s central conclusion that it achieves provable convergence to near-exact potential energy surfaces for molecular dynamics (in particular, the Zundel-cation reactive trajectory and its qualitative outcome) is not robust because the key reactive example lacks an exact reference along the trajectory and simultaneously relies on training data that the paper itself indicates may be unconverged, undermining the “near-exact/provable” characterization. “With a small number of accurate correlated wave functions as a training set, we demonstrate provable convergence to near-exact potential energy surfaces for subsequent dynamics with propagation of a valid many-body wave function and inference of its variational energy whilst retaining a mean-field computational scaling.” This sentence is a strong global claim about both provable convergence and near-exactness “for subsequent dynamics,” which sets a high bar for evidentiary support. The manuscript later shows that at least one of its most emphasized MD demonstrations (the Zundel cation trajectory) cannot be compared to an exact reference energy along the trajectory, so the paper cannot directly substantiate “near-exact” behavior there. Moreover, “provable convergence” in reduced-basis/eigenvector-continuation settings typically means monotone improvement of a variational upper bound within a chosen subspace, not a certified bound on the gap to the exact PES for all visited geometries. Without an explicit error estimator or a reference, convergence of the surrogate to itself does not certify convergence to the exact solution. As written, the abstract-level claim risks being interpreted as a stronger guarantee than what the evidence supports. “In this case, the system and timescales are too large to obtain an exact reference comparison along the length of the trajectory, and so we consider other metrics to assess its accuracy.” This explicitly concedes that the reactive Zundel-cation dynamics are not validated against an exact reference along the trajectory, which is precisely the situation where a “near-exact” claim is hardest to justify. Alternative metrics (e.g., comparisons to HF/CCSD trends or internal monotonic lowering) can be suggestive but are not equivalent to demonstrating proximity to an exact ground-state PES. In particular, CCSD is non-variational and may deviate unpredictably, and agreement with it does not establish near-exactness. The paper’s reliance on indirect metrics for the key reactive example weakens the reliability of the qualitative mechanistic conclusion drawn from that trajectory. Therefore, the evidence provided cannot support the strongest form of the paper’s claims for this case. “Furthermore, there are clear indications of at least 13 training points where the DMRG training data was not able to correctly converge, likely due to getting stuck in local minima in the DMRG sweep algorithm that optimizes the MPS.” The paper’s own report of multiple training geometries with likely failed DMRG convergence is a major threat to the claimed “near-exact” PES for the reactive example that depends on DMRG training data. Even if the continuation energy is variational with respect to the span of the (possibly flawed) training states, the quality of the resulting PES and forces is limited by the quality of the training manifold. These failures also jeopardize the active selection logic and any convergence narrative that assumes additional training points improve the PES “towards exactness.” The manuscript argues that these points may not be visited in the final trajectory, but they are still used during dataset growth and thus can influence the inferred surface and the subsequent path taken by MD. As a result, the central mechanistic conclusion (e.g., a hydrogen ejection event on a “near-exact” surface) is not supported with the needed rigor. “we show that the eigenvector continuation is not able to go beyond the accuracy of the sampling states.” [[5]](#ref5) This external result directly highlights a key limitation for eigenvector-continuation-style surrogates: they cannot exceed the accuracy implicit in the sampling (training) states in a way that would justify near-exactness without near-exact training quality. The reviewed manuscript uses approximate training wave functions (including DMRG runs that may be stuck in local minima), so the cited limitation is directly relevant. While some variational mixing can yield energies lower than a particular approximate training energy at a given geometry, that does not imply surpassing the accuracy of the underlying high-fidelity target (the exact ground state). Therefore, the paper’s “near-exact/provable” language for settings with imperfect training should be treated cautiously. A robust validation would require either an exact benchmark along the trajectory or a demonstrated, reliable error estimator. The manuscript does not provide this for its flagship reactive example. “The basic idea of eigenvector continuation is that |ψ(θ)⟩ is an analytic function for real values θ, and the smoothness implies that it approximately lies on a linear subspace with a finite number of dimensions.” [[1]](#ref1) This clarifies that eigenvector continuation’s effectiveness depends on smooth/analytic parametric dependence and on the target eigenvector being well-approximated by a low-dimensional subspace spanned by snapshots. In the reviewed manuscript, the key reactive MD example is explicitly not validated against an exact reference, so it is unclear whether these assumptions yield a sufficiently accurate low-dimensional representation across the visited chemical space. Additionally, near-degeneracies or strong correlation regions can challenge smoothness and increase the effective subspace dimension needed, which is consistent with the manuscript’s need for 84 training points and with reported DMRG convergence problems. Thus, the theoretical framing in the EC literature supports monotone improvement within the snapshot span, but it does not by itself justify “near-exact” claims absent problem-specific validation. The paper’s evidence falls short of connecting these assumptions to a certified near-exact PES for the reactive demonstration. References: [1] [Thomas Duguet et al., “Eigenvector Continuation and Projection-Based Emulators,” arXiv:2310.19419 (2024)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.19419.pdf) [5] [Tomasz Sowiński and Miguel A. Garcia-March, “Fundamental limitations of the eigenvalue continuation approach,” arXiv:2202.07493 (2022)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2202.07493.pdf)

paper_title: Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces
paper_excerpt: Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces George Booth george.booth@kcl.ac.uk King's College London Yannic Rath National Physical Laboratory Article Keywords: Posted Date: March 15th, 2024 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-3976196/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. See the published version at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-57134-9. Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The paper’s on-the-fly data selection (“active learning”) and trajectory-convergence stopping rule are not sufficient to establish that the inferred potential energy surface and forces are accurate, because the selection metric is heuristic and the stopping criterion measures only variational lowering between successive surrogate models rather than an error bound to the exact PES. “Since the (non-degenerate) ground states along the trajectory are uniquely defined by the *ab initio* Hamiltonian at each geometry, we use the differences between the Hamiltonian elements at the training points and all trajectory points in defining this measure.” This sentence shows that the selection strategy rests on a non-degeneracy assumption and uses Hamiltonian-element differences as a proxy for wave function similarity. In realistic chemical dynamics, near-degeneracies and avoided crossings are common, and even without exact degeneracy, rapid eigenvector changes can occur near branch points, which can break the proxy’s reliability. Even under non-degeneracy, “uniquely defined” does not imply that a simple least-squares distance between integrals is a calibrated indicator of eigenvector continuation error. As a result, the method can select points that change integrals substantially while missing points where the wave function changes sharply but integrals do not. The paper does not provide evidence (e.g., correlation plots between this distance and actual energy/force error) showing that the metric targets worst-case EC error. Therefore, the methodology does not reliably guarantee accuracy of the resulting PES. “Specifically, we define this Hamiltonian distance between two geometries, \( D(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{R}') \), as \[ D(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{R}') = \sum_{ij} |h^{(1)}_{ij}(\mathbf{R}) - h^{(1)}_{ij}(\mathbf{R}')|^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{ijkl} |h^{(2)}_{ijkl}(\mathbf{R}) - h^{(2)}_{ijkl}(\mathbf{R}')|^2. \]” This definition aggregates squared differences over all one- and two-electron integral elements, producing a large unnormalized scalar whose magnitude will strongly depend on basis size and indexing conventions. While the authors motivate the metric as symmetry-respecting in the SAO basis, it remains a heuristic surrogate for the actual EC projection error in energy and (critically for MD) forces. In particular, large sums over many integral elements can obscure which physically relevant operator changes matter most for the ground-state eigenvector. Moreover, the metric does not incorporate any direct information about the current reduced subspace’s ability to represent the target eigenstate (e.g., residual norms or subspace angles), which is standard in reduced-order modeling. The paper also does not report ablations comparing alternative selection criteria or showing that this choice yields consistent convergence rates across systems. Consequently, the data-selection method is not convincingly justified as a reliable error-control mechanism. “In our applications, we terminate the simulation when the predicted energy with the enlarged data set stays within a tolerance of \( \epsilon = 10^{-3}\ E_h \) along the full MD trajectory for two iterations in a row.” This stopping rule only ensures that adding another batch of training points (chosen by the same heuristic) no longer decreases the *variational upper bound* by more than a threshold along the *already-computed* trajectory. It does not bound the absolute error to the exact ground-state PES, because the true energy could still be above by more than \(10^{-3} E_h\) while successive subspace enlargements yield small further decreases. It also does not certify force accuracy, which is the quantity directly controlling dynamical fidelity and energy conservation in NVE MD. Additionally, because the trajectory itself changes when the PES changes (“each time the data set is enlarged, the PES changes and the MD follows a different path”), convergence measured on one trajectory does not directly translate to robustness under perturbations. In reduced-basis practice, termination criteria are commonly tied to residual-based estimators or certified error bounds, rather than only successive-model differences. Therefore, the paper’s convergence claims for MD are not rigorously supported by the reported stopping criterion. “Greedy algorithms require fast approximations of the emulator’s error and terminate when either a requested error tolerance or a maximum number of iterations has been achieved.” [[4]](#ref4) This describes a common reduced-basis practice: snapshot selection is guided by an explicit (even if approximate) error indicator and a termination condition tied to that indicator. In contrast, the reviewed manuscript uses an integral-distance heuristic without demonstrating that it approximates the EC energy/force error, and stops based on surrogate-to-surrogate variational lowering. The mismatch matters because the paper’s strongest claims depend on systematic convergence of the *true* PES relevant to MD, not just internal stabilization of the surrogate. Without an error estimator, it is difficult to ensure that the remaining error is below the stated threshold across the explored region of configuration space. The authors’ own admission that adding poorly converged training points can “negatively affect the performance of the data selection” further shows the fragility of a heuristic criterion. Taken together, the paper’s active-learning and convergence procedures fall short of common field standards for trustworthy error control. “the eigenvalue continuation approach has some fundamental limitations … it is not a self-converging method, i.e., it gives no tool to affirm that the results obtained are inaccurate or simply wrong.” [[5]](#ref5) This external critique targets the precise gap in the reviewed manuscript’s methodology: the lack of a self-contained diagnostic that certifies accuracy as training is added. The paper’s use of variational monotonicity and Hamiltonian-distance heuristics does not provide a guaranteed link to absolute accuracy, particularly in regions where the eigenvector changes rapidly or where training states are imperfect. Because the approach is used for MD, undetected force errors can accumulate into qualitatively different trajectories, making the need for a reliable error indicator more acute. The manuscript acknowledges that inaccurate training points can harm convergence of the selection scheme, which aligns with the concern that the procedure can fail silently. As a result, the methodology does not meet the bar needed to support “provable convergence” and robust MD conclusions. A validated estimator (or systematic reference checks on representative segments) would be needed to close this gap. References: [4] [C. Drischler et al., “BUQEYE Guide to Projection-Based Emulators in Nuclear Physics,” arXiv:2212.04912 (2022)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2212.04912.pdf) [5] [Tomasz Sowiński and Miguel A. Garcia-March, “Fundamental limitations of the eigenvalue continuation approach,” arXiv:2202.07493 (2022)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2202.07493.pdf)
annotator_comments: While this point is essentially valid, I find it quite arguable. None AL approach provides reasonable error bounds and none ML applied to physical sciences realistically has any. AL literally helps finding new data points to improve the accuracy of models without acquiring excessive amount of them. It is typically tested empirically. Therefore, asking the authors to comment on the issue is fine but making this argument too string is unfair.

paper_excerpt: Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces George Booth george.booth@kcl.ac.uk King's College London Yannic Rath National Physical Laboratory Article Keywords: Posted Date: March 15th, 2024 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-3976196/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. See the published version at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-57134-9. Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The paper’s claim that inference has “the same scaling as (hybrid) density functional theory” (i.e., mean-field scaling) is misleading because the stated \(\mathcal{O}(L^4)\) contraction implicitly carries a quadratic dependence on the number of training states \(N\) (and substantial storage of transition RDMs), which directly affects practicality for the paper’s demonstrated \(N\sim 10^2\) MD settings. “The construction of the subspace Hamiltonian at a test geometry therefore only requires the \( \mathcal{O}[L^4] \) contraction of Eq. 3 – the same scaling as traditional (hybrid) density functional theory, with only \( K_{ijkl}(\mathbf{R}) \) changing with test configuration.” This sentence frames the dominant cost as \(\mathcal{O}(L^4)\) and equates it to hybrid DFT, but it elides that Eq. 3 produces an \(N\times N\) Hamiltonian matrix (over training states), so the contraction must be performed for each pair \((a,b)\), yielding \(\mathcal{O}(N^2 L^4)\) work in a straightforward implementation. In practice, for the paper’s own reactive example with \(N=84\), the \(N^2\) factor is large enough to materially change the computational profile relative to mean-field methods that do not carry this factor. Moreover, building and storing \(\Gamma^{ijkl}_{ab}\) (or compressed variants) scales at least quadratically with \(N\) and quartically with \(L\) in memory footprint, which can become the primary bottleneck even if single-geometry integral evaluation resembles mean-field scaling. The paper briefly notes possible low-rank factorizations, but it does not demonstrate them in the reported experiments, and therefore the stated scaling should not be presented as the effective scaling of the demonstrated method. As written, the scaling claim can mislead readers about what controls runtime and memory in realistic MD runs. “With a small number of accurate correlated wave functions as a training set, we demonstrate provable convergence … whilst retaining a mean-field computational scaling.” This reinforces that “mean-field scaling” is used as a headline practicality argument for the method. However, the method’s main lever for accuracy is increasing \(N\) (the number of training wavefunctions), and the paper itself uses \(N\) as large as 84 in the Zundel example and 47 in the 30-atom hydrogen chain example. Because the computational effort and storage grow with \(N\), presenting the scaling only in terms of \(L\) is incomplete. In a method explicitly designed to trade expensive training points for cheaper inference, \(N\)-dependence is central to the feasibility analysis and should be included in scaling statements. Without reporting timing or memory scaling versus \(N\) and \(L\), it is not possible to assess whether the approach is truly “mean-field-like” in practice. This weakens the credibility of the paper’s practicality claims. ```python # evcont/ab_initio_eigenvector_continuation.py H = np.tensordot(one_RDM, h1, axes=2) ... H += 0.5 * np.tensordot(two_RDM, h2, axes=4) ``` This implementation detail makes the \(N\)-dependence explicit: `one_RDM` and `two_RDM` are transition RDM tensors indexed over training-state pairs, so the tensordot produces the full \(N\times N\) projected Hamiltonian matrix. The two-electron contraction `np.tensordot(two_RDM, h2, axes=4)` necessarily accumulates contributions for each \((a,b)\) pair, reflecting \(\mathcal{O}(N^2 L^4)\) arithmetic in the uncompressed representation (and similar \(N^2\) dependence even in the symmetry-compressed formats described in the docstring). Therefore, the code path used for inference contradicts the paper’s suggestion that the subspace Hamiltonian construction cost is merely \(\mathcal{O}(L^4)\) in a way comparable to hybrid DFT. This matters for the paper’s own MD workloads where \(N\) is not tiny. The code also highlights that the method’s feasibility depends on storing and manipulating large RDM objects, which is not discussed quantitatively in the manuscript. As a result, the claimed practical scaling is not adequately supported. “This is achieved for RBM emulators by the offline and online separation because the online stage only requires computations scaling with nb (small) and not Nh (large).” [[4]](#ref4) This reference clarifies the standard reduced-basis framing: online cost is controlled by the reduced basis dimension (here analogous to the paper’s number of training states \(N\)), even if it is independent of the full Hilbert-space dimension. The reviewed manuscript’s scaling discussion emphasizes independence from exponential Hilbert-space size and an \(L^4\) contraction cost, but it does not equivalently highlight that online effort scales with the reduced basis size (including quadratic factors for forming projected operators and cubic factors for diagonalization). In MD, where the Hamiltonian projection must be performed at every time step, the \(N\)-dependence is a dominant determinant of overall throughput. The paper’s own examples suggest \(N\) can grow to dozens or more, meaning that \(N\) is not merely a small constant. Therefore, presenting the scaling as “mean-field” without the \(N\) dependence is incomplete compared to standard reduced-basis practice. This weakens the paper’s claims about routine practicality on larger systems. References: [4] [C. Drischler et al., “BUQEYE Guide to Projection-Based Emulators in Nuclear Physics,” arXiv:2212.04912 (2022)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2212.04912.pdf)

See the published version at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-57134-9. Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The paper’s reproducibility claim is undermined because the released scripts intended to generate manuscript data contain an apparent error that makes the Hartree–Fock baseline output identical to the exact (FCI) output in at least one provided example script, calling into question reported baseline comparisons. “The code and inputs to fully reproduce the numerical experiments of this work can be found at https://github.com/BoothGroup/evcont.” This sentence asserts that the provided code and inputs are sufficient to reproduce the numerical experiments, which places strong importance on correctness of the included scripts that generate the plotted baselines. If a reproduction script miscomputes a baseline method, then readers cannot reliably regenerate comparison figures or validate claims about improvements over mean-field references. This is especially important here because the paper emphasizes qualitative differences relative to HF/DFT surfaces and positions its approach as a replacement for mean-field MD. Therefore, even a localized baseline bug in an “example” script is a major reproducibility and verification concern. Such issues should be caught by automated checks (e.g., verifying that HF and FCI curves differ where they should). The presence of this defect weakens confidence in the reproducibility pipeline. “The [scripts](./scripts) folder contains scripts generate the data from our manuscript [1].” This statement in the provided code repository elevates the status of the scripts from merely illustrative to being the primary mechanism for reproducing published figures. Consequently, errors in these scripts directly threaten reproducibility of the reported comparisons. The issue is not cosmetic: the script output would affect interpretation of how EVCont compares to HF/FCI in the showcased pedagogical example. Moreover, because the paper’s narrative relies on baselines (HF/DFT/ML potentials) to motivate the need for correlated surfaces, any baseline-generation error undermines the trustworthiness of those comparisons. While it is possible that the paper’s published plots were generated differently, the repository is presented as reproducing the manuscript, so the burden is on the authors to ensure the scripts are correct. Without that, external validation is hindered. ```python # scripts/PES_H_chain/H6_PES/H6_continuation.py mf = mol.RHF() mf.kernel() with open("HF_surface.txt", "a") as fl: fl.write("{} {}\n".format(test_dist, e + mol.energy_nuc())) ``` In this script, `e` is the FCI electronic energy computed earlier (`fci.direct_spin0.FCI().kernel(...)`), not the Hartree–Fock total energy from `mf`. Therefore, the script writes FCI energies into `HF_surface.txt`, making the HF baseline numerically identical to the exact FCI curve for every geometry in this script. This is a substantive functional error, not a minor formatting issue, and it invalidates any HF-vs-exact comparison produced by the script. Because the README explicitly describes these scripts as generating manuscript data, this calls into question the reliability of the code release for reproducing baseline comparisons. At minimum, it indicates that the code package needs corrective review and basic regression tests to ensure baselines differ where expected. Until corrected, the reproducibility claim is not fully met. References: [1] [Thomas Duguet et al., “Eigenvector Continuation and Projection-Based Emulators,” arXiv:2310.19419 (2024)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.19419.pdf)

Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The paper inaccurately claims that the inference of properties from the model can be achieved with the same computational scaling as (hybrid) density functional theory (DFT). "inference of properties from the model can be achieved in a scaling which is the same as (hybrid) density functional theory, rendering this a practical scheme." This claim is factually incorrect regarding the asymptotic scaling with respect to system size (number of orbitals $N$). The method requires transforming two-electron integrals (or two-body reduced density matrices) from the atomic orbital (AO) basis to the symmetrically orthogonalized atomic orbital (SAO) basis at each geometry. This transformation scales as $O(N^5)$ [2, 3], whereas standard hybrid DFT scales as $O(N^4)$ (or $O(N^3)$ with density fitting). `h2 = np.einsum("...ijkl,ai,bj,ck,dl->...abcd", h2, trafo, optimize="optimal")` The provided code in `electron_integral_utils.py` confirms that the four-index transformation is performed using `np.einsum`, which involves a computational cost proportional to $O(N^5)$. This exceeds the scaling of mean-field methods and is characteristic of correlated methods like MP2. "The computational effort of MP2 scales as $N^5$ with the size of the basis set." [2] Standard textbooks confirm that operations involving four-index integral transformations scale as $O(N^5)$, contradicting the claim of mean-field scaling. References: [2] A. Szabo and N. S. Ostlund, "Modern Quantum Chemistry: Introduction to Advanced Electronic Structure Theory", Dover Publications (1996). https://store.doverpublications.com/products/9780486691862

Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The proposed eigenvector continuation ansatz uses a linear combination of global many-body wave functions, which is not size-extensive and may degrade in accuracy for larger systems or dissociation limits. "we can instead interpolate the many-body electronic wave function itself through chemical space." The ansatz $|\Psi(\mathbf{R})\rangle = \sum_a c_a |\Psi(\mathbf{R}^{(a)})\rangle$ represents a linear subspace projection. For a system composed of non-interacting subsystems A and B, the exact ground state is a product $|\Psi_A \Psi_B\rangle$. A linear combination of product states does not generally preserve this product structure unless the basis size grows exponentially with the number of subsystems. This lack of size extensivity is a known limitation of configuration interaction (CI) and reduced basis methods [3], which contrasts with the size-extensive nature of Coupled Cluster or DFT. "The method of configuration interaction ... is not size consistent." [2] Since the method is mathematically equivalent to a CI in a specific basis (the training states), it inherits the lack of size extensivity, which is a critical property for applying the method to large molecular systems or studying dissociation processes where fragments separate. References: [2] A. Szabo and N. S. Ostlund, "Modern Quantum Chemistry: Introduction to Advanced Electronic Structure Theory", Dover Publications (1996). https://store.doverpublications.com/products/9780486691862 [3] T. Helgaker, P. Jorgensen, and J. Olsen, "Molecular Electronic-Structure Theory", Wiley (2000). https://www.wiley.com/en-us/Molecular+Electronic+Structure+Theory-p-9780470011874

E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The paper characterizes the approach as "practical" and "black-box", but this overlooks the significant complexity and fragility of generating the required high-accuracy training data. "inference of properties from the model ... rendering this a practical scheme." While the inference step is robust, the overall workflow depends entirely on the generation of accurate training wave functions (e.g., using DMRG or CAS). The paper itself admits that this step is prone to failure. "At a number of these training geometries, the HF and/or CCSD were not able to fully converge... DMRG training data was not able to correctly converge" The authors acknowledge that generating training data is difficult and failed for a significant fraction of points (e.g., 13 points for Zundel cation). This contradicts the earlier claim of the method being a "practical" solution that overcomes the lack of black-box character in correlated methods, as the method's success is contingent on the very calculations that are described as non-black-box.

Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The validation of the method is limited to small or low-dimensional systems (1D Hydrogen chain, Zundel cation), which does not sufficiently demonstrate its applicability to general 3D molecular dynamics of larger systems. "trajectory of the 30-atom hydrogen chain ... Zundel cation dynamics" The Hydrogen chain is a 1D model system, and the Zundel cation ($H_5O_2^+$) is a small molecule. The paper does not present results for larger, general 3D molecules where the complexity of the potential energy surface and the number of degrees of freedom would be significantly higher. "Gaussian approximation potentials: The accuracy of quantum mechanics, without the electrons" [4] In contrast, established machine learning potentials (like GAP) have been demonstrated on much larger and more complex systems (e.g., bulk silicon, water clusters). The limited scope of the evaluation makes it difficult to assess whether the proposed method can compete with or complement existing techniques for realistic chemical problems. References: [4] A. P. Bartók, M. C. Payne, R. Kondor, and G. Csányi, "Gaussian approximation potentials: The accuracy of quantum mechanics, without the electrons", Phys. Rev. Lett. 104, 136403 (2010). https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevLett.104.136403

Interpolating many-body wave functions for accelerated molecular dynamics on near-exact electronic surfaces Yannic Rath1,2* and George H. Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The method relies on the Symmetrically Orthogonalized Atomic Orbital (SAO) basis to map wave functions between geometries, but the stability and continuity of this basis in general chemical reactions are not sufficiently analyzed. "This allows the training wave functions to be transferred between the Hilbert spaces of different geometries by fixing their many-body probability amplitudes in this representation." The SAO basis is defined via the inverse square root of the overlap matrix $S^{-1/2}$. If the overlap matrix becomes ill-conditioned (e.g., when atoms are very close) or if the character of the atomic orbitals changes significantly (e.g., during bond breaking/formation where hybridization changes), the SAO basis functions may change rapidly or discontinuously. "Eigenvector continuation with subspace learning" [1] The original eigenvector continuation method assumes a smooth dependence of the Hamiltonian on parameters. In the proposed method, the "parameters" are nuclear coordinates, but the basis itself changes with coordinates. The paper does not provide a rigorous analysis of the smoothness of the Hamiltonian matrix elements in the SAO basis across global potential energy surfaces, particularly near dissociation limits or transition states. References: [1] D. Frame, R. He, I. Ipsen, D. Lee, D. Lee, and E. Rrapaj, "Eigenvector continuation with subspace learning", Phys. Rev. Lett. 121, 032501 (2018). https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevLett.121.032501

review_item: Observables that are interpolated with machine learning methods are usually calculated within milliseconds on test geometries, once the training is complete. On the other hand they need much more training data so that the training is numerically expensive. How do the timings compare to the method the authors propose?

review_item: If I understood correctly, the authors aim at calculating electronic properties along classical nuclear trajectories. The configurational space here is much smaller as compared to the case when the nuclei are quantum. Especially in Zundel the quantum effects of the nuclei are extremely important. Could their method also be applied here?

review_item: In the idea of moving to more complex and relevant chemical systems, made of more particles, it is not clear which limitations would be faced in the applications of the approach. Is it how infeasible would be to calculate several training points with accurate wavefunctions? Is it the number of training points to reach convergence that would drastically increase? Is the complexity of the potential energy surface that would not allow it?

review_item: The scaling of the method for increasing training states was not discussed carefully. It seems that matrix elements take M^4, whereas the number of states would form the matrix so T^2, so a total scaling of T^2M^4? And does the interpolated wavefunction require diagonalization of a T^2 matrix, which typically would take T^3 operations?

review_item: Would the author advise using CASSCF, RASSCF, or better CASPT2 to properly include both static and dynamic correlations? That could represent an important application for the method, especially in the framework of dynamics. CAS-based dynamics is highly employed and reliable, being able to accelerate it by reducing the number of electronic structure calls using the approach proposed would be an important achievement.

review_item: It is known that in CAS-based dynamics the stability of the active space is an issue, and preserving an active state along the dynamics is not guaranteed. In less problematic cases the approach proposed does not show any theoretical issue. But how do the authors think their method would perform on highly unstable active spaces?

E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. With a small number of accura
review_item: In Fig. 6, the instability of CAS-based dynamics is very pronounced. While the DMRG curves look sensible, CAS-based eigenvector continuation fails due to the inclusion of very disparate CAS spaces along the trajectory. When no crossing of BO surfaces is present, CASSCF with sensible choice (such as full valence space) should fix it. Alternatively, using automatic generation of valence active space (AVAS) might circumvent the issues without CASSCF. If crossing across different BO surfaces, more complexity is involved to interpolate between near the conical intersection of the surfaces.

review_item: The increasing challenges when increasing the number of particles become clearer in the dynamics of the Zundel cation. Although the reason why DMRG dynamics were not run is clearly explained by the authors, the lack of dynamics obtained at the accurate DMRG level, the one used for the training, does not allow a chemical evaluation of the profile obtained by molecular dynamics. The authors should try to run AIMD at the same level of theory that they use for the training, to benchmark their method and make it trustworthy for future applications.

This should be ideally run on an ensemble of trajectories.

review_item: In Figure 7, could the author comment on why the difference in energy between predicted and DMRG oscillates so intensely? Although the accuracy of the relevant parameters computed along the dynamics is satisfying, is it correct to have a nonconstant error in the overall electronic energy? Would be interesting to see what is the correlation leading to this phenomenon.

review_item: In cases of dissociations, the delocalization error (self-interaction error) is very common for DFT. A range-separated hybrid DFT is advised for a fair comparison. Perhaps that is the reason for the stable half charge of the hydronium at the end of the DFT dynamics in Fig. 5.

review_item: Through the text, the author uses the term “Chemical space”, although in a misleading way. Indeed, this term is often used to indicate a space that composes all the possible chemical compositions of molecules, while in the manuscript is used to indicate different geometrical conformations of the same system. As one of the limitations of the approach is indeed the incapability to transfer the training obtained for a system to another one, a rephrase of the term chemical space is advised.

review_item: Deriving the forces for these eigenvector continuation states is a very important development (shown in the supplementary information). This is especially useful since it is made public in an open-source code that seems to be understated, and we encourage the authors to underline that achievement. Furthermore, these derivatives with respect to the overlap matrix are typically called “Pulay forces” in the quantum chemistry literature, and we advise the authors to use that term instead of the Hellman-Feynman theorem which does not have any wavefunction derivatives with respect to nuclear displacement.

review_item: the introduction correctly states the problems of density functional based AIMD but fails to present a balanced view of current challenges and contemporary developments in the field. For example, AIMD of condensed phase systems utilizing correlated electronic structure methods have been developed (see e.g. Hutter et al DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-42913-7_58-1; Spura et al DOI: https://doi.org/10.1039/C4CP05192K). The introduction should reflect such recent advances.

E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item:
1. The manuscript contains a number of inaccuracies, both, pertaining to language and notation, for example:
- Page 8: “realistic chemistry on the exact electronic surface” – the term exact electronic surface typically refers to converged data with respect to electronic structure method and basis set, but the presented data are for a limited basis set and probably far from convergence. The problems of simulations are somehow acknowledged on page 9 (l. 497-503), but not reflected in the section heading.
- Page 9: “interpolating across ab initio potential energy surfaces” implies multiple potential energy surfaces, like excited states which are not covered in the manuscript.
- Page 9, eq. 8: the R-dependence of 2-ERI. Similarly, eq.9 drops the R-dependence of K and 2-ERI, and E_nuc(R) appears in eq. 5.6 but not in eq. 9 where it is explained. Such inaccuracies are particularly unfortunate, as the method is argued to benefit from the R-independent \(\Gamma_{	ext{Gamma_ab}}^{ij|kl}\) used to interpolate between geometries (see below 3.)

Booth2* 1National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, TW11 0LW, United Kingdom. 2Department of Physics and Thomas Young Centre, King’s College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, United Kingdom. *Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: transition two-body density matrices \(\Gamma_{\text{Gamma_ab}}^{ij|kl}\) are assumed to be independent of test nuclear configuration R which is at the core of the method for a common expansion of many body state. While the argument is made on page 3 “We can then choose to interpolate the state (and all resulting properties) between atomic configurations without re-optimizing the many-electron state by simply transferring the probability amplitudes, while ensuring the consistent SAO basis definition”, I am not fully convinced by the argument. As the SAO basis is R-dependent, in principle expansion coefficients (eq. 1) and \(\Gamma_{\text{Gamma_ab}}^{ij|kl}\) all become R-dependent, unless orthogonality of SEO between nuclear configurations is assured. In the current approach orthogonality between SAO is only assured for a fixed R leading in principle to a R- (and thus t-)dependent moving SAO basis. The justification of the method should be worked out more thoroughly.

E-mail(s): yannic.rath@npl.co.uk; george.booth@kcl.ac.uk; Abstract While there have been many developments in computational probes of both strongly-correlated molecular systems and machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, there remains a significant gap in capabilities in simulating accurate non-local electronic structure over timescales on which atoms move. We describe a practical approach to bridge these fields by interpolating the correlated many-electron state through chemical space, whilst avoiding the exponential complexity of these states. With a small number of accura
review_item: The relevance of the presented dynamics for H5O2+ is not fully convincing as no high level reference dynamics is presented. Deviations are recorded with respect to HF and B3LYP that both are prone to problems. Uncertainties of the dynamics are acknowledged to some extend on page 9 (l. 497-503) but no effort is undertaken to sufficiently corroborate the observations of the observed dynamics. For example (1) much more accurate and reliable functionals are available than B3LYP; alternatively (2) the PES of H5O2+ is known on a very high level of theory (see e.g. Huang et al DOI: https://doi.org/10.1063/1.1834500) and Born Oppenheimer dynamics on this surface should reproduce the current observations; (3) AIMD on the DMRG level of theory should be able to corroborate the method: 84 expansion points are already used, with the (very small) timestep 0.121 fs this amounts to ~10 fs propagation time on which deviations to HF and B3LYP are clearly recognizable. The argument that MD on DMRG level is not possible is thus not fully valid. To corroborate the observations, a more reliable reference has to be presented.
