Nuclear quantum effects (NQEs) are known to influence the structure and dynamics of protons, but their inclusion in a rigorous way through the path integral molecular dynamics (PIMD) formalism was so far beyo
review_item: Two other points of clarification. First, the idea of using the imaginary time Feynman path centroid in a quantum TST as done in the authors’ Eq. (S7.17) certainly did not originate with them nor with Manolopoulos. The earliest reference in that regard would be Voth, et al, J. Chem. Phys. **91**, 7749-7760 (1989) which should be cited along with some related earlier work by Gillan cited therein. Secondly, for calculating the quantum “dynamical” effects via a classical-like Bennett-Chandler formula, the authors appear to use the ad hoc RPMD approach. Here it should be clarified that RPMD is ad hoc and has no real theoretical justification in terms of actual quantum dynamics [see S. Jang, A. V. Sinitskiy, and G. A. Voth, J. Chem. Phys. **140**, 154103 (2014)]. PS-The authors should also not claim that the so-called “Matsubara dynamics” of Althorpe somehow “derives” RPMD as it does not. Matsubara dynamics is also quite approximate and one cannot really derive one approximation from another.

paper_title: Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning
paper_excerpt: Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning Sunghwan Choi (sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr) Korea Institute of Science and Technology Information https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4330-7710 Article Keywords: Transition state, Machine learning, Reaction path, Chemical reaction Posted Date: September 29th, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-2082595/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning Sunghwan Choi1* 1*Division of National Supercomputing, Korea Institute of Science and Technology Information, 245 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, 34141, Daejeon, Republic of Korea. Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: The paper's title and abstract claim the model predicts TS structures for "general chemical reactions" and "general organic reactions," but the training and evaluation exclusively use the Grambow dataset which contains only gas-phase unimolecular reactions with small molecules (up to 7 heavy atoms, CHNO only). This represents a narrow subset of organic chemistry that does not support the generalization claims. * Quote (Title and Abstract): "Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning" and "Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed." The term "general" strongly implies broad applicability across diverse reaction types, molecular sizes, and chemical systems. However, the model was trained and evaluated exclusively on unimolecular gas-phase reactions with molecules containing at most 7 heavy atoms. This is a highly restricted chemical space that excludes bimolecular reactions, solution-phase chemistry, larger molecules common in pharmaceutical and industrial applications, and elements beyond C, H, N, and O. "The proposed model is trained and validated using the organic reaction database released by Grambow et al." The Grambow database, as described in the original publication, contains reactions generated from molecules selected from GDB-7 (molecules with at most 7 heavy atoms). The database specifically states "All reactions are gas-phase calculations involving up to seven carbon, oxygen, or nitrogen atoms per molecule." This severely limits the chemical diversity and molecular complexity represented in the training data. * Quote from Grambow et al. [[1]](#citation-list): "We only considered molecules with at most seven heavy atoms (C, N, O). All molecules with six or fewer heavy atoms were selected from GDB-7 (~770) and a random selection of ~430 molecules were selected from the set with seven heavy atoms." The original dataset paper explicitly acknowledges the small molecule limitation. Drug molecules, natural products, and industrial chemicals typically contain 20-50 or more heavy atoms and include elements like halogens, sulfur, and phosphorus. Without evaluation on such systems, claims about "general" chemical reactions are not substantiated. * Quote from TSNet paper (Jackson et al.) [[2]](#citation-list): "TSNet is presented, a new end-to-end Siamese message-passing neural network based on tensor field networks shown to be capable of predicting transition state geometries. Also presented is a small dataset of SN2 reactions which includes transition state structures." Even the comparison method TSNet recognized the need to evaluate on different reaction types (SN2 reactions) beyond the Grambow dataset. The present paper does not demonstrate generalization to out-of-distribution reaction types or larger molecules, yet makes stronger claims about generality. References: [1] [Grambow, C.A., Pattanaik, L. & Green, W.H. Reactants, products, and transition states of elementary chemical reactions based on quantum chemistry. Sci Data 7, 137 (2020).](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41597-020-0460-4) [2] [Jackson, R., Zhang, W. & Pearson, J. TSNet: predicting transition state structures with tensor field networks and transfer learning. Chem. Sci. 12, 10022–10040 (2021).](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2021/sc/d1sc01206a)

Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: Intrinsic Reaction Coordinate (IRC) calculations, which are the gold standard for verifying that a transition state connects the correct reactant and product, were performed only on the 126 cases with energy errors greater than 0.1 kcal/mol. This represents approximately 11% of the test set, leaving 89% of predictions without rigorous chemical validation of their connectivity. "Intrinsic reaction coordinate (IRC) calculations are performed for the structures on right side of the red bar (126 cases)." Figure 4 shows that IRC calculations were performed only for structures with energy errors exceeding 0.1 kcal/mol. While these are the higher-error cases, chemical validity requires confirming that a TS structure actually connects to the intended reactant and product through IRC calculations. Without IRC verification, a structure with low energy error might still correspond to a different reaction pathway or be a different conformer of the TS. The 88.8% of cases below 0.1 kcal/mol were not IRC-validated. "88.8% of the optimization results have energy errors of less than 0.1 kcal/mol." While low energy error is encouraging, it does not guarantee chemical validity. A predicted TS structure that optimizes to a low-energy saddle point might represent a different elementary step than the reference reaction if the IRC path connects to different products. Standard practice in computational chemistry, as noted in multiple references, requires IRC calculations to confirm that transition states connect to the correct minima. * Quote from standard computational chemistry practice [[3]](#citation-list): "Intrinsic reaction coordinate (IRC) calculations were executed to confirm that transition states connect two relevant minima." The computational chemistry literature consistently emphasizes IRC calculations as essential validation for transition states. By performing IRC on only 11% of the test cases, the paper cannot claim that 93.8% of predictions are chemically valid—only that they converge to saddle points and have reasonable energies. "Distributions of absolute energy errors of structures whose reference product and reactant structures are acquired (orange) or not acquired (blue) by IRC calculations." Even among the IRC-validated cases, Figure 4(c) shows that some structures failed to recover the reference reactant and product, indicating that even converged structures with certain energy errors can correspond to incorrect reaction pathways. This reinforces the need for comprehensive IRC validation across all test cases. References: [3] [Unveiling the Origins of Enantioconvergence in Cobalt-Catalyzed Reactions - ACS Catalysis (2025)](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acscatal.5c06147)

Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: The paper claims that TSGen and TSNet were "trained with the same database," but provides insufficient details about whether these re-implementations faithfully reproduce the original methods, particularly regarding TSNet's transfer learning protocol which was identified as important for its performance. "To compare the performances against the other ML models, two existing models, TSGen and TSNet, are trained with the same train dataset." The paper states that comparison models were retrained but does not specify the hyperparameters used, whether the original architectures were exactly reproduced, or the source of the implementations. Both TSGen and TSNet papers provide specific details about their architectures and training that would need to be carefully followed for a fair comparison. "Both comparison models are trained with the same database as the database used for training of the proposed model." While using the same database is necessary for fair comparison, it is not sufficient. TSNet in particular relies on transfer learning from equilibrium molecular structures (the ANI-1 dataset) to improve its performance on transition states. If this pretraining step was omitted, the comparison would be unfair to TSNet. * Quote from TSNet paper (Jackson et al.) [[2]](#citation-list): "Finally, transfer learning, a low data remedial technique, is explored to understand the viability of pretraining TSNet on widely available chemical data may provide better starting points during training, faster convergence, and lower loss values." The TSNet paper explicitly emphasizes transfer learning as a key component of their approach. The present paper does not mention whether TSNet was pretrained on equilibrium structures before fine-tuning on transition states. If transfer learning was not performed, the reported ~15% error for TSNet on bond-forming/breaking pairs (versus ~5% for the proposed method) may not represent TSNet's full capability. "TSGen exhibited slightly better performance than TSNet. However, both models showed more than two-fold greater errors (for both error metrics) compared with the proposed model." The claim of two-fold improvement is significant but requires validation through independent replication. Without access to the actual code used for training or detailed hyperparameter specifications, the community cannot verify these comparisons. References: [2] [Jackson, R., Zhang, W. & Pearson, J. TSNet: predicting transition state structures with tensor field networks and transfer learning. Chem. Sci. 12, 10022–10040 (2021).](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2021/sc/d1sc01206a)

Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: The paper employs 90 trained models for ensemble predictions, which substantially increases computational resources and inference time, yet provides no systematic ablation study showing how performance scales with ensemble size or whether fewer models could achieve comparable results. "For the ensemble predictions, 90 trained models are employed." Using 90 models represents a very large ensemble that likely requires 90× the inference computation compared to a single model. While ensembles are known to improve accuracy, such a large number is unusual and warrants justification through an ablation study showing the marginal benefit of additional models. "Six different combinations of c and c' in Equation 5 are used to train the model and three independent training with different random seeds are performed for every combination of c and c'. Although all the hyperparameters are identical, depending on the random seed, ML model are differently trained. For each training, five best models are saved and used for the ensemble predictions." The ensemble construction methodology (6 hyperparameter settings × 3 random seeds × 5 saved models = 90 models) is described, but the paper does not analyze how much each factor contributes to the final performance. It is unclear whether using fewer hyperparameter settings, fewer random seeds, or fewer saved models per training would substantially degrade performance. "The error of the outputs of the single-model and ensemble include 3.681% and 3.407% (molecular MAPE), respectively, and 11.56pm and 10.70pm (molecular MAE), respectively." The improvement from single model to 90-model ensemble is relatively modest (~8% relative reduction in MAPE). Given the 90× increase in inference cost, a systematic study of performance vs. ensemble size would help users make informed decisions about the accuracy-efficiency tradeoff. Such ablation studies are standard practice in ensemble learning literature to demonstrate the value proposition of increased computational investment.

paper_title: Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning
paper_excerpt: Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning Sunghwan Choi (sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr) Korea Institute of Science and Technology Information https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4330-7710 Article Keywords: Transition state, Machine learning, Reaction path, Chemical reaction Posted Date: September 29th, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-2082595/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning Sunghwan Choi1* 1*Division of National Supercomputing, Korea Institute of Science and Technology Information, 245 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, 34141, Daejeon, Republic of Korea. Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: This paper’s primary chemical-validity conclusions (Validity, Conclusions) are not sufficiently supported because the main validation metric is saddle-point optimization convergence and energy agreement, while explicit pathway/connectivity verification (via IRC) is applied only to a small, biased subset; therefore the reported 93.8% “success ratio” does not reliably establish that predicted TS structures correspond to the intended reactant–product transformation. “The predicted TS structures result in a high success ratio (93.8%) of quantum chemical saddle-point optimizations, and 88.8% of the optimization results have energy errors of less than 0.1 kcal/mol.” This sentence elevates convergence rate and a very tight energy-difference threshold to headline evidence of correctness, but neither metric alone establishes that the optimized saddle point connects the intended reactant and product. A TS optimization can converge to an unintended saddle point (e.g., a different reaction channel or a conformational TS) while still achieving a stable first-order saddle point. Likewise, an energy close to a reference TS does not guarantee the same intrinsic reaction pathway or correct connectivity. In transition-state methodology, chemical validity is normally tied to whether the TS connects the target minima on the potential energy surface. Because the paper’s central claim is “prediction of TS structures” for reactions, the validation must directly test that relationship rather than relying primarily on optimizer behavior. “Among the 1196 test molecules, 1122 (93.8%) of the molecular structures are successfully converged. This high success ratio implies that the model inferences provide structures close to the reference ones.” The implication that convergence “implies” proximity to the reference is not logically guaranteed for TS searches, where optimization basins can lead to alternate saddle points or unintended reactions. The paper itself later notes non-uniqueness of reaction paths and that even large energy differences can still be classified as “IRC success,” which further weakens the inference that convergence implies correctness. Moreover, a high convergence rate can reflect that the predicted structure is a decent optimizer starting point, not that the ML prediction is chemically faithful to the intended transformation. Without systematically confirming reactant/product connectivity for converged structures, this statement over-interprets what convergence demonstrates. This affects the reliability of the paper’s headline performance claim. “The structures with errors greater than 0.1kcal/mol (shown on the right-hand side of the red line in Fig. 4 (b) ) are selected for testing (126 cases). If the reference reactant and product structures are obtained by IRC calculations, the cases are classified into the IRC success group, otherwise, they are classified into the IRC failure group.” This explicitly shows IRC validation is restricted to a subset selected by a post hoc energy-error threshold, rather than being applied broadly to the test set. Selecting only high-energy-error cases creates a biased sample that cannot support any global statement about what fraction of all converged TSs connect the intended reactants/products. It also leaves open the possibility that many low-energy-error cases still correspond to incorrect reaction pathways (e.g., wrong connectivity but similar energies within the same method). Because the paper uses the overall 93.8% convergence rate and 0.1 kcal/mol threshold as headline claims, the validation should be representative of those populations. Standard practice in TS validation is to use IRC (or equivalent connectivity checks) precisely because energy/convergence are insufficient. “Interestingly, this same products are generated by IRC validation of the reference TS structure.” This admission shows that even the reference TS in the dataset can lead, under the authors’ own validation pipeline, to products different from the labeled reference product. That makes the paper’s reliance on “reference” energies and geometries problematic as ground truth for validating chemical correctness. It also raises the possibility that some reported “successes” (convergence or small energy differences) are successes relative to a flawed reference rather than to the intended reaction transformation. In such settings, a robust evaluation would either (i) validate connectivity for a representative portion of the dataset or (ii) redefine success criteria in terms of recovered reactant/product identities rather than energy proximity. As written, the paper’s validation metrics are not robust to the acknowledged reference inconsistencies. “It then feeds the optimized transition state to two intrinsic reaction coordinate (IRC) calculations, which link the saddle point to its corresponding reactant and product PES minima by a mass-weighted downhill optimization.” [2](#ref2) This describes a standard, rigorous TS verification workflow used in closely related ML TS-generation work: after TS optimization, run forward and reverse IRC and verify recovered minima. Compared to this practice, the reviewed paper’s selective IRC testing (only 126 cases chosen by energy error) is insufficient to justify global correctness claims. The cited workflow clarifies why convergence to a saddle point is not enough: the key question is whether that saddle point connects the intended minima. In the reviewed paper, this connection is only partially tested, and therefore the reported global success ratios are overstated. Aligning with community practice would require applying IRC/connectivity checks to a much larger and representative portion of the test set. “Following the intrinsic reaction coordinate (IRC) implies following the steepest descent path from the transition state to reactants and products.” [5](#ref5) This definition underscores why IRC is central when claiming a TS corresponds to a specific reaction: it explicitly traces the path from the TS to reactant and product basins. The reviewed paper’s headline metrics (convergence and energy differences) do not provide this pathway information. Consequently, without broad IRC-based validation (or an equivalent connectivity verification), the paper cannot reliably claim that predicted TS structures correspond to the intended transformations across the dataset. The paper’s own example where the reference TS yields an unexpected product further motivates the need for path-based validation rather than energy-only metrics. This gap directly impacts validity because TS correctness is fundamentally a pathway/connectivity question.

paper_excerpt: Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning Sunghwan Choi (sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr) Korea Institute of Science and Technology Information https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4330-7710 Article Keywords: Transition state, Machine learning, Reaction path, Chemical reaction Posted Date: September 29th, 2022 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-2082595/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning Sunghwan Choi1* 1*Division of National Supercomputing, Korea Institute of Science and Technology Information, 245 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, 34141, Daejeon, Republic of Korea. Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: This paper makes an overgeneralized methodological claim (Validity, Conclusions) that the approach “can be extended to all types of chemical reactions” and “any type of element,” but the demonstrated training/validation domain is limited to small organic reactions and a narrow element set, so the stated scope is not supported by the evidence presented. “To achieve high accuracy with the satisfaction of various necessary invariances, the ML model contained PSI layers designed to handle any size of molecules and any type of element. This layer relies not on any prior knowledge of chemical reaction type but on reactant and product structures, thus can be extended to all types of chemical reactions without modifications.” This passage is a strong claim about universality across both molecular size and element types, extending beyond the experiments reported in the paper. Demonstrating size-extensivity or permutation invariance does not by itself demonstrate accuracy across arbitrarily large molecules, transition-metal chemistry, charged states, or elements not present in training. In chemical ML, “can be extended” typically requires either training on representative chemistry or explicit experiments showing transfer to new element sets or reaction classes. The paper does not provide such out-of-domain experiments or ablations demonstrating that embeddings/generalization remain reliable when introducing unseen elements. Therefore, the claim is not supported by the presented empirical evidence and risks misleading readers about the method’s validated scope. “In this study, two publicly available reaction databases are used. The first was released by Grambow et al.[28] … ωB97X-D3/def2-TZVP results (11961 reactions) … The second database includes 53 S_N2 reactions …” This sentence defines the effective experimental domain of the work: a single large dataset derived from a specific automated reaction-discovery pipeline and a very small SN2 set. These two datasets do not span “all types of chemical reactions” in the usual sense (e.g., multi-step mechanisms, organometallic catalysis, ionic and solvent-mediated reactions, excited states, etc.). The small SN2 dataset also limits the strength of any claims about generalization across reaction mechanisms. Because the paper ties broad scope to model design rather than to demonstrated transfer, the empirical support is not commensurate with the breadth of the claim. A more accurate conclusion would restrict the claim to the element/reaction types present in these datasets. “we used automated potential energy surface exploration to generate 12,000 organic reactions involving H, C, N, and O atoms calculated at the ωB97X-D3/def2-TZVP quantum chemistry level.” [1](#ref1) This establishes that the primary dataset used by the reviewed paper is restricted to a limited element set (H/C/N/O) and a specific computational level of theory. If the training data do not include other common elements in organic chemistry (e.g., halogens, sulfur, phosphorus) or any transition metals, the paper’s claim of “any type of element” cannot be empirically supported by the reported experiments. Generalization to unseen elements is nontrivial because learned embeddings and distance distributions can shift substantially when element types change. Therefore, the broad applicability claim conflicts with the scope of the dataset used for training and testing. At minimum, the paper would need separate evaluation on datasets with broader element coverage to justify such statements. “Because of the unfavorable scaling of quantum chemical calculations, we only considered molecules with at most seven heavy atoms (C, N, O).” [1](#ref1) This dataset design choice sharply constrains molecular size and composition, which is directly relevant to the reviewed paper’s “any size of molecules” claim. Even if the model architecture is formally size-extensive, accuracy and stability of predictions can degrade with increasing size due to long-range distance distributions, conformational complexity, and combinatorial growth in pairwise interactions. Without evaluation on larger molecules, it is not justified to claim practical applicability to “any size” TS structures. This mismatch between stated scope and demonstrated domain affects how readers should interpret the significance and usability of the method. It also suggests that additional experiments are needed before making general claims about scaling.

Read Full License Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning Sunghwan Choi1* 1*Division of National Supercomputing, Korea Institute of Science and Technology Information, 245 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, 34141, Daejeon, Republic of Korea. Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: The paper’s experimental reporting (Data and methodology) is insufficient for independent reproduction because the implementation and key hyperparameters are delegated to an unversioned external repository and supplementary material not contained in the main paper package, and the provided code artifacts do not include the described TS-prediction model or scripts. “The implemented model and scripts for training and inference are uploaded at https://gitlab.com/sunghwan.choi/learnts. All hyperparameters used to train the model are summarized in Table S1.” This statement places the essential implementation details and hyperparameters outside the main manuscript. For reproducibility, readers typically need an archived, versioned snapshot (commit hash, release tag, or DOI) and a complete configuration that reproduces reported results. A bare URL without a version identifier does not guarantee long-term accessibility or that the referenced code corresponds to the paper version. Additionally, “Table S1” is required to know model configuration, but it is not included in the main text and, in the provided materials, is not available as a readable supplementary table. As a result, the methodology as packaged is not sufficient for an independent group to reproduce the training or inference pipeline. “Supplementary Files This is a list of supplementary files associated with this preprint. Click to download. • v2Supportinginformation.docx” The paper explicitly depends on supplementary material for crucial training details (e.g., hyperparameters in Table S1), but the supplementary content is not integrated into the main preprint. In practice, missing or inaccessible supplementary files prevent verification of architecture, feature settings (e.g., Gaussian grid), loss weights, and training schedules—parameters that can materially affect outcomes in deep learning. Because the paper reports substantial performance improvements over prior models, the lack of a self-contained description makes it difficult to assess whether the comparison is fair and whether the results are robust. This is a significant reproducibility deficiency rather than a cosmetic documentation issue. Standard reproducibility expectations in ML-enabled chemistry papers include providing a complete experimental configuration and scripts to run the pipeline. ```markdown **The deep learning framework to pretrain and finetune AI models.** **Serving models?** Use [LitServe](https://github.com/Lightning-AI/litserve?utm_source=ptl_readme&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=ptl_readme) to build custom inference servers in pure Python. ``` This excerpt from the provided codebase indicates that the code artifact is the general PyTorch Lightning framework rather than the paper-specific TS prediction implementation (PSI layers, distance prediction, NLOpt reconstruction, TTA/ensemble logic, etc.). A reader cannot reproduce the reported experiments from a generic training framework alone; the core model definition, data preprocessing, and evaluation scripts are necessary. The mismatch between what is described (a bespoke PSI-based model for TS distance prediction) and what is provided (a general deep-learning framework) prevents replication and auditing of key choices. This is a major issue because the paper’s claims depend on nuanced methodological details (e.g., distance-matrix constraints, ensemble/NLOpt procedures). Without the actual implementation, reproducibility and detailed methodological scrutiny are not possible. “With the exception of the growing string method code … and the Q-Chem quantum chemistry package, all code necessary to reproduce the generated data is available on GitHub …” [1](#ref1) This illustrates a reproducibility standard for the underlying dataset used in the paper: the dataset authors provide a public, script-based pipeline describing how the data were generated. In contrast, the reviewed paper’s model-training pipeline is not similarly reproducible from the materials provided because its core code/hyperparameters are not available in an archived and directly runnable form. Given that the paper’s contribution is primarily methodological (a new TS structure predictor), its own training/inference code should meet at least the same standard of accessibility and specificity. Otherwise, independent verification cannot distinguish genuine methodological improvement from implementation-dependent effects. This comparison highlights why delegating critical information to non-archived supplementary sources is insufficient. “Our code is available on GitHub at https://github.com/PattanaikL/ts_gen.” [2](#ref2) This provides an example from a closely related TS-generation ML paper of how the community enables reproducibility: an explicit public code repository is given in the paper itself. The reviewed paper instead relies on an external GitLab URL without version pinning and, in the provided package, does not include the described model code. Because model performance can be sensitive to preprocessing details, training hyperparameters, and optimization procedures, having runnable code is crucial to validate and extend the work. The lack of readily accessible and complete code materially limits the scientific value of reported benchmarks and prevents straightforward follow-up studies. This is particularly important because the paper claims strong performance gains over TSGen and TSNet.
annotator_comments: The authors provide the code and it does not matter whether it is on GitHub or GitLab.

Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: The experimental design (Conclusions; Data and methodology) relies on a random 80/10/10 split, which is widely recognized to be an optimistic evaluation for chemical ML and does not substantiate the paper’s broad claims about generalization across reaction space. “The database, is split randomly into training, validation, and test sets with an 80-10-10 ratio.” A random split primarily measures interpolation performance under the same chemistry distribution, which can overestimate real-world generalization when near-duplicate molecules or closely related reactions appear across splits. For reaction and molecular datasets, more challenging splits are often used to approximate prospective generalization, such as scaffold splits or splits by reaction templates/mechanistic families. Because the paper’s narrative emphasizes “general” reactions and broad applicability, the evaluation should stress out-of-distribution settings rather than a single random split. The paper does not report sensitivity to alternative splits or any analysis of similarity between train and test reactions. This limits the strength of the conclusions drawn from the benchmark. “Here, a novel ML model that infers TS structures of general single-step reactions is proposed …” The claim of covering “general single-step reactions” implies the model can generalize beyond narrow subsets of chemistry. However, the reported evaluation setup (random split) does not directly test such generalization and therefore cannot fully support this framing. In chemical ML, “general” typically implies robustness to new scaffolds, new functional group combinations, or new reaction templates not seen during training. Without explicit out-of-distribution testing, readers cannot assess how performance degrades as test reactions become less similar to training reactions. Thus, the evaluation protocol does not match the breadth of the conclusion. “Random splitting, common in machine learning, is often not correct for chemical data.” [4](#ref4) This statement from a widely used molecular ML benchmark highlights that random splits can be misleading in chemistry due to structured chemical similarity and non-i.i.d. sampling. The reviewed paper’s sole reliance on a random split is therefore at odds with established best practices for evaluating chemical ML generalization. Given the paper’s claims of broad applicability, it is particularly important to avoid overly optimistic splits that might inflate apparent performance. The cited source does not imply random splits are always invalid, but it clearly motivates complementary split strategies to assess robustness. The reviewed paper does not provide such complementary evaluations. “Since scaffold splitting attempts to separate structurally different molecules into different subsets, it offers a greater challenge for learning algorithms than the random split.” [4](#ref4) This clarifies a concrete, commonly used alternative evaluation strategy that better probes generalization by separating chemical cores. For TS structure prediction, analogous strategies (e.g., splitting by reactant scaffold or reaction template) would more directly test whether the model generalizes to new chemistry rather than memorizing patterns in similar compounds. The reviewed paper does not report any such robustness checks, yet draws broad conclusions about general reactions. Without these evaluations, it is unclear whether reported improvements would persist in a prospective setting where target reactions differ substantially from those in training. This gap primarily affects the robustness of the paper’s conclusions.

Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: The proposed representation and reconstruction pipeline (Validity; Data and methodology) predicts only interatomic distances and reconstructs coordinates via nonlinear optimization, which is inherently ambiguous up to reflection and therefore cannot, in general, distinguish enantiomeric or mirror-related TS geometries; this undermines claims of general 3D TS structure prediction. “The model is designed to predict the interatomic distances of TS structures … The model outputs are then converted to the predicted interatomic distances (\( d^{TS}_{ij} \)) and used to generate the atomic positions in the TS structures (\( X^* \)) by nonlinear optimization.” This describes a pipeline where the predicted object is a distance matrix, not a unique 3D geometry, and the final coordinates are obtained by a reconstruction step. Distance matrices determine structures only up to rigid motions and reflection, so the reconstruction can yield mirror images that are indistinguishable in distance space. For many applications, especially stereoselective chemistry, mirror-related TS structures are not interchangeable because they can correspond to different stereochemical outcomes or interact differently with chiral environments. The paper does not discuss or evaluate this ambiguity, yet presents the method as predicting “TS structures” in a general sense. This methodological limitation affects the validity of broad structural claims. “The pair features satisfy the translational and rotational invariances because neither atomic numbers nor distances are changed by translation or rotation.” While translational and rotational invariance are desirable, the features described here are also invariant to reflection because distances are unchanged under mirror operations. That means the model input and output spaces cannot encode chirality or handedness information required to distinguish enantiomeric configurations. The paper does not provide an additional mechanism (e.g., signed volumes or chiral constraints) to break this symmetry in reconstruction. As a result, the method can be fundamentally unable to represent certain stereochemical distinctions even before considering model error. This is not a minor detail because TS stereochemistry can be central in organic reaction outcomes. “First, we directly output Cartesians as opposed to distance matrices. … it aﬀords our model the ability to diﬀerentiate enantiomers – something not possible with only a distance matrix.” [3](#ref3) This explicitly states, in a closely related TS structure prediction paper, that distance-matrix approaches cannot distinguish enantiomers, motivating direct Cartesian (equivariant) prediction. The reviewed paper’s method is distance-matrix-based and therefore inherits this limitation unless it introduces extra orientation/chirality constraints, which it does not. This directly conflicts with broad claims of predicting general TS “structures,” because in the presence of chirality the structure is not uniquely defined by distances alone. Even if some datasets do not emphasize enantioselective reactions, the paper’s own scope statements are general enough that this limitation should be addressed. The absence of such discussion or experiments weakens the methodological completeness. “Because we use the distance matrix to generate a TS structure, the target geometry is not entirely unique, and in a few cases, this fact results in a converged TS guess that contains one or more planes of symmetry with the ground truth geometry.” [2](#ref2) This is a direct acknowledgment, from a distance-matrix TS-generation approach, that non-uniqueness can manifest as mirror-related structures and can affect downstream optimization/verification. The reviewed paper similarly reconstructs coordinates from predicted distances, but it does not report whether such mirror ambiguities occur or how often they lead to failed or incorrect TS optimizations. Without quantifying or mitigating this issue, the method’s reliability for general TS prediction remains uncertain, particularly for cases where mirror symmetry is chemically consequential. The cited evidence shows this is not a hypothetical corner case but a known issue in the immediate literature. Therefore, the reviewed paper should either constrain chirality/orientation or explicitly scope its claims.

Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: The paper claims to outperform existing models (TSGen, TSNet) but uses a highly informative additional input (linearly interpolated structure) that the baselines do not use, making the comparison unfair and the attribution of performance to the model architecture questionable. "The model is designed to predict the interatomic distances of TS structures from three molecular structures: reactant, product and their linear interpolation." The proposed model explicitly takes the linearly interpolated structure as input. This structure provides a strong geometric prior, effectively giving the model an "intermediate" guess that is often close to the transition state, especially for the bond breaking/forming distances that are critical for the reaction. "TSGen... generate the initial interatomic distance and weight matrix from interatomic distances of reactant and product structures." As described in the paper (and the original TSGen paper), the baseline model TSGen only uses the reactant and product structures as input. It does not have access to the interpolated structure. "TSNet... directly predicts the atomic positions of TS structures." Similarly, the TSNet baseline is described as predicting TS structures directly, typically from reactant/product information, without the explicit guidance of a linear interpolation input. "The proposed model outperforms existing models particularly for predicting the rarely distributed cases (e.g. bond formation or breakage)." The paper attributes the superior performance on bond formation/breakage to the model architecture. However, since the linear interpolation explicitly provides intermediate distances for these bonds (averaging short and long distances), the model's success is likely driven by this input feature rather than the "Pair Sequence Interaction" architecture. Comparing the proposed model (Inputs: Reactant, Product, Interpolation) against baselines (Inputs: Reactant, Product) is unfair. An ablation study removing the interpolation input is necessary to validate the architectural claims.
annotator_comments: It is an architectural choice and it is valid to have advantages by using it.

Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: The use of a random split for the dataset likely leads to data leakage, overestimating the model's generalization capability, as the dataset contains chemically similar reactions generated by automated exploration. "The database, is split randomly into training, validation, and test sets with an 80-10-10 ratio." The paper explicitly states that a random split was used. In chemical datasets, random splitting is known to be problematic because it places structurally similar molecules or reactions (e.g., conformers or analogs) in both the training and test sets. "The first was released by Grambow et al.[28]... 11961 reactions are employed" The Grambow et al. dataset (ReactionData) consists of elementary reactions generated by automated exploration algorithms (like GSM). These algorithms often generate clusters of very similar reactions (e.g., the same reaction template applied to slightly different substrates). "Reactants, products, and transition states of elementary chemical reactions based on quantum chemistry" [1] The nature of the dataset implies high redundancy. By using a random split, the model can achieve high accuracy by memorizing the local manifold of reaction types seen during training, rather than learning generalizable chemical rules. A scaffold split or reaction-type split is required to properly evaluate the model's ability to generalize to new reactions. References: [1] Grambow, C.A., Pattanaik, L., Green, W.H.: Reactants, products, and transition states of elementary chemical reactions based on quantum chemistry. Scientific Data 7(1), 1–8 (2020). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-020-0460-4

Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: The model predicts the ratio of TS distance to interpolated distance, which is numerically unstable and prone to exploding values when the interpolated distance is small. "the model predicts the normalized distances of TS structures by those of the interpolated structure (\( d^{TS}_{ij}/d^{l}_{ij} \))." The target variable is defined as the ratio of the transition state distance to the linearly interpolated distance. This formulation is risky because if the denominator \( d^{l}_{ij} \) approaches zero, the ratio explodes. "Linear interpolated geometries are frequently used for TS search algorithms because of their reliable structures and low costs. [13, 37]" The paper cites linear interpolation (LST) as a standard method. However, LST in Cartesian coordinates (which is implied by "linearly interpolated structures") often results in unphysical geometries where atoms can overlap or pass through each other, leading to near-zero interatomic distances (\( d^{l}_{ij} \approx 0 \)). The paper does not describe any mechanism to handle these singularities (e.g., clipping, log-space prediction), which casts doubt on the robustness of the method.

Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: The loss function designed to enforce Euclidean constraints is mathematically insufficient as it only constrains the rank and trace of the distance matrix, failing to ensure the positive semi-definiteness required for a valid Euclidean Distance Matrix (EDM). "The 3D Euclidean distance matrices can have at most 5 nonzero eigenvalues and the the sum of eigenvalues should be zero.[40]" The paper correctly identifies that a distance matrix for points in 3D has a rank of at most 5 and a trace of 0. However, these are necessary but not sufficient conditions. "\( L_3 = \sum_{i=1}^{\min(5,N_{\text{atom}})} e_i + \sum_{i=\min(5,N_{\text{atom}})}^{N_{\text{atom}}} |e_i| \)" The proposed loss function \( L_3 \) minimizes the magnitude of eigenvalues beyond the 5th one and enforces the sum of the first 5 to be zero (implied by trace=0). However, a valid EDM must also satisfy specific inertia properties (e.g., having exactly one positive eigenvalue for the distance matrix itself, as discussed in Gower [4]). The proposed loss allows for invalid matrices (e.g., with incorrect eigenvalue signs) that satisfy the rank/trace conditions but cannot be embedded in 3D Euclidean space. References: [4] Gower, J.C.: Properties of Euclidean and non-Euclidean distance matrices. Linear Algebra and its Applications 67, 81–97 (1985). https://doi.org/10.1016/0024-3795(85)90187-9

review_item: The provided code archive contains the source code for the PyTorch Lightning library instead of the implementation of the proposed model, making it impossible to reproduce the results or verify the implementation details. "The implemented model and scripts for training and inference are uploaded at https://gitlab.com/sunghwan.choi/learnts." The paper claims the code is available. However, the code provided for review (in the `code` directory) does not contain the "learnts" project. "# Why PyTorch Lightning? Training models in plain PyTorch requires writing and maintaining a lot of repetitive engineering code." The `README.md` file in the provided `code` directory identifies the content as the PyTorch Lightning library itself. "name='lightning', version=__version__," The `setup.py` file in the provided `code` directory confirms that the package being installed is `lightning`, `pytorch_lightning`, and `lightning_fabric`. There is no source code for the proposed TS prediction model, preventing any form of reproducibility or code verification.
annotator_comments: The code is available.

review_item: This work exploits extensive reaction dataset of Grambow et al. which contains 10k reactions with atomic indices already mapped between reactant and product. If I understood correctly, in Pair sequence interface layer process, permutation-invariance and size-extensivity are preserved in transformer encoder but it seems correct atom mapping of reactant and product seem to be assumed in “Bidirectional GRU” step. However in other many cases, outside of this dataset, this correct atom mapping between reactant and product is not guaranteed to exist and itself is not a trivial problem. Although this model exhibits excellent performance within current dataset as training input, the author may need to address its applicability toward general molecular reactions. Please clarify in the manuscript if this model can be also generally applicable to any elementary reaction or it requires the atom-mapping process as preliminary step.

review_item: As this is being machine learning approach, its performance/accuracy scales with the size of the training set. In this work training-validation-test set as splited into 8-1-1 ratio, but would be interesting to present performance improvement with respect to number of training set (learning curve). Then readers might be able to expect approximated accuracy when trained with this model.

review_item: It is unclear to me how the author plans to handle chirality with their proposed model. As they state (in many places) most clearly on page 16, \"... the ML model predicts only interatomic distances\". It is, of course, an inconvenient truth that a stereogenic carbon with 4 unique substituents will produce an equivalent distance matrix of all enantiomers. I cannot interpret how the authors subsequent non-linear optimization step would correct this oversight. So, I ask the author to address this. Specifically, can the model succeed in stereochemical TS predictions? And if so, how?

review_item: Again, I was confused about the statement on page 9 regarding the error in absolute energy of the optimized structures. It isn't clear to me how one measures this error. I understand that a TS is predicted from the ML model and that is a \"predicted\" structure. Subsequently a user can optimize that predicted structure using a traditional saddle point optimization from first principles. Therefore an \"error\" can be determined from the difference in energies of these two structures. However, What structure does one compare to if they wish to assess the error of the \"optimized\" TS structure? Even the reference database used the same theoretical technique (namely, ωB97X-D3/def2-TZVP). I would ask that the author please elaborate on their explanation of \"error\" for optimized structures.

The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: I am not particularly in favour of the authors TTA approach. In this approach, the authors use both the forward and reverse directions of a reaction to essentially generate \"more\" data in a somewhat artificial way. The result may be that the prediction becomes directionally invariant but I believe there is a significant risk of overfitting in doing so. My preference would be for directional invariance to be \"baked in\" to the model. I am not, however, asking the author to develop a new model but I would demand to see more training statistics. A training curve is an important element of any ML paper that is sorely lacking in this work.

review_item: While it is nice that ~94% of the optimizations converged, one should perform frequency calculations and verify the presence of exactly one imaginary frequency to correctly characterize the saddle point. I highly recommend that this analysis be performed for all of the successful optimizations. They should definitely be performed for the 126 cases with energies differences higher than 0.1 kcal/mol. Without a proper frequency analysis, it is uncertain whether the structures are true TS structures (even if they were verified by IRC).

Read Full License Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning Sunghwan Choi1* 1*Division of National Supercomputing, Korea Institute of Science and Technology Information, 245 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, 34141, Daejeon, Republic of Korea. Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): sunghwanchoi@kisti.re.kr; Abstract The elucidation of transition state (TS) structures is essential for understanding the mechanisms of chemical reactions and exploring reaction networks. Despite advances in computational approaches, TS searches remain still a challenging problem due to the difficulty of constructing an initial structure and heavy computational costs. Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed. The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: There are few key limitations the author should highlight. First, that the reactant and product geometries used to generate TS guesses in this study were obtained from the Grambow dataset and are hence QM-optimized reactants and products. Thus, the model still requires QM-optimized reactants and products as input, which should be clearly stated in the manuscript. Second and more importantly, for any bimolecular reactions, the reactants and products need to be aligned in a reacting configuration, which is inherently the case in the Grambow dataset because the data were generated using the growing string method. A typical workflow performs opt-freq calculations on each species independently as traditional TST calculations expect partitions functions for each species rather than for the VDW complex that is found in the Grambow dataset. For reactions that have 2 or 3 products, which represent about 30% of this dataset, it would normally require another step to align these species to create the complex that was used to train the model presented here. Without a robust alignment scheme, it is unclear whether or not the presented model can be successfully applied to bimolecular reactions. This limitation should also be clearly stated in the paper.

The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: To train the TS model, Choi uses random splits on the Grambow dataset. However, several recent publications have noted the issues with using random splits on this dataset (see ref. 1 and 2). While the dataset contains ~12k unique reactions, it only contains ~1-2k unique reactants. So, when using a random split, some of the reactants may be duplicated in the test set. For TS geometry prediction, this is not a severe issue, but I suggest reproducing Fig. 2 using reactant-based scaffold splits (see ref. 2 for additional details). There’s no need to redo the QM optimization section. [1].Heid, E., & Green, W. H. (2021). Machine learning of reaction properties via learned representations of the condensed graph of reaction. Journal of chemical information and modeling. [2].Spiekermann, Kevin A., Lagnajit Pattanaik, and William H. Green. \"Fast Predictions of Reaction Barrier Heights: Toward Coupled-Cluster Accuracy.\" The Journal of Physical Chemistry A 126.25 (2022): 3976-3986.

The proposed model derives interatomic distances of a TS structure from atomic pair features reflecting reactant, product, and linearly interpolated structures. The model shows excellent accuracy, particularly for the atomic pairs where bond formation or breakage occurs. The predicted TS structures resu
review_item: The statement at the beginning of section 4.5 is not exactly true. First, the model from ref. 3 does in fact predict an interatomic distance matrix, but it reconstructs Cartesian coordinates from the predicted distance matrix in an end-to-end fashion. So the statement that “previous studies...directly infer the 3D atomic positions” isn’t strictly true. In fact, the nonlinear optimization used here is nearly identical to the one used in ref. 3, aside from the fact that ref. 3 used learned weights (w_ij). Second, an important work which was missed in the citations (ref. 4), uses a related approach of predicting a Columb matrix intermediate and obtaining the Cartesian coordinates through an optimization. [3].Pattanaik, L., Ingraham, J. B., Grambow, C. A., & Green, W. H. (2020). Generating transition states of isomerization reactions with deep learning. Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics, 22(41), 23618-23626. [4].Makoś, M. Z., Verma, N., Larson, E. C., Freindorf, M., & Kraka, E. (2021). Generative adversarial networks for transition state geometry prediction. The Journal of Chemical Physics, 155(2), 024116.

review_item: The choice of 90 models for the ensemble seems a bit excessive and may negatively impact downstream usage (i.e. longer runtimes). It would be nice to include some analysis on how many models are truly necessary as the choice of 90 seems arbitrary.

SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Data-Efficient and Accurate Interatomic Potentials Simon Batzner,1 Tess E. Smidt,2 Lixin Sun,1 Jonathan P. Mailoa,3 Mordechai Korubluth,3 Nicola Molinari,1 and Boris Kozinsky1,3 1John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Harvard University, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA 2Computational Research Division and Center for Advanced Mathematics for Energy Research Applications, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Berkeley, CA 94720, USA 3Robert Bosch Research and Technology Center, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA Th
review_item: The paper presents all benchmark results (Tables I-VI) as single point estimates without any error bars, standard deviations, or confidence intervals from multiple training runs, making it impossible to assess the statistical significance of the claimed performance improvements over competing methods. "TABLE I: MAE of force components on the MD-17 data set, trained on 1,000 configurations, forces in units of [meV/Å]. For the benzene molecule, two different data set exists from [17, 31] with different levels of accuracy in the DFT reference data." This table presents single MAE values for NequIP and comparison methods (SchNet, DimeNet, sGDML, FCHL19/GPR) without any uncertainty measures. Given that neural network training involves stochastic elements such as random weight initialization, data shuffling, and potentially different train/validation splits, the actual performance can vary significantly between runs. Without error bars from multiple independent training runs with different random seeds, it is impossible to determine whether the reported differences between methods (e.g., NequIP's 11.2 vs FCHL19/GPR's 11.2 meV/Å for Malonaldehyde) are statistically significant or within natural variance. "We train a series of networks with identical hyperparameters, but vary the training set sizes between 10 and 1,000 structures, sampled uniformly from the full data set, as well as a validation set consisting of 100 structures." Even for the ablation study in Figure 7 comparing l=0 vs l=0/l=1 networks, the paper does not report error bars despite the learning curves being critical evidence for the claimed data efficiency advantage of equivariant features. The figure shows smooth curves without any indication of variance across runs, which is inconsistent with typical neural network training behavior where different random seeds can lead to noticeable performance variations. "We measure the Li diffusivity in ten Nequip-driven MD simulations (computed via the slope of the mean square displacement), all of length 50 ps and started from different initial velocities, randomly sampled from a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution. We find a mean diffusivity of 1.42 × 10⁻⁵ cm²/s" While the paper runs ten MD simulations for diffusivity measurements, it only reports the mean value without the standard deviation across these ten runs. This is a missed opportunity to demonstrate statistical reproducibility. Additionally, this approach of running multiple simulations is not applied to the model training itself—no information is provided about variance in model performance when trained with different random seeds. "For each task, we trained four independent models using different random seeds and report the mean and standard deviation of these independent runs" [[1]](#citation-list) In contrast to this paper, the published version in Nature Communications includes error bars and multiple training runs, following the best practices established in contemporary machine learning benchmarking. The preprint version under review lacks this critical statistical rigor that is necessary to validate the claimed improvements. References: [1] Batzner, S. et al. "E(3)-equivariant graph neural networks for data-efficient and accurate interatomic potentials." [Nature Communications 13, 2453 (2022)](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-29939-5)
annotator_comments: Funnily, this AI reviewer references in the last quote the same paper published later in 2022.
