paper_excerpt: SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Data-Efficient and Accurate Interatomic Potentials Simon Batzner Harvard University Tess Smidt Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory https://orcid.org/0000-0001-5581-5344 Lixin Sun Massachusetts Institute of Technology Jonathan Mailoa Bosch Research and Technology Center Mordechai Kombluth Robert Bosch Research and Technology Center https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6705-8133 Nicola Molinari Harvard University Boris Kozinsky ( bkoz@seas.harvard.edu ) Harvard University https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0638-539X Article Keywords: Molecular Dynamics Simulations, Symmetry-aware Models, Equivariant Convolutions, Geometric Tensors Posted Date: March 1st, 2021 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-244137/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Version of Record: A version of this preprint was published at Nature Communications on May 4th, 2022. See the published version at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-29939-5. SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Data-Efficient and Accurate Interatomic Potentials Simon Batzner,1 Tess E. Smidt,2 Lixin Sun,1 Jonathan P. Mailoa,3 Mordechai Korubluth,3 Nicola Molinari,1 and Boris Kozinsky1,3 1John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Harvard University, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA 2Computational Research Division and Center for Advanced Mathematics for Energy Research Applications, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Berkeley, CA 94720, USA 3Robert Bosch Research and Technology Center, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA Th
review_item: The comparison between NequIP (133 training structures) and DeepMD (133,500 training structures) in Table III does not constitute a valid data efficiency comparison because it compares different architectures at vastly different operating points rather than showing learning curves for both methods. "A DeepMD NN-IP model was previously trained [15] for water and ice using a joint training set containing 133,500 reference calculations of these four systems. To assess data efficiency of the NequIP architecture, we similarly train a model jointly on all four parts of the data set, but using only 133 structures for training, i.e. 1000x fewer data." While this comparison shows that NequIP achieves better force RMSE with 133 structures than DeepMD does with 133,500 structures, it does not actually demonstrate "data efficiency" in the proper sense. Data efficiency would require showing that NequIP consistently achieves lower errors than DeepMD at the same training set size, or equivalently, that NequIP reaches a target accuracy with fewer data points. The comparison as presented could simply mean that NequIP is a fundamentally better architecture regardless of data size, rather than being more "data-efficient." "TABLE III: Root mean square error (RMSE) of force components on liquid water and the three ices in units of [meV/Å]. Note that the NequIP model was trained on < 0.1% of the training data of DeepMD." A proper data efficiency study would train both NequIP and DeepMD on identical training set sizes (e.g., 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 10000 structures) and plot learning curves showing error as a function of training data size. This is precisely what the paper does for the l=0 vs l=0/l=1 comparison in Figure 7, but fails to do for the DeepMD comparison. Without this, the 1000x claim is scientifically imprecise because we cannot determine where the crossover point is or whether DeepMD might actually be more data-efficient at certain data regimes. "The algorithm learns as new data become available. Empirical results have shown that this exponent typically remains fixed across different learning algorithms for a given data set, and different methods only shift the learning curve, leaving the log-log slope unaffected" [[1]](#citation-list) The published Nature Communications version acknowledges that different methods often have similar scaling exponents, meaning they learn at similar rates as data increases. The preprint's comparison at a single point (133 vs 133,500) cannot capture this scaling behavior, which is essential for characterizing data efficiency. References: [1] Batzner, S. et al. "E(3)-equivariant graph neural networks for data-efficient and accurate interatomic potentials." [Nature Communications 13, 2453 (2022)](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-29939-5)
annotator_comments: To a large extent, all three AI reviewers raise very similar points. Thus, the overall review would not cover many substantial points human reviewer raised.

paper_title: SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Data-Efficient and Accurate Interatomic Potentials
paper_excerpt: SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Data-Efficient and Accurate Interatomic Potentials Simon Batzner Harvard University Tess Smidt Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory https://orcid.org/0000-0001-5581-5344 Lixin Sun Massachusetts Institute of Technology Jonathan Mailoa Bosch Research and Technology Center Mordechai Kombluth Robert Bosch Research and Technology Center https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6705-8133 Nicola Molinari Harvard University Boris Kozinsky ( bkoz@seas.harvard.edu ) Harvard University https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0638-539X Article Keywords: Molecular Dynamics Simulations, Symmetry-aware Models, Equivariant Convolutions, Geometric Tensors Posted Date: March 1st, 2021 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-244137/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Version of Record: A version of this preprint was published at Nature Communications on May 4th, 2022. See the published version at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-29939-5. SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Data-Efficient and Accurate Interatomic Potentials Simon Batzner,1 Tess E. Smidt,2 Lixin Sun,1 Jonathan P. Mailoa,3 Mordechai Korubluth,3 Nicola Molinari,1 and Boris Kozinsky1,3 1John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Harvard University, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA 2Computational Research Division and Center for Advanced Mathematics for Energy Research Applications, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Berkeley, CA 94720, USA 3Robert Bosch Research and Technology Center, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA Th
review_item: The ablation study in Figure 7, which is central to the claim that equivariant features are responsible for data efficiency gains, does not adequately control for differences in model capacity (number of parameters) between the l=0 and l=0/l=1 networks. "In particular, we run a set of experiments for a system with a fixed number of training configurations in which we explicitly turn on or off interactions of higher order than l = 0. This defines two settings: first, we train the network with both l = 0 and l = 1 features and all five interactions as previously outlined in this work. Second, when all interactions involving l = 1 are turned off, this turns the network into a conventional invariant GNN-IP, involving only invariant convolutions over scalar features in a SchNet-style fashion." The l=0/l=1 network inherently has more parameters than the l=0-only network because it includes vector features and additional tensor product operations. The paper states "identical hyperparameters" are used, but this does not account for the fact that the l=0/l=1 network has fundamentally higher capacity. The observed performance difference could be attributed to increased model capacity rather than the specific benefits of equivariant operations. A controlled comparison would either match the total parameter count between architectures or show that increasing the l=0 network's parameters (e.g., more channels, layers) does not close the gap. "We further note, that in [39], a Behler-Parrinello Neural Network (BPNN) was trained on 1303 structures, yielding a RMSE of ≈ 120 meV/Å in forces when evaluated on the remaining 290 structures. We find that NequIP models trained with as little as 50 and 100 data points obtain RMSEs of 122.9 meV/Å and 93.3 meV/Å on their respective test sets" The comparison with Behler-Parrinello networks is similarly confounded by differences in architecture, hyperparameters, and potentially train/test splits. Without controlling for model size and computational cost, attributing the gains specifically to SE(3)-equivariance is not fully justified. "This suggests that it is indeed the use of tensor (in our specific case vector) features and equivariant convolutions that enables the high data efficiency of NequIP." This conclusion, while plausible, is not rigorously supported by the experimental design. The claim would be strengthened by showing that (a) l=0 networks with matched parameter count still underperform, (b) l=0 networks with significantly more parameters cannot match l=0/l=1 performance, or (c) theoretical analysis of the inductive bias provided by equivariance.

SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Data-Efficient and Accurate Interatomic Potentials Simon Batzner,1 Tess E. Smidt,2 Lixin Sun,1 Jonathan P. Mailoa,3 Mordechai Korubluth,3 Nicola Molinari,1 and Boris Kozinsky1,3 1John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Harvard University, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA 2Computational Research Division and Center for Advanced Mathematics for Energy Research Applications, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Berkeley, CA 94720, USA 3Robert Bosch Research and Technology Center, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA Th
review_item: For the formate decomposition on Cu system—a scientifically important reactive surface chemistry application—the paper provides only force MAE metrics without MD simulation validation, deferring dynamics analysis to future work. "TABLE IV: MAE of force components for Formate on Cu system, per-element basis. The training set consists of 2,500 structures, force units are [meV/Å]" Table IV reports per-element force MAE values (e.g., 2.2 meV/Å for Cu, 24.7 meV/Å for C) but provides no validation that these errors translate to physically meaningful MD simulations. For a reactive system involving C-H bond cleavage and the transition between monodentate, bidentate formate, and CO₂+H states, force accuracy alone is insufficient—the model must correctly predict reaction barriers, transition states, and dynamical pathways. "A more detailed analysis of the resulting dynamics will be subject of a separate study." This statement acknowledges the limitation but does not mitigate it for the purpose of this paper's claims. The abstract and introduction emphasize that NequIP "enables high-fidelity molecular dynamics simulations" and can reproduce "structural and kinetic properties... with very high fidelity." However, this claim is not substantiated for the reactive formate/Cu system, which is arguably one of the more challenging and scientifically relevant test cases presented. The deferral of dynamics analysis to future work is problematic when the paper's core claim is about MD simulation capability. "The decomposition process of formate on Cu involves configurations corresponding to the cleavage of the C-H bond, initial and intermediate states (monodentate, bidentate formate on Cu < 110 >) and final states (H ad-atom with a desorbed CO₂ in the gas phase)." Given that the dataset explicitly includes reaction intermediates and products, validating that NequIP can correctly simulate the reaction pathway would significantly strengthen the paper's claims. Other systems (Li₄P₂O₇, LiPS) receive extensive MD validation including RDF, ADF, and diffusivity comparisons with AIMD. The asymmetric treatment of the formate/Cu system leaves a significant gap in the validation.
annotator_comments: Formally, this criticism is correct, but it can be omitted because the authors provide molecular dynamics simulations for other benchmark systems.

SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Data-Efficient and Accurate Interatomic Potentials Simon Batzner,1 Tess E. Smidt,2 Lixin Sun,1 Jonathan P. Mailoa,3 Mordechai Korubluth,3 Nicola Molinari,1 and Boris Kozinsky1,3 1John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Harvard University, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA 2Computational Research Division and Center for Advanced Mathematics for Energy Research Applications, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Berkeley, CA 94720, USA 3Robert Bosch Research and Technology Center, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA Th
review_item: The paper does not provide systematic analysis of energy drift, simulation stability over long timescales, or demonstrate that NequIP-driven simulations remain stable for the duration required for practical applications. "We run a series of MD simulations with the NequIP potential trained on 2,500 structures in the NVT ensemble at the same temperature as the AIMD simulation for a total simulation time of 50 ps and a time step of 0.25 fs, which we found advantageous for reliability and stability of long simulations." The maximum simulation length reported is 50 ps, which may be insufficient to detect energy drift or identify rare unstable configurations that could cause simulation failures. Many practical applications of MD potentials require simulations on nanosecond to microsecond timescales. The phrase "we found advantageous for reliability and stability" suggests that some stability issues were encountered, but no quantitative analysis is provided (e.g., energy drift per nanosecond, percentage of simulations that completed successfully). "Recent studies have shown that force error metrics alone can be poor predictors of MD stability" [[2]](#citation-list) Contemporary work on machine learning force fields has highlighted the importance of testing stability beyond force accuracy metrics. Issues such as atomic clashes, unphysical bond breaking, and energy drift can occur even when force MAE is low. The paper would benefit from explicit stability testing, such as running extended simulations and reporting the number of steps until failure or measuring total energy conservation in NVE simulations. "Figure 6 shows the mean square displacements of Li for an example run." Presenting only "an example run" rather than aggregate statistics across multiple simulations does not provide sufficient evidence for reproducibility and stability. While the paper mentions running ten MD simulations for diffusivity, the MSD figure shows only one trajectory comparison, leaving questions about the consistency of dynamics across runs and the potential for outlier failures. References: [2] Fu, X. et al. "Forces are not Enough: Benchmark and Critical Evaluation for Machine Learning Force Fields with Molecular Simulations." [Transactions on Machine Learning Research (2023)](https://openreview.net/forum?id=A8pqQipwkt)

paper_title: SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Data-Efficient and Accurate Interatomic Potentials
paper_excerpt: SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Data-Efficient and Accurate Interatomic Potentials Simon Batzner Harvard University Tess Smidt Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory https://orcid.org/0000-0001-5581-5344 Lixin Sun Massachusetts Institute of Technology Jonathan Mailoa Bosch Research and Technology Center Mordechai Kombluth Robert Bosch Research and Technology Center https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6705-8133 Nicola Molinari Harvard University Boris Kozinsky ( bkoz@seas.harvard.edu ) Harvard University https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0638-539X Article Keywords: Molecular Dynamics Simulations, Symmetry-aware Models, Equivariant Convolutions, Geometric Tensors Posted Date: March 1st, 2021 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-244137/v1 License: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Read Full License Version of Record: A version of this preprint was published at Nature Communications on May 4th, 2022. See the published version at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-29939-5. SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Data-Efficient and Accurate Interatomic Potentials Simon Batzner,1 Tess E. Smidt,2 Lixin Sun,1 Jonathan P. Mailoa,3 Mordechai Korubluth,3 Nicola Molinari,1 and Boris Kozinsky1,3 1John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Harvard University, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA 2Computational Research Division and Center for Advanced Mathematics for Energy Research Applications, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Berkeley, CA 94720, USA 3Robert Bosch Research and Technology Center, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA Th
review_item: This paper’s headline conclusions about extreme data efficiency and superiority over prior ML interatomic potentials are not robust because key comparisons (notably the “1000× fewer data” DeepMD comparison) are evaluated on highly correlated MD trajectory frames with train/test splits that can leak near-duplicate configurations, undermining the validity of the reported “orders-of-magnitude” gains. "NequIP outperforms existing models with up to three orders of magnitude fewer training data, challenging the widely held belief that deep neural networks require massive training sets." This is the central, strongest quantitative claim in the abstract and it materially affects the paper’s conclusions about data efficiency. Because it is a comparative statement (“outperforms existing models”), it requires a tightly controlled evaluation protocol that rules out confounders. In MD-generated datasets, adjacent frames can be extremely similar, so “randomly sampled” training frames can have near-neighbors in the test set even if they are formally disjoint. If such leakage occurs, test errors can substantially underestimate true generalization error for deployment (e.g., new thermodynamic conditions or different trajectories). The paper does not establish that its orders-of-magnitude comparisons are robust to these well-known correlation/leakage effects. "To assess data efficiency of the NequIP architecture, we similarly train a model jointly on all four parts of the data set, but using only 133 structures for training, i.e. 1000x fewer data. The 133 structures were sampled uniformly from the full data set available online, consisting of water and ice structures, made up of a total of 140,000 frames, coming from the same MD trajectories that were used in the earlier work [15]. We also use a validation set of 50 frames and report the test accuracy on all remaining structures in the data set. ... We find that with 1000x fewer training data, NequIP significantly outperforms DeepMD on all four parts of the data set." The protocol explicitly samples training/validation frames from the same MD trajectories and then evaluates on “all remaining” frames, which is exactly the situation where temporal correlation can make held-out frames very similar to training frames. That similarity can make interpolation look like strong generalization, especially when the test set contains many near-duplicates of training points. The DeepMD numbers are attributed to a model “previously trained [15]”, but the paper does not specify whether DeepMD was evaluated on exactly the same held-out frames and with the same metric definition, leaving comparability unclear. Since the conclusion hinges on a 1000× data reduction, even modest evaluation mismatches or leakage can qualitatively change the interpretation. As written, the evidence does not justify that the reported 1000× advantage reflects a robust, apples-to-apples generalization improvement. "However, if the data has many similar conditions, e.g., as occurs for data generated from AIMD trajectories or small perturbations to existing structures, then these random CV approaches will yield overly optimistic predictions. The predictions will be overly optimistic due to the “twin” problem, where extremely similar data is present in both the train and test sets, and the model predictions are thus indicative of data that looks just like the training data." ([1](#citation-list)) This directly describes the failure mode of using random splits on trajectory data: even without explicit leakage, near-duplicate configurations across train/test can yield overly optimistic errors. The paper’s water/ice comparison and several other experiments explicitly sample “uniformly” from MD frames and evaluate on the remaining frames from the same trajectories, matching the scenario warned about here. Because the paper’s main scientific claim is exceptional data efficiency, any optimism bias in the evaluation can inflate the apparent sample-efficiency advantage. The quoted guidance also clarifies what more robust alternatives look like (e.g., testing on new conditions or later trajectory segments), which the paper does not consistently implement for its headline “orders-of-magnitude” comparisons. Therefore, this literature evidence supports that the reported gains may not reflect robust out-of-sample performance. "We show that the proposed scheme provides an efficient and accurate protocol in a variety of systems, including bulk materials and molecules. In all these cases, DPMD gives results that are essentially indistinguishable from the original data, at a cost that scales linearly with system size." ([4](#citation-list)) This highlights that DeepMD (the principal baseline used for the 1000× claim) is itself presented in the literature as an accurate, symmetry-preserving approach trained on ab initio labels. That makes the paper’s claim of “significantly outperforms DeepMD” with 1000× less data unusually strong, and therefore it warrants especially careful, transparent, and matched evaluation. When a baseline is described as reproducing “results ... essentially indistinguishable from the original data,” modest differences in dataset composition, splitting, and metric definitions can dominate apparent gaps. The paper does not provide enough detail to verify that the DeepMD and NequIP results were produced under identical test conditions. As a result, the conclusion that NequIP fundamentally changes the data requirement relative to DeepMD is not convincingly supported.
annotator_comments: While I agree with the points raised by this AI reviewer, I found them somewhat too lengthy and repetitive.

Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Harvard University, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA 2Computational Research Division and Center for Advanced Mathematics for Energy Research Applications, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Berkeley, CA 94720, USA 3Robert Bosch Research and Technology Center, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA Th
review_item: This paper’s methodological conclusion that the performance/data-efficiency gains are directly caused by the SE(3)-equivariant convolution architecture is not convincingly established, because the presented ablation is limited in scope and does not robustly isolate equivariance from other confounded factors (e.g., representation capacity, split variance, and evaluation difficulty). "We directly verify that the performance gains are connected with the unique SE(3)-equivariant convolution architecture of the new NequIP model." This is a causal attribution claim, not merely a descriptive performance report, and therefore requires especially careful experimental controls. In ML architecture studies, isolating a causal factor typically involves multiple controlled ablations, matched capacity, and repeated runs across splits/seeds to quantify variance. The paper later provides a specific ablation, but it is limited to a single test system and a single form of “turning off” vector interactions. Without stronger controls, the statement “directly verify” overstates what is actually demonstrated. Because the paper’s broader significance rests on equivariance being the key mechanism, this unsupported causal leap materially weakens the conclusions. "In order to gain insight into the reasons behind increased accuracy and data efficiency, we perform a series of experiments with the goal of isolating the effect of using equivariant convolutions of geometric tensors compared to invariant convolutions over scalars. ... Second, when all interactions involving \( l = 1 \) are turned off, this turns the network into a conventional invariant GNN-IP, involving only invariant convolutions over scalar features in a SchNet-style fashion." Turning off all \(l=1\) interactions changes not only equivariance properties but also the representational form and information flow (e.g., removing vector channels and associated tensor products). Even if hyperparameters are “identical,” the effective model class and expressivity can change substantially, so attributing all differences to equivariance alone is not cleanly justified. Additionally, labeling the ablated model as “SchNet-style” does not guarantee equivalence to SchNet’s exact architecture, optimization, and inductive biases, which makes interpretation ambiguous. Since the paper claims to have “isolated” the effect, it should demonstrate that other factors (capacity, parameter count, training stability, and evaluation variance) are controlled or measured. Without that, the ablation supports “equivariant performs better than this particular non-equivariant variant” but not the stronger causal claim. "Finally, we introduce a more challenging benchmark with chemical and structural variations that suggests the path for further work." ([5](#citation-list)) This highlights that, even within closely related molecular ML potential literature, the community recognizes the need for benchmarks that stress generalization beyond standard MD trajectory interpolation. The reviewed paper’s equivariance ablation is performed on a single bulk-water dataset with random sampling from a finite set of configurations, which may not reflect these harder generalization regimes. If the benchmark is not challenging enough (e.g., due to “twin” configurations or near-interpolation), architectural differences can be amplified or suppressed in ways that do not transfer to harder settings. Therefore, using only such an evaluation to claim a mechanistic explanation for broad data-efficiency gains is insufficient. The cited sentence supports the expectation that stronger, more varied benchmarks are needed before making sweeping causal conclusions about architectural choices. "Averaging an error metric over all *k* folds yields a measure that is less affected by random noise induced by the exact choice of train-/test-set splitting." ([2](#citation-list)) The reviewed paper’s architectural attribution relies on experiments that use randomly sampled training sets from correlated MD data, but it does not report repeated trials over multiple splits/seeds for the ablation. Without quantifying split-induced variance, it is difficult to know whether the observed gap is stable or partly an artifact of a favorable/unfavorable sampling. The cited sentence articulates why multi-split evaluation (e.g., k-fold or repeated splits) is important to reduce sensitivity to one particular split. Since the paper’s claim is about the *reason* behind data efficiency, it is not enough to show a single curve; it must show robustness across splits. This gap weakens the strength of the mechanistic conclusion.
annotator_comments: Similar to the previous item, this AI reviewer tends to overemphasize each quotation and comment. It would be sufficient to state that repeating the simulation with different starting points and random seeds is necessary to estimate the variance and thus draw more reliable conclusions. This AI reviewer also appears to misuse the term of "uncertainty".

review_item: The paper claims "favorable computational efficiency" but fails to provide a computational cost comparison (inference or training time) against other Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials (ML-IPs) such as SchNet, DimeNet, or DeepMD. "retaining favorable computational efficiency [1][13]." (Abstract) The abstract claims that the method retains favorable computational efficiency, implying it is competitive with or superior to existing ML-IP methods. However, this claim is not substantiated with relevant benchmarks against these methods. "Computational Efficiency ... we report the computational efficiency of NequIP and compare it to that of the ab-initio methods on two examples shown in this work: for a molecular system, we choose the Toluene molecule, computed at the CCSD(T)-level of theory [31]; for a material with periodic boundary conditions, we choose the Formate on Cu system, in which reference data were obtained with DFT." (Computational Efficiency section) The "Computational Efficiency" section only compares NequIP's inference time with *ab-initio* methods (CCSD(T) and DFT). While NequIP is naturally faster than *ab-initio* calculations, the relevant comparison for an ML-IP is against other ML models. Equivariant Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) involving tensor products are typically more computationally expensive than invariant GNNs like SchNet [1] or DeepMD [2]. The lack of comparison with these baselines hides the potential computational overhead of the proposed method. "It should be noted, however, that the evaluation cost of kernel methods scales linearly with the number of training configurations." (Introduction) The authors criticize kernel methods for their scaling behavior but do not provide a similar analysis or benchmark for their own method's scaling relative to other neural networks. Later work by the same authors (Allegro [3]) explicitly addresses the scalability limitations of NequIP, noting that its message-passing architecture limits parallelization and scaling, which further highlights the omission of this analysis in the current paper. References: [1] K. T. Schütt, H. E. Sauceda, P.-J. Kindermans, A. Tkatchenko, and K.-R. Müller, "SchNet – A deep learning architecture for molecules and materials," *The Journal of Chemical Physics*, vol. 148, no. 24, p. 241722, 2018. https://doi.org/10.1063/1.5019779 [2] L. Zhang, J. Han, H. Wang, R. Car, and W. E, "Deep Potential Molecular Dynamics: A Scalable Model with the Accuracy of Quantum Mechanics," *Physical Review Letters*, vol. 120, no. 14, p. 143001, 2018. https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevLett.120.143001 [3] A. Musaelian, S. Batzner, A. Johansson, L. Sun, C. J. Owen, M. Kornbluth, and B. Kozinsky, "Learning local equivariant representations for large-scale atomistic dynamics," *Nature Communications*, vol. 14, p. 579, 2023. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-36329-y

review_item: The claim that NequIP requires "orders of magnitude fewer training data" is not rigorously supported because the comparison with DeepMD is unfair (comparing NequIP trained on 133 samples against DeepMD trained on 133,500 samples) and lacks a direct control experiment. "NequIP outperforms existing models with up to three orders of magnitude fewer training data, challenging the widely held belief that deep neural networks require massive training sets." (Abstract) This is a very strong claim that requires rigorous evidence. The paper attempts to support this by comparing NequIP's performance on a small dataset with DeepMD's performance on a large dataset, rather than comparing both on the same small dataset. "Table III shows the comparison of the predictive force accuracy of NequIP trained on the 133 structures vs DeepMD trained on 133,500 structures. We find that with 1000x fewer training data, NequIP significantly outperforms DeepMD on all four parts of the data set." (Liquid Water and Ice Dynamics section) Table III compares NequIP (133 samples) with DeepMD (133,500 samples). This comparison is flawed because it does not show how DeepMD performs on 133 samples. It is possible that DeepMD would perform poorly, but without that data point, the "orders of magnitude" claim is an inference based on the assumption that DeepMD *needs* 133,500 samples to reach that performance, which is not proven. A fair comparison would involve training DeepMD (and other baselines) on the same 133 samples to demonstrate the specific advantage of NequIP in the low-data regime. "We further note, that in [39], a Behler-Parrinello Neural Network (BPNN) was trained on 1303 structures... We find that NequIP models trained with as little as 50 and 100 data points obtain RMSEs..." (Data Efficiency section) The comparison with BPNN [4] is made across different papers with potentially different data splits and evaluation protocols ("evaluated on the remaining 290 structures" for BPNN vs "on their respective test sets" for NequIP). This lacks the rigor of a controlled side-by-side comparison on the same data splits. References: [4] B. Cheng, E. A. Engel, J. Behler, C. Dellago, and M. Ceriotti, "Ab initio thermodynamics of liquid and solid water," *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences*, vol. 116, no. 4, pp. 1110–1115, 2019. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1815117116

review_item: The paper reports performance metrics (MAE/RMSE) as single values without uncertainty quantification (e.g., standard deviation over multiple runs), which is critical given the small dataset sizes used in the experiments. "The mean absolute error in the force components is shown in Table I in units of [meV/\AA]." (MD-17 small molecule dynamics section) Table I reports single MAE values for NequIP and baselines. When training on small datasets (e.g., 1,000 structures), the model's performance can be highly sensitive to the specific random split of the data and the weight initialization. Without error bars or standard deviations obtained from multiple independent training runs, it is difficult to assess the statistical significance of the reported improvements, especially where the differences are small (e.g., Toluene: 4.4 vs 6.1 meV/A). "Table III shows the comparison of the predictive force accuracy of NequIP trained on the 133 structures vs DeepMD trained on 133,500 structures." (Liquid Water and Ice Dynamics section) Similarly, Table III reports single RMSE values for a model trained on only 133 structures. The variance in performance is likely high in such a low-data regime. Reporting the mean and standard deviation over multiple folds or seeds is standard practice to ensure the robustness of the results. "We measure the Li diffusivity in ten Nequip-driven MD simulations... We find a mean diffusivity of 1.42 x 10^-5 cm^2/s... thus achieving a relative error of as little as 3%." (Lithium Phosphate Amorphous Glass Formation section) While the authors perform multiple MD simulations to estimate diffusivity, they do not report the uncertainty (e.g., standard deviation) of the diffusivity estimate itself, only the mean. Furthermore, the training of the model itself seems to be a single run, so the uncertainty arising from the model training process is not quantified.

review_item: The evaluation on complex material systems (Formate on Cu and Li4P2O7) lacks comparison with other ML-IP baselines, making it difficult to contextualize the performance and verify the claim of "state-of-the-art accuracy" for these specific systems. "The MAE of the predicted forces using a NequIP model trained on 2,500 structures is shown in Table IV demonstrating that NequIP is able to accurately model the interatomic forces for this complex reactive system." (Heterogeneous catalysis of formate dehydrogenation section) Table IV presents only NequIP's performance. Without comparing to other methods (e.g., SchNet, DeepMD) on the same dataset, it is impossible to determine if this performance is truly "state-of-the-art" or if simpler models could achieve similar accuracy. The paper claims to be accurate "compared to existing ML-IP methods across a wide variety of systems" (Introduction), but this comparison is absent for the material systems. "Table V shows the test set error in the force components on both the test set from the AIMD molten trajectory and the full AIMD quenched glass trajectory." (Lithium Phosphate Amorphous Glass Formation section) Similarly, Table V only reports NequIP's results. The lack of baselines weakens the argument that NequIP is uniquely capable of handling these complex systems compared to existing approaches.

review_item: Something that will immediately capture readers attention is why only forces are being compared against other models while in all the cited references there is always a comparison of both, total energies and forces. Furthermore, there is no discussion or arguments of why the authors only train the model on forces, while there are methods in the literature that even use energies and stress tensors for training on top of forces. Everything needs to be justified. To address these issues, the comparison of energies and forces against other methods must be in the article, otherwise it could send the wrong message to the reader, meaning that the energy predictions are not accurate enough in this model (NequIP).

review_item: On the applications side, the authors show two interesting examples, Lithium Thiophosphate (LiPS) and Lithium Phosphate (Li4P2O7). Table V summarizes the training performances for MAE of the forces. Interestingly, the learning capacity (according to this measure) is reached at 2500 training points for LiPS, converging to ~24 meV/A. This is actually a good error compared to other reported methodologies. Nevertheless, it is well known that the MAE gives a good overview of the general performance of the model, but it softens the error implications of outliers. It would be more illustrative to present both performances, MAE and RMSE, given that comparing the differences between these two measures provide some degree of insights to monitor large errors on individual samples (local environments). Again, a complete evaluation of the learning performance of the method is not possible given that the energy errors are not reported.

review_item: In the case of Li4P2O7, I cannot give a proper evaluation of the application if the RMSE are missing but more importantly, because of the missing energy errors. The energetics in diffusion is crucial, and because of this, I’m not sure how to judge the reported diffusivity values. Furthermore, this application needs a convergence analysis (diffusivity vs length of the simulations) since 50 ps is an extremely short time for such a macroscopic variable. Hence, in order to discard any possible artefacts of the NequIP, such analysis should be provided.

review_item: Although it is very impressive that the authors are able to train a MLP for water with 1000x fewer training data compared to DeepMD, bear in mind that the first generation of DeepMD (the un-smooth version) is highly data-inefficient due to the \"cusps\" introduced by the permutations of atoms. Also the water data set contains the whole MD trajectory, meaning that the training data is highly correlated so significant sparsification can be done before hampering the amount of information contained in the data set. I suggest that the authors consider including these more nuanced points, so that readers who are not familiar with the field won't be misled.
annotator_comments: I think, one could ask the authors to provide the results they would obtain with DeepMD using the same dataset size instead.

review_item: The idea of learnable radial functions in the decomposition of the convolution filters presented in Eq. (5) is an interesting one. Could the authors provide some justification for why it is necessary to learn these using a small NN as opposed to simply expanding them in a basis set or as a polynomial and then learning the coefficients of each as part of the overall training scheme. This idea has been investigated for learning activation functions in neural networks [see, for example, Goyal, et al. 101, 1-18 (2019)]. Wouldn't this be an easier scheme overall?
annotator_comments: I find the comparison with learning activation functions somewhat strange.

paper_title: First in vivo images of the human brain revealed with the Iseult 11.7T MRI scanner
paper_excerpt: # Abstract The understanding of the human brain is one of the main scientific challenges of the 21st century. In this context, in the early 2000s the French Atomic Energy Commission (CEA) launched a program to conceive and build the first human MRI scanner operating at 11.7T. More than a decade of developments followed to deliver the magnet while six more years were necessary to complete the commissioning and finally obtain approval from the regulatory agencies to acquire the first ever human brain images in vivo at such magnetic field. We deployed parallel transmission tools to mitigate the radiofrequency field inhomogeneity problem and tame the specific absorption rate. To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects. The unprecedented field strength combined with the acquisition techniques deployed yield T2 and T2*-weighted images reaching mesoscale resolutions within short acquisition times and with a high signal and contrast to noise ratio. - [Biological sciences/Biological techniques/Imaging/Magnetic resonance imaging](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FBiological%20techniques%2FImaging%2FMagnetic%20resonance%20imaging) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Diseases of the nervous system](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FDiseases%20of%20the%20nervous%20system) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Cognitive neuroscience](/b
review_item: The paper claims that "no significant differences between the two groups exposed at 11.7T and 0T could be identified" in safety-related measurements, yet no statistical power analysis is provided to determine whether the sample size of 20 subjects per group was adequate to detect meaningful differences, raising concerns about whether the absence of statistical significance reflects true safety or simply insufficient power. "Twenty volunteers (24.2±4.8 year old, 8 males) were scanned at 11.7T and performed the tests mentioned below. Another set of twenty volunteers (23.6±6.0 year old, 7 males) was exposed to a 0T, nocebo, field and underwent the same tests (except genotoxicity)." The study uses only 20 subjects per group for safety assessment. For a first-in-human safety study at such an unprecedented field strength, this sample size is notably small. Without a power analysis, readers cannot determine whether the study was adequately powered to detect clinically meaningful differences in physiological, vestibular, or cognitive measures between groups. The absence of detectable differences could reflect either true safety equivalence or simply Type II error due to inadequate statistical power. "In all tests performed throughout the protocol, no significant differences between the two groups exposed at 11.7T and 0T could be identified (extended data Fig. 3 and Table 1)." The authors interpret the lack of statistical significance as evidence for safety. However, in statistical hypothesis testing, failure to reject the null hypothesis does not confirm the null hypothesis—particularly when power is unknown. Standard practice in safety studies would include equivalence or non-inferiority analyses with predefined margins, or at minimum, report power calculations showing adequate sensitivity to detect effects of concern. "Methods: A total of 29 subjects were exposed to a 10.5 T MRI field and underwent vestibular, cognitive, and physiological testing before, during, and after exposure; for 26 subjects, testing and exposure were repeated within 2-4 weeks of the first visit." [[1]](#citation-1) The comparable 10.5T safety study by Grant et al. (2020) used a larger sample size (29 subjects) and implemented a repeated-measures design where 26 subjects underwent testing twice with 2-4 weeks separation. This design provides substantially more statistical power through within-subject comparisons and enables assessment of short-term versus longer-term effects. The current study's single-visit design with fewer subjects represents a less rigorous approach to safety assessment. References: [1] Grant, A., Metzger, G.J., Van de Moortele, P.F., et al. "10.5 T MRI static field effects on human cognitive, vestibular, and physiological function." Magnetic Resonance Imaging 73, 163-176 (2020). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32822819/
annotator_comments: This is the least interesting aspect of the paper. None of the 3 human reviewers even bothered with it, because the paper is using safety as an excuse to not talk about the MR engineering. The AI may be right (I am not a statistics expert), but it is a marginal issue.

review_item: The genotoxicity assessment was performed only for the 11.7T group using before/after intra-subject comparisons without a 0T control group, which is methodologically inconsistent with how other safety measures were evaluated and may be insufficient given that prior literature has demonstrated genotoxic effects from repeated MRI exposure. "Genotoxicity was also assessed but only for the 11.7T group with blood samples drawn before and after the 90 min exposure to perform intra-subject comparisons." While the behavioral, vestibular, and physiological tests included both 11.7T and 0T control groups for between-group comparisons, the genotoxicity assessment inexplicably omits the 0T control group. This creates an asymmetry in the study design where arguably the most concerning potential safety outcome (genetic damage) receives less rigorous evaluation than other measures. An intra-subject pre/post design without a control group cannot account for time-dependent changes or procedural effects unrelated to magnetic field exposure. "No statistically significant differences in micronuclei count for genotoxicity for each subject before versus after exposure at 11.7T were found either." The absence of statistical significance in micronuclei counts from a single 90-minute exposure in 20 subjects has limited interpretability. Reference [15] cited in the paper (Herate et al., 2022 [[4]](#citation-4)) specifically examined effects of "repeated brain MRI" at 3T. The current study's single-exposure design may be insufficient to detect cumulative genotoxic effects that could emerge with the repeated exposures typical of clinical or research MRI use. The authors cite this reference but do not adequately address why single-exposure testing is sufficient given the documented effects from repeated exposures. "Having shown that the repetitive exposure to 3T in MRI [15] induces mainly terminal deletions, the micronuclei protocol was performed with cytochalasin B addition at 68h after lymphocyte growth stimulation" The authors acknowledge that their reference [15] demonstrated genotoxic effects from repeated MRI exposure. However, the study design tests only single exposure, which does not address whether repeated 11.7T exposures would produce similar or enhanced effects. Given that chronic or repeated exposure has been identified as the relevant exposure scenario for genotoxicity concerns, the single-exposure design represents a significant gap in the safety assessment. References: [4] Herate, C., Brochard, P., De Vathaire, F., et al. "The effects of repeated brain MRI on chromosomal damage." European Radiology Experimental 6, 12 (2022). https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s41747-022-00264-2
annotator_comments: The entire safety portion of the paper is just filler to justify not talking about the MR engineering. This is partially the paper’s fault, but the human reviewers were not fooled by the misdirection.

paper_title: First in vivo images of the human brain revealed with the Iseult 11.7T MRI scanner
paper_excerpt: # Abstract The understanding of the human brain is one of the main scientific challenges of the 21st century. In this context, in the early 2000s the French Atomic Energy Commission (CEA) launched a program to conceive and build the first human MRI scanner operating at 11.7T. More than a decade of developments followed to deliver the magnet while six more years were necessary to complete the commissioning and finally obtain approval from the regulatory agencies to acquire the first ever human brain images in vivo at such magnetic field. We deployed parallel transmission tools to mitigate the radiofrequency field inhomogeneity problem and tame the specific absorption rate. To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects. The unprecedented field strength combined with the acquisition techniques deployed yield T2 and T2*-weighted images reaching mesoscale resolutions within short acquisition times and with a high signal and contrast to noise ratio. - [Biological sciences/Biological techniques/Imaging/Magnetic resonance imaging](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FBiological%20techniques%2FImaging%2FMagnetic%20resonance%20imaging) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Diseases of the nervous system](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FDiseases%20of%20the%20nervous%20system) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Cognitive neuroscience](/b
review_item: The paper claims superior signal-to-noise ratio at 11.7T compared to 3T and 7T, but provides only visual comparison of images acquired from different subjects using different RF coil configurations, without any quantitative SNR measurements to support the claim. "A 2D T2*-weighted acquisition was repeated with the same protocol (resolution, bandwidth, acquisition time, number of receive elements) at 3T and 7T on different volunteers. The comparison highlights the gain in signal:noise ratio (SNR) at 11.7T" The authors explicitly acknowledge using "different volunteers" for the cross-field comparison. Inter-subject variability in brain anatomy, tissue composition, and physiological noise is well-documented in MRI literature and can significantly confound comparisons. While matching acquisition parameters is appropriate, subject-to-subject variability can introduce substantial differences in image quality that are unrelated to field strength. A rigorous comparison would require within-subject measurements or use of standardized phantoms. "Acquisitions were performed with 1Tx (body coil)-32Rx, 8Tx-32Rx and 8Tx-32Rx head coils at 3T, 7T and 11.7T respectively." The RF coil configurations differ significantly across field strengths (1Tx body coil at 3T versus 8Tx dedicated head coils at 7T and 11.7T). Transmit coil efficiency, receive coil geometry, and channel count all substantially affect SNR independent of field strength. The use of a body coil for transmission at 3T versus optimized parallel transmit head coils at higher fields introduces additional confounds beyond field strength that are not accounted for in the comparison. "The comparison highlights the gain in signal:noise ratio (SNR) at 11.7T, which can be traded for higher spatial (or temporal) resolutions" Despite claiming SNR gains, the paper provides no quantitative SNR measurements. Standard MRI literature reports SNR values using established methodologies (e.g., signal from tissue regions divided by noise from background or difference images). The claim of "supralinear gain in sensitivity" referenced from prior work [[2]](#citation-2) was based on quantitative measurements. The current paper's visual comparison in Figure 2C, while visually compelling, does not provide the quantitative evidence needed to substantiate SNR claims or enable comparison with published values at other field strengths. "The SNR was measured at the center of a spherical water saline phantom with a gradient‐recalled echo sequence. Measurements were performed at NeuroSpin at 3, 7, and 11.7 T." [[3]](#citation-3) Notably, the same research group published a rigorous quantitative SNR comparison (Le Ster et al., 2022) using phantom measurements with controlled experimental conditions across field strengths. This methodology, which yielded precise SNR scaling data (B₀^1.94±0.16), would have been directly applicable to the current in vivo study. The absence of similar quantitative measurements in the current paper, despite the group's demonstrated capability, represents a missed opportunity to provide objective evidence for the claimed SNR benefits. References: [2] Pohmann, R., Speck, O., Scheffler, K. "Signal-to-noise ratio and MR tissue parameters in human brain imaging at 3, 7, and 9.4 tesla using current receive coil arrays." Magnetic Resonance in Medicine 75, 801-809 (2016). https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/mrm.25677 [3] Le Ster, C., Grant, A., Van de Moortele, P.F., et al. "Magnetic field strength dependent SNR gain at the center of a spherical phantom and up to 11.7T." Magnetic Resonance in Medicine 88, 2131-2138 (2022). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35849739/
annotator_comments: This is well-argued and correct!

paper_title: First in vivo images of the human brain revealed with the Iseult 11.7T MRI scanner
paper_excerpt: # Abstract The understanding of the human brain is one of the main scientific challenges of the 21st century. In this context, in the early 2000s the French Atomic Energy Commission (CEA) launched a program to conceive and build the first human MRI scanner operating at 11.7T. More than a decade of developments followed to deliver the magnet while six more years were necessary to complete the commissioning and finally obtain approval from the regulatory agencies to acquire the first ever human brain images in vivo at such magnetic field.
