To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects. The unprecedented field strength combined with the acquisition techniques deployed yield T2 and T2*-weighted images reaching mesoscale resolutions within short acquisition times and with a high signal and contrast to noise ratio. - [Biological sciences/Biological techniques/Imaging/Magnetic resonance imaging](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FBiological%20techniques%2FImaging%2FMagnetic%20resonance%20imaging) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Diseases of the nervous system](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FDiseases%20of%20the%20nervous%20system) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Cognitive neuroscience](/b
review_item: The safety follow-up consisting of phone calls up to one week after exposure is considerably shorter than the follow-up period used in comparable ultra-high field safety studies, limiting the ability to detect delayed or longer-term adverse effects. "Follow up phone calls by the NeuroSpin medical team up to 1 week after exposure at 11.7T did not indicate any abnormality." A one-week follow-up period via phone calls represents relatively limited post-exposure monitoring. While acute effects may manifest within this timeframe, some biological responses to novel environmental exposures may take longer to develop or detect. The informal nature of phone-based follow-up also limits the sensitivity of adverse event detection compared to structured clinical assessments or repeat objective testing. "Methods: A total of 29 subjects were exposed to a 10.5 T MRI field and underwent vestibular, cognitive, and physiological testing before, during, and after exposure; for 26 subjects, testing and exposure were repeated within 2-4 weeks of the first visit." [[1]](#citation-1) The Grant et al. (2020) study at 10.5T brought subjects back for formal repeat testing at 2-4 weeks post-exposure, allowing quantitative assessment of whether any acute effects persisted or resolved over a longer timeframe. This design enables detection of delayed-onset effects and provides objective data on recovery from any transient effects. The current study's one-week phone follow-up provides substantially less robust evidence regarding longer-term safety than the prior 10.5T study design. "Comparisons were made between short and long term time points in the study with respect to the parameters measured in the study; short term comparison included pre-vs-isocenter and pre-vs-post (1-24 h), while long term compared pre-exposures 2-4 weeks apart." [[1]](#citation-1) The 10.5T study explicitly distinguished between short-term (same day) and long-term (2-4 weeks) comparisons, recognizing that safety assessment requires evaluation at multiple timescales. By limiting follow-up to one week of phone contact without repeat objective measurements, the current study cannot adequately characterize the longer-term safety profile that would be expected for authorizing routine human imaging at this unprecedented field strength. References: [1] Grant, A., Metzger, G.J., Van de Moortele, P.F., et al. "10.5 T MRI static field effects on human cognitive, vestibular, and physiological function." Magnetic Resonance Imaging 73, 163-176 (2020). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32822819/
annotator_comments: Again irrelevant. The safety aspects belong in another paper and the duration of the follow-up is very field-specific. While it is true that the Grant et al. study looked at longer time horizons, I doubt that a follow-up of 1 week vs. 2-4 weeks makes any difference.

paper_title: First in vivo images of the human brain revealed with the Iseult 11.7T MRI scanner
paper_excerpt: # Abstract The understanding of the human brain is one of the main scientific challenges of the 21st century. In this context, in the early 2000s the French Atomic Energy Commission (CEA) launched a program to conceive and build the first human MRI scanner operating at 11.7T. More than a decade of developments followed to deliver the magnet while six more years were necessary to complete the commissioning and finally obtain approval from the regulatory agencies to acquire the first ever human brain images in vivo at such magnetic field. We deployed parallel transmission tools to mitigate the radiofrequency field inhomogeneity problem and tame the specific absorption rate. To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects. The unprecedented field strength combined with the acquisition techniques deployed yield T2 and T2*-weighted images reaching mesoscale resolutions within short acquisition times and with a high signal and contrast to noise ratio. - [Biological sciences/Biological techniques/Imaging/Magnetic resonance imaging](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FBiological%20techniques%2FImaging%2FMagnetic%20resonance%20imaging) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Diseases of the nervous system](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FDiseases%20of%20the%20nervous%20system) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Cognitive neuroscience](/b
review_item: This paper draws overly strong conclusions about human safety/tolerability at 11.7T from a small, short-term, endpoint-limited protocol, which weakens the validity of the safety claims made in the Abstract and Main text. "To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects." This pair of sentences is used to justify a broad safety conclusion, but “no evidence of adverse effects” is a non-equivalence statement that depends strongly on sample size, endpoint sensitivity, and follow-up duration. The described measures target a subset of plausible acute and subacute effects, but do not address detection of rare events or delayed outcomes, which is central when translating a first-in-human ultra-high-field exposure into a generalized “safety” assertion. The paper does not report an a priori safety endpoint hierarchy, minimally important differences, or any power/sensitivity analysis for the bioeffects outcomes, so readers cannot assess what magnitude of harm the study could have detected. In MRI safety contexts, the field’s novelty (11.7T whole-body) raises the bar for evidentiary strength, because the appropriate inference is typically “no concerning signal observed under this protocol” rather than “safe” in general. As written, the conclusion materially overreaches what the reported study design can support. "Twenty volunteers (24.2±4.8 year old, 8 males) were scanned at 11.7T and performed the tests mentioned below." A cohort of 20 healthy young adults is a reasonable starting point for feasibility, but it is not sufficient to substantiate broad claims about safety and innocuousness of 11.7T scanning, particularly for outcomes that may be infrequent or heterogeneous across populations. The inclusion criteria (18–40 years) also intentionally narrow the population, which further limits external validity of the “humans can safely tolerate” implication. The manuscript does not provide a rationale that this N is adequate for the multiple physiological, vestibular, cognitive, and genotoxicity endpoints it assesses. Without a quantitative sensitivity/power discussion, the negative findings are hard to interpret because the study could simply be underpowered for clinically meaningful or rare effects. In safety-focused first-in-human work, it is standard to explicitly frame conclusions as preliminary and bounded by the sample and follow-up. "Randomised trials offer the best approach for providing safety data as well as efficacy data, although they cannot detect rare harms." [[5]](#cite-5) This statement highlights a core limitation of using small human studies to infer safety: even rigorous randomized designs are structurally limited in detecting rare adverse events. The present paper’s safety assessment is based on two cohorts of 20 participants and is not positioned as an adequately powered harms-detection study, making the “no adverse effects” framing particularly vulnerable to false reassurance. The issue is amplified because the paper does not quantify uncertainty around adverse-event rates (e.g., confidence bounds) or specify which harms were actively adjudicated versus passively reported. The quoted guidance is consistent with common practice that early studies can screen for frequent/large effects but cannot establish absence of uncommon harms. Therefore, the manuscript’s broad safety conclusion is not justified by its study scale. "Authors should particularly avoid the common error of interpreting a non-significant result as indicating equivalence of interventions." [[5]](#cite-5) The paper repeatedly interprets “no significant differences” between 11.7T and 0T as evidence for innocuousness, but the statistical non-significance of group differences does not establish equivalence without an explicit equivalence/non-inferiority design and margins. The manuscript does not report equivalence margins, confidence intervals for group differences, or an analysis plan tailored to equivalence, so its interpretation risks precisely the error described in the CONSORT elaboration. This is not a cosmetic reporting issue: it directly affects whether the central safety conclusion is logically warranted. In safety studies, equivalence-style reasoning is often crucial because the claim of “no effect” must be defined quantitatively. The absence of that framework makes the conclusion unreliable. "Likewise, a relatively large p-value does not imply evidence in favor of the null hypothesis; many other hypotheses may be equally or more consistent with the observed data." [[2]](#cite-2) The manuscript’s safety narrative leans heavily on finding no statistically significant differences between groups and then describing the impact of field exposure as “negligible,” but the cited principle warns against that inference. A large p-value is compatible with a wide range of effect sizes when the study is small or measurements are noisy, which is exactly the situation for many physiological and behavioral endpoints in N=20 cohorts. The paper does not present confidence intervals for key endpoints or a structured equivalence analysis, so the reader cannot tell whether clinically meaningful differences have been excluded. In MRI safety evaluation, the magnitude and uncertainty of effects matter at least as much as whether a threshold is crossed, because small effects may still accumulate or interact with other stressors. This mismatch between statistical interpretation best practices and the manuscript’s conclusions undermines the robustness of its “no adverse effects” messaging. "A modified testing protocol informed by these results was devised with the goal of permitting increased enrollment while providing continued monitoring to evaluate field effects." [[4]](#cite-4) This prior ultra-high-field study (10.5T) illustrates a more cautious and methodologically standard posture: initial observations are treated as preliminary signals that motivate larger enrollment and continued monitoring, rather than being used to broadly confirm safety. The contrast matters because the paper under review claims that its “safety and imaging data” confirm applicability and that “humans can safely tolerate” 11.7T, despite having a comparable or smaller cohort than other UHF safety pilots. The quoted sentence evidences a field norm that early UHF human exposure studies remain vigilant to subtle or low-frequency effects and explicitly plan follow-on monitoring to strengthen inference. That norm is especially relevant at 11.7T, where multiple physiological systems (vestibular, cognitive, cardiovascular, thermal) could be affected. Compared to this practice, the reviewed paper’s conclusion is stronger than its evidentiary base. References: [2] Wasserstein RL, Lazar NA. *The ASA’s statement on p-values: context, process, and purpose*. The American Statistician (2016). https://sciences.ucf.edu/biology/d4lab/wp-content/uploads/sites/23/2017/03/WassersteinLazar2016.pdf [4] Grant A, Metzger GJ, Van de Moortele P-F, et al. *10.5 T MRI static field effects on human cognitive, vestibular, and physiological function* (Abstract). PubMed (PMID: 32822819). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32822819/ [5] Moher D, Hopewell S, Schulz KF, et al. *CONSORT 2010 Explanation and Elaboration: updated guidelines for reporting parallel group randomised trials* (PDF). BMJ (2010). https://www.bmj.com/content/bmj/340/bmj.c869.full.pdf
annotator_comments: Too verbose. I am not a statistics expert, but this is a paper about MR methodology. The first comment should not be six paragraphs about biological effects.

paper_title: First in vivo images of the human brain revealed with the Iseult 11.7T MRI scanner
paper_excerpt: # Abstract The understanding of the human brain is one of the main scientific challenges of the 21st century. In this context, in the early 2000s the French Atomic Energy Commission (CEA) launched a program to conceive and build the first human MRI scanner operating at 11.7T. More than a decade of developments followed to deliver the magnet while six more years were necessary to complete the commissioning and finally obtain approval from the regulatory agencies to acquire the first ever human brain images in vivo at such magnetic field. We deployed parallel transmission tools to mitigate the radiofrequency field inhomogeneity problem and tame the specific absorption rate. To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects. The unprecedented field strength combined with the acquisition techniques deployed yield T2 and T2*-weighted images reaching mesoscale resolutions within short acquisition times and with a high signal and contrast to noise ratio. - [Biological sciences/Biological techniques/Imaging/Magnetic resonance imaging](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FBiological%20techniques%2FImaging%2FMagnetic%20resonance%20imaging) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Diseases of the nervous system](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FDiseases%20of%20the%20nervous%20system) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Cognitive neuroscience](/b
review_item: This paper’s conclusion that 11.7T provides a higher SNR (and related contrast-to-noise benefits) is not robustly supported because the 3T/7T/11.7T comparisons are performed on different subjects and different transmit/receive configurations, and no quantitative SNR/CNR measurement protocol is reported for the comparison. "A 2D T2*-weighted acquisition was repeated with the same protocol (resolution, bandwidth, acquisition time, number of receive elements) at 3T and 7T on different volunteers. The comparison highlights the gain in signal:noise ratio (SNR) at 11.7T..." The paper uses a cross-subject comparison to argue a field-strength-driven SNR gain, but SNR is sensitive to subject anatomy/positioning, motion, and physiological noise, so “different volunteers” introduces an uncontrolled confound. The text also limits “same protocol” to a subset of parameters, leaving other contributors (coil loading, shimming success, flip-angle accuracy, reconstruction settings) potentially different across sites/systems. Because the comparison is not within-subject and the paper does not present quantitative SNR estimation (with uncertainties), the evidence is largely qualitative and cannot cleanly attribute observed differences to B0. In standard MRI methodology, field-strength SNR comparisons are typically designed to isolate B0 effects by holding subjects and coil geometry as constant as possible. As written, the paper’s phrasing overstates causal attribution from an under-controlled comparison. "Acquisitions were performed with 1Tx (body coil)-32Rx, 8Tx-32Rx and 8Tx-32Rx head coils at 3T, 7T and 11.7T respectively." This sentence directly indicates that the transmit chain differs substantially across field strengths (body-coil transmit at 3T versus multi-channel transmit at 7T/11.7T), which alters flip-angle uniformity and achievable signal even at fixed nominal sequence parameters. Coil design is a major determinant of receive sensitivity and parallel imaging behavior, so changing coil type makes it difficult to attribute differences to B0 alone. The paper does not provide a calibration/normalization method to separate coil-related gains from field-strength gains (e.g., intrinsic SNR modeling, flip-angle correction, or controlled phantom normalization). In ultra-high field, coil performance and B1 inhomogeneity are known to be major limiting factors, making this confound especially consequential. Therefore, the claimed field-strength “gain in SNR” is not methodologically well supported. "The same human subjects were scanned at all three field strengths, using identical sequence parameters and similar 31- or 32-channel receive coil arrays." [[3]](#cite-3) This published field-strength comparison provides a clear methodological contrast: it removes major confounds by scanning the same subjects and using matched acquisition parameters and comparable receive arrays. That design choice is widely regarded as necessary to make credible statements about how SNR scales with B0 in vivo, because it limits inter-subject variability and coil-related effects. The reviewed paper explicitly does the opposite (different subjects and different transmit strategies), so its evidence does not reach the rigor level commonly expected for quantitative SNR claims. Importantly, the literature example shows that controlling these factors is feasible and standard for SNR scaling studies, undermining any implication that qualitative cross-subject figures are sufficient. This gap weakens the reliability of the manuscript’s interpretation. "The g-factors did not show the expected decrease, indicating a dominating role of coil design." [[3]](#cite-3) This result emphasizes that hardware (especially coil design) can dominate performance metrics even when field strength changes, which directly bears on the reviewed paper’s use of different coil/transmit configurations at 3T/7T/11.7T. If coil design can dominate acceleration penalties and effective sensitivity, then attributing observed image differences to B0 without a controlled design is unreliable. The reviewed paper does not report g-factor, SNR maps, or reconstruction comparability across systems, so the potential coil-dominance confound remains unaddressed. This is a major issue because the paper’s broader claims about “high signal and contrast to noise ratio” at 11.7T implicitly point to B0 as the primary cause. The literature indicates that without controlling or quantifying coil effects, such causal claims are not robust. References: [3] Pohmann R, Speck O, Scheffler K. *Signal-to-noise ratio and MR tissue parameters in human brain imaging at 3, 7, and 9.4 tesla using current receive coil arrays* (Abstract). PubMed (PMID: 25820458). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25820458/
annotator_comments: Too verbose. The previous two AI reviewers did a much better job pinpointing the main issues.

paper_title: First in vivo images of the human brain revealed with the Iseult 11.7T MRI scanner
paper_excerpt: # Abstract The understanding of the human brain is one of the main scientific challenges of the 21st century. In this context, in the early 2000s the French Atomic Energy Commission (CEA) launched a program to conceive and build the first human MRI scanner operating at 11.7T. More than a decade of developments followed to deliver the magnet while six more years were necessary to complete the commissioning and finally obtain approval from the regulatory agencies to acquire the first ever human brain images in vivo at such magnetic field. We deployed parallel transmission tools to mitigate the radiofrequency field inhomogeneity problem and tame the specific absorption rate. To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects. The unprecedented field strength combined with the acquisition techniques deployed yield T2 and T2*-weighted images reaching mesoscale resolutions within short acquisition times and with a high signal and contrast to noise ratio. - [Biological sciences/Biological techniques/Imaging/Magnetic resonance imaging](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FBiological%20techniques%2FImaging%2FMagnetic%20resonance%20imaging) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Diseases of the nervous system](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FDiseases%20of%20the%20nervous%20system) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Cognitive neuroscience](/b
review_item: The manuscript’s methodological reporting is insufficient to evaluate whether the claimed SAR mitigation and pTx safety control are robust across subjects, because key safety-relevant quantities (e.g., achieved SAR margins, hotspot validation, and sensitivity to motion) are not presented for the 11.7T pTx protocols. "We deployed parallel transmission tools to mitigate the radiofrequency field inhomogeneity problem and tame the specific absorption rate." This statement links the main technical contribution (pTx) to a core human safety requirement (SAR control), but it is presented as a high-level assertion without the quantitative details necessary for independent evaluation. At ultra-high field, SAR limitations and local hotspots are among the primary safety constraints, so a reader needs to see at least the operational SAR limits used, how conservatism was set, and how compliance was verified in vivo. The paper later mentions virtual observation points and constrained pulse design, but does not provide the actual SAR values (global/head SAR and local 10g SAR), margins to limits, or per-subject distributions that would substantiate “tamed” SAR. Because the safety conclusion depends on this control being reliable for all scanned subjects, the lack of quantitative reporting undermines the credibility of the safety claim. This is a methodological transparency gap that directly affects validity. "The GRAPE pulse was a universal RF pulse [28] and was designed offline on a database constituted of the 6 first subject field maps." A universal pulse designed from a limited database can be practical, but it raises two safety-relevant questions: how robust is it to out-of-distribution anatomy/positioning, and how conservative are the SAR constraints under variability. The manuscript does not report any validation results showing that the universal pulse maintained SAR compliance and flip-angle targets across the remaining subjects beyond the six used to design it. In pTx, performance robustness and SAR robustness are not guaranteed to coincide; a pulse can appear to work (acceptable flip-angle error) while still creating higher local SAR hotspots in some anatomies. Without reporting robustness statistics or worst-case analyses, readers cannot judge whether the approach generalizes safely beyond the design set. This is particularly important because the paper’s conclusions generalize to “humans” rather than to the six-map database. "It enables spatial field manipulation, but at the same time, it may create hotspots by focusing the RF fields. This means that accurate subject-specific SAR measurement is mandatory to operate pTx appropriately." [[1]](#cite-1) This safety review summarizes a widely accepted concern in high-field pTx: hotspot creation is an intrinsic risk, and safe operation depends on accurate (often subject-specific) SAR assessment. The reviewed paper does not report subject-specific SAR verification, nor does it describe any empirical validation (e.g., temperature monitoring or phantom verification) that would allow independent assessment of SAR modeling accuracy at 11.7T. While the paper mentions virtual observation points, it does not show how conservative the VOP set was, what head models were used, or whether model mismatch was accounted for. Given the “mandatory” nature of accurate SAR assessment emphasized in the literature, omission of these details is a major methodological gap rather than a minor reporting choice. Consequently, the reader cannot verify that the pTx approach was safely controlled across participants. "They compared conventional quadrature excitation and multi-spoke parallel-transmit excitation after RF shimming and found that the local SAR increased by more than 100% in 1 and 4 cm3 for quadrature and parallel-transmit excitation, respectively, in the worst case where the head model was shifted by 20 and 10 mm in right and posterior directions, respectively." [[1]](#cite-1) This quoted finding highlights that relatively plausible head-position changes can dramatically increase local SAR under pTx, which is directly relevant for a 90-minute session where motion is likely. The reviewed paper acknowledges motion problems for image quality but does not connect motion to SAR safety, and it does not report how head stabilization and motion were handled from a safety standpoint. Without a motion-sensitivity analysis or conservative SAR margins that explicitly account for potential motion, the claim that SAR was “tamed” is not fully supported. In ultra-high-field first-in-human studies, demonstrating safety robustness to realistic deviations (including motion) is an important part of safety reporting. The absence of this analysis reduces confidence that the protocol is safe beyond idealized conditions. References: [1] Okada T, Akasaka T, Thuy DHD, Isa T. *Safety for Human MR Scanners at 7T*. Magnetic Resonance in Medical Sciences (2022). https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9618930/

To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects. The unprecedented field strength combined with the acquisition techniques deployed yield T2 and T2*-weighted images reaching mesoscale resolutions within short acquisition times and with a high signal and contrast to noise ratio. - [Biological sciences/Biological techniques/Imaging/Magnetic resonance imaging](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FBiological%20techniques%2FImaging%2FMagnetic%20resonance%20imaging) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Diseases of the nervous system](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FDiseases%20of%20the%20nervous%20system) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Cognitive neuroscience](/b
review_item: The paper’s causal interpretation that observed bioeffects outcomes reflect “no effect of field exposure” (rather than psychological or procedural confounds) is weakened by the non-randomized sequential cohort design and limited reporting on blinding effectiveness and protocol stability. "Those tests were performed on the 20 subjects scanned at 11.7T and on another cohort of 20 subjects with the magnetic field switched off, to disentangle any effect from a possible psychological bias (nocebo effect)." The manuscript explicitly frames the 0T cohort as enabling causal disentanglement of field effects from psychological bias, which is a strong claim that typically requires careful control of allocation, blinding, and protocol stability. However, the paper does not report objective checks of blinding success (e.g., whether subjects correctly guessed condition, or whether staff were blinded) beyond stating that participants were not informed. It also does not report whether any protocol elements changed over time (e.g., operator practice, task instructions, sequence tweaks) that could differentially affect the first and second cohort. Because “nocebo disentanglement” is a central support for the safety conclusion, these missing design/reporting elements weaken the internal validity of the comparison. In the absence of such controls, “no significant differences” is consistent with multiple alternative explanations beyond “no field effect.” "To minimize the number of field ramps for the magnet, the study first included the 0T group, followed by the 11.7T one." This sentence makes clear that group assignment was not randomized and that the cohorts were run in a fixed order, which introduces temporal confounding (learning effects in operators, protocol drift, seasonal/organizational changes, or differing recruitment). In biomedical research, non-randomized sequential allocation can bias group comparisons even when baseline demographics appear similar, particularly for behavioral and subjective endpoints. The manuscript does not provide mitigation strategies for this design choice (e.g., alternating conditions, pre-registered analysis, or sensitivity analyses), nor does it present evidence that measurement conditions were stable across the study period. Because the core inference is based on between-group differences (11.7T vs 0T), the fixed-order design reduces the credibility of the causal claim. This is a substantial internal-validity limitation rather than a minor procedural detail. "Vertigo was apparently more frequent and severe at 7T, but unpleasantness by moving in and out of the scanners was noted even at 0T, although the severity was higher at 7T. Moreover, five subjects noticed mild magnetic field at 0T." [[1]](#cite-1) This literature quote demonstrates two problems that directly apply to the reviewed paper’s nocebo-control logic: (i) subjective discomfort can occur even in 0T mock conditions, and (ii) participants may infer (correctly or incorrectly) field presence, compromising blinding. The reviewed paper tries to mimic gradient noise and keep subjects uninformed, but it does not report whether subjects detected the condition or whether expectancy differed between cohorts. The literature indicates that such factors can materially influence subjective and behavioral outcomes, meaning that simple “0T + same instructions” may be insufficient to fully isolate nocebo effects. This matters because the paper’s conclusion hinges on the idea that psychological bias is neutralized by design. Without explicit checks, the disentanglement claim is not well supported. "Proper randomisation reduces selection bias at trial entry and is the crucial component of high quality RCTs." [[5]](#cite-5) While the reviewed study is not a therapeutic RCT, it makes a causal claim that depends on between-group comparability, which is exactly the context where randomization is the standard bias-reduction tool. The paper’s stated reason for fixed ordering (minimizing field ramps) is operationally understandable, but it does not address the resulting risk of bias in interpreting group differences as field effects. The CONSORT elaboration emphasizes that without randomization, selection and confounding biases can prejudice comparisons, especially when outcomes include subjective experiences and cognitive task performance. The reviewed manuscript’s reporting does not include compensatory design features (e.g., cross-over design, counterbalancing, or randomized assignment) that would restore the strength of the causal inference. Therefore, the claimed separation of nocebo and field effects is methodologically weaker than presented. References: [1] Okada T, Akasaka T, Thuy DHD, Isa T. *Safety for Human MR Scanners at 7T*. Magnetic Resonance in Medical Sciences (2022). https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9618930/ [5] Moher D, Hopewell S, Schulz KF, et al. *CONSORT 2010 Explanation and Elaboration: updated guidelines for reporting parallel group randomised trials* (PDF). BMJ (2010). https://www.bmj.com/content/bmj/340/bmj.c869.full.pdf

We deployed parallel transmission tools to mitigate the radiofrequency field inhomogeneity problem and tame the specific absorption rate. To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects. The unprecedented field strength combined with the acquisition techniques deployed yield T2 and T2*-weighted images reaching mesoscale resolutions within short acquisition times and with a high signal and contrast to noise ratio. - [Biological sciences/Biological techniques/Imaging/Magnetic resonance imaging](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FBiological%20techniques%2FImaging%2FMagnetic%20resonance%20imaging) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Diseases of the nervous system](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FDiseases%20of%20the%20nervous%20system) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Cognitive neuroscience](/b
review_item: The manuscript fails to provide a detailed description of the imaging sequences and reconstruction methods used to acquire the "first in vivo images", violating reproducibility standards. "We tackled this problem by deploying parallel transmission hardware and software, first developed by the laboratory at 7T and further optimized for 11.7T (Methods)." The "Methods" section referenced here is missing from the manuscript. The only methods sections present are "Genotoxic tests", "Behavioral tests", and "Balance tests", leaving the core imaging methodology undefined. "T2 and T2*-weighted acquisitions were successfully performed by deploying the parallel transmission techniques (Fig. 2)." No sequence parameters (e.g., TR, TE, Flip Angle, Bandwidth, Matrix size, Acceleration factor) are provided for these acquisitions. Without these details, it is impossible for other researchers to reproduce the study or assess the technical quality of the images. "Acquisition parameters... must be reported." [1] The COBIDAS guidelines, a standard for neuroimaging reporting, explicitly state that full acquisition parameters must be reported to ensure reproducibility and transparency. References: [1] Nichols, T. E., et al. (2017). Best practices in data analysis and sharing in neuroimaging using MRI. Nature Neuroscience, 20(3), 299-303. https://www.nature.com/articles/nn.4500
annotator_comments: The AI made factual errors. The sequence parameters were reported, and the methods section starts with this. It is true that the MRI methodology does not have a subheading, which is why the AI got confused.

review_item: The paper relies on qualitative statements to assess image quality and SNR gain, lacking quantitative metrics essential for validating the performance of the new 11.7T scanner. "The presented in vivo human brain images acquired at 11.7T thereby reveal good signal homogeneity consistent with the simulations, without severe RF field inhomogeneity artefacts." "Good" is a subjective descriptor. The authors do not present B1+ maps or quantitative homogeneity metrics (e.g., coefficient of variation) for the in vivo data to objectively demonstrate the performance of the parallel transmission system at 11.7T. "The comparison highlights the gain in signal:noise ratio (SNR) at 11.7T, which can be traded for higher spatial (or temporal) resolutions..." No SNR values, maps, or measurements are provided to quantify this claimed gain. A scientific comparison of field strengths requires quantitative data to substantiate claims of improvement. "Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) maps were calculated... The SNR increased supralinearly with field strength..." [2] Previous studies comparing field strengths (e.g., Pohmann et al.) provide detailed quantitative SNR maps and measurements to validate expected gains, setting a standard that this paper fails to meet. References: [2] Pohmann, R., Speck, O., & Scheffler, K. (2016). Signal-to-noise ratio and MR tissue parameters in human brain imaging at 3, 7, and 9.4 Tesla using current receive coil arrays. Magnetic Resonance in Medicine, 75(2), 801-809. https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/mrm.25677
annotator_comments: Good point and well-argued!

review_item: Given the unprecedented field strength, the description of safety monitoring, particularly regarding RF heating (SAR), is insufficient. "The specific absorption rate (SAR), also growing with Bo [10], was tamed with virtual observation points [11] and dedicated RF pulse design algorithms [12]." While the method is named, no data is shown on the actual SAR levels monitored during the scans or the validation of the Virtual Observation Points (VOP) model at 11.7T. Given the high risk of local heating at this frequency, detailed safety data is crucial. "The data shows no evidence of adverse effects." The paper focuses on physiological and behavioral side effects but omits the technical safety data (e.g., temperature simulations vs. measurements) that would prove the RF safety of the imaging session itself.

review_item: The provided code repository only contains scripts for the behavioral task (ANT), while the core technical innovation (pTx pulse design and reconstruction) is not shared. "We tackled this problem by deploying parallel transmission hardware and software..." The paper attributes the success of the imaging to this software, yet it is not provided. ``` ANT-R task ============= ... `ant-r-classic.py` is a script implementing the ANT-R task described in Fan, J. et al (2009). ``` The `README.md` in the provided code repository confirms that the code is exclusively for the "Attentional Network Task" (behavioral), not for the MRI pulse design or image reconstruction. "The code repository... contains several Python scripts implementing variants of the ANT task." The absence of the imaging code prevents reproduction of the pTx pulses and image reconstruction, which are the primary technical contributions enabling the 11.7T images.
annotator_comments: Impressive that the AI looked at the code and realized there is nothing about the MRI reconstruction. It missed that some of the links are broken even for the ANT-R task.

review_item: The comparison with 7T is limited to a single T2*-weighted protocol and does not benchmark against state-of-the-art 7T imaging. "A 2D T2*-weighted acquisition was repeated with the same protocol (resolution, bandwidth, acquisition time, number of receive elements) at 3T and 7T..." Using the "same protocol" across field strengths is often suboptimal because relaxation times (T1, T2*) change with field strength. A fair comparison would use protocols optimized for each field strength to demonstrate the true potential of 11.7T over 7T. "Images indicate sufficient SNR to boost further spatial resolution or decrease acquisition time in the future." Without a comparison to optimized 7T images (which can also achieve very high resolution), the claim that 11.7T provides a unique advantage is not fully supported by the evidence presented.
annotator_comments: This could be argued better, but to do this the AI would need to be able to analyze the images. It went by text only, did not download the figures from Figshare, so it gave boilerplate feedback.

review_item: In order to maximize the impact of this scientific report, it is important to provide open access to the initial data presented in figure 2. This will pique the curiosity of the community and greatly increase the number of citations for this study. Although the authors have stated that \"All data supporting the findings of this study are available within the paper and its Extended Method,\" I was unable to locate this information in the materials provided for review. Sharing data is now more accessible than ever through platforms like neurovault.org, which I highly recommend to the authors.
annotator_comments: While the comment is spot on, that reviewer did not perform basic SNR analysis once the data were shared. A simple SNR analysis of the shared images from extended data figure 1 gives the following result, which invalidates the paper’s claims 3T_t2star_gre2d_tra_0.2x0.2x1.0mm3.nii: SNR = 7.33 7T_t2star_gre2d_tra_0.2x0.2x1mm3.nii: SNR = 4.79 11.7T_t2star_gre2d_tra_0.2x0.2x1.0mm3.nii: SNR = 12.60 This makes no sense theoretically, and it may be because the 7T image is not representative. In theory, the 7T image should have SNR~15 (instead of 5), whereas the 11.7T should have SNR > 25 (instead of 12.6). Something is off and I cannot understand it with the limited data that is shared.

paper_title: First in vivo images of the human brain revealed with the Iseult 11.7T MRI scanner
paper_excerpt: # Abstract The understanding of the human brain is one of the main scientific challenges of the 21st century. In this context, in the early 2000s the French Atomic Energy Commission (CEA) launched a program to conceive and build the first human MRI scanner operating at 11.7T. More than a decade of developments followed to deliver the magnet while six more years were necessary to complete the commissioning and finally obtain approval from the regulatory agencies to acquire the first ever human brain images in vivo at such magnetic field. We deployed parallel transmission tools to mitigate the radiofrequency field inhomogeneity problem and tame the specific absorption rate. To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects. The unprecedented field strength combined with the acquisition techniques deployed yield T2 and T2*-weighted images reaching mesoscale resolutions within short acquisition times and with a high signal and contrast to noise ratio. - [Biological sciences/Biological techniques/Imaging/Magnetic resonance imaging](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FBiological%20techniques%2FImaging%2FMagnetic%20resonance%20imaging) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Diseases of the nervous system](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FDiseases%20of%20the%20nervous%20system) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Cognitive neuroscience](/b
review_item: While the authors briefly mention next steps in the technical developments, I miss a more detailed discussion of how much these will improve SNR (compared to theoretical possible SNR) and resolution (e.g. with the improved motion mitigation strategies) and other aspects.

review_item: In that sense I was also somewhat surprised that the authors didn’t attempt to break any “records” for resolution. For instance, doi: 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2013.08.013 acquired images at 9.4T with 0.13x0.13x0.8 mm^3 and doi: 10.1371/journal.pone.0133921 presented 0.44 mm isotropic resolution. Without robbing any novelty from the current work, it may be beneficial to at least discuss these previous works and state the highest state-of-the-art resolutions in other fields (perhaps also vis-a-vis scan time and motion effects).

review_item: In Fig 2 it is unclear which exact conditions were used in each of the 3/7/11.7 T measurements. Would they be expected to only vary in resolution (i.e. were TE/TR adjusted for the different field T2(*/T1) or were they performed under the same conditions (TE/TR)? If the latter, contrast is expected to vary, and to make a good comparison it is recommended to match the contrast conditions while only varying resolution if possible. Please clarify.

review_item: The paper's central motivation for measuring the 583 MHz transition is that it is "isolated" from other hyperfine transitions, which should reduce systematic line-pulling effects. However, the discovery of significant n=3 state population introduces substantial line-pulling that undermines this advantage, yet the paper still concludes this is "the best candidate for future precision M LS measurements" without adequately demonstrating how the proposed mitigation strategies would work in practice. "Due to the reduced influence of other nearby transitions, the systematic uncertainties related to line-pulling would become negligible." This statement from Section I presents the key motivation for choosing the 583 MHz transition over the other allowed transitions at 1140 MHz and 1326 MHz. The paper follows the approach of the most precise hydrogen Lamb shift measurements ([Bezginov et al., Science 365, 1007 (2019)][1]) which selected the F=0→F=1 transition specifically because it is isolated. However, the authors' own experimental results reveal that while this transition may be isolated from other n=2 hyperfine levels, it is significantly contaminated by n=3 states that were not initially considered in the measurement design. "In our measurement, the comparably large population of 3S states distorts the line-shape and introduces line-pulling, which might seem to defeat the purpose of choosing the isolated n = 2 transition." This statement from Section IV explicitly acknowledges that the supposed advantage of the isolated transition is compromised by the 3S contamination. The detected B₃S/B₂S ratio of 0.20(4) means that roughly one-fifth of the detected signal comes from n=3 states, which is substantial. The fit parameters change dramatically when 3S is included: the frequency offset shifts from -24.8(74) MHz to 2.3(68) MHz—a change of ~27 MHz that far exceeds the final quoted uncertainty of 6.8 MHz. This model dependence is critical because the correct extraction of the Lamb shift relies on proper accounting of all contributing signals. "However, the n = 3 population can be electrically quenched with a weak electrical field, leaving a large fraction of the n = 2 population unharmed as shown by measurements from Bezginov et al. [41]." While mitigation strategies are proposed, no quantitative analysis is provided to demonstrate that n=3 quenching would be effective for muonium under the specific experimental conditions (different velocity distributions, different field strengths required, etc.). The reference to Bezginov's PhD thesis concerns hydrogen, not muonium, and the quenching dynamics may differ due to the different reduced masses and level spacings. The claim that this transition is "the best candidate for future precision M LS measurements" would benefit from demonstrating the feasibility of the proposed 3S suppression techniques. References: [1] N. Bezginov, T. Valdez, M. Horbatsch, A. Marsman, A. C. Vutha, and E. A. Hessels, "A measurement of the atomic hydrogen Lamb shift and the proton charge radius," [Science 365, 1007 (2019)](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.aau7807)

review_item: The paper reports the first measurement of the 2S hyperfine splitting (HFS) in muonium (559.6(72) MHz) and compares it to a "theoretical value" of 557.9 MHz with uncertainty "<0.1 MHz." However, this theoretical value is not derived from rigorous bound-state QED calculations but rather from a simple 1/n³ scaling of the measured 1S HFS, which neglects state-specific QED corrections that differ between n=1 and n=2 levels. "The value was estimated by taking the most precise 1S HFS measurement in muonium [12] and dividing it by a factor of 2³. The uncertainty was estimated by calculating the QED D₂₁ difference [35]." This footnote in Table I reveals that the theoretical 2S HFS value is derived by scaling the experimental 1S HFS measurement of 4463.302765(53) MHz ([Liu et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 82, 711 (1999)][2]) by 1/8, yielding ~557.9 MHz. While the 1/n³ scaling is the leading-order behavior for hydrogen-like atoms, it neglects state-specific QED corrections. The D₂₁ correction mentioned represents only one component of the difference between 1S and 2S hyperfine structure corrections. Modern QED calculations of hyperfine structure include terms such as higher-order binding corrections, radiative-recoil corrections, and state-dependent nuclear structure effects. For a first measurement claiming to determine "for the first time the 2S hyperfine splitting in Muonium," a comparison to a rigorous QED calculation would strengthen the scientific significance of the result. "Theoretical value HFS (*): 557.9, <0.1" The presentation of this scaled value as a "Theoretical value" in Table I alongside the rigorous theoretical Lamb shift value of 1047.498(0.001) MHz (from Janka, Ohayon, and Crivelli [23]) creates a misleading impression of equivalent theoretical rigor. The Lamb shift theoretical value cited is based on comprehensive QED calculations, while the 2S HFS "theoretical value" is an experimental value divided by eight with an uncertainty estimate from a specific correction term. The paper would benefit from either citing proper QED calculations for the 2S HFS or clearly distinguishing the nature of these two theoretical comparisons. * Quote (from [Karshenboim, Physics Reports 422, 1 (2005)][3]): "The hyperfine interval in muonium has attracted a lot of attention because of the possibility of high-precision measurements... The theoretical uncertainty is mainly due to unknown higher-order contributions." This review article on precision physics of simple atoms emphasizes that hyperfine structure calculations require careful treatment of multiple QED corrections. The scaling approach used in the paper under review, while providing a reasonable estimate, does not capture the full complexity of state-dependent QED effects that affect the 2S HFS differently from the 1S HFS. References: [2] W. Liu et al., "High precision measurements of the ground state hyperfine structure interval of muonium and of the muon magnetic moment," [Phys. Rev. Lett. 82, 711 (1999)](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.82.711) [3] S. G. Karshenboim, "Precision physics of simple atoms: QED tests, nuclear structure and fundamental constants," [Physics Reports 422, 1 (2005)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0370157305003625)

review_item: The paper reports a detected 3S/2S ratio of 0.29(7) after correcting for decay during transit, which is lower than the estimate of 0.44(4) derived from hydrogen measurements. This ~2σ discrepancy is not explained, and the possibility that the observed 3S signal might be partially due to other systematic effects rather than purely 3S population is not fully explored. "The effectively detected amount of 3S/2S created at the foil is 0.29(7). This agrees with the estimations done by C. Fry [36] of 0.36 and is slightly lower than the estimate of 0.44(4) from a combination of hydrogen population measurements [28, 29]." The paper describes the discrepancy with the hydrogen-based estimate of 0.44(4) as "slightly lower," but a comparison of 0.29(7) to 0.44(4) yields a difference of 0.15, which represents approximately 1.9 standard deviations when combining the uncertainties in quadrature. While not definitive, this tension warrants discussion. Possible explanations could include: (1) differences in charge-exchange dynamics between hydrogen and muonium formation, (2) systematic effects in the detection or fit that could mimic 3S contributions, or (3) energy-dependent formation probabilities. The agreement with Fry's estimate of 0.36 is better, but the origin of the discrepancy with the more recent hydrogen measurements is left unexplained. "An additional uncertainty in the estimated 3S/2S ratio could arise from the assumption that the detection efficiency of Lyα and Lyβ is similar. In fact, the efficiency depends on a number of factors, which make an accurate determination of its wavelength-dependency challenging." This acknowledgment that the Lyα (121.5 nm) versus Lyβ (102.5 nm) detection efficiency difference is unknown but potentially significant affects not only the 3S/2S ratio extraction but also the overall lineshape fit. Since the 3S contribution is extracted from lineshape fitting rather than direct spectroscopic identification, any systematic difference in detection efficiency between these wavelengths would directly impact the determination of the 3S population and potentially affect the extracted transition frequency. The paper would benefit from an estimate of how much the detection efficiency ratio would need to differ to explain the 3S/2S discrepancy, or from independent verification of the 3S identification.

review_item: The systematic uncertainty budget in Table I lists several contributions as upper bounds (e.g., "<0.16 MHz" for MW-Beam alignment, "<0.07 MHz" for MW field intensity) without providing the derivations or methods used to establish these bounds. For a precision measurement intended to guide future experiments, the methodology for estimating systematic uncertainties should be more thoroughly documented. "MW-Beam alignment: <0.16; MW field intensity: <0.07; M velocity distribution: <0.01; AC Stark 2P₃/₂: +0.39, <0.02; 2nd-order Doppler: +0.03, <0.01; Earth's Field: <0.05; Quantum Interference: <0.04" Table I presents seven systematic contributions as upper bounds, but the main text provides limited detail on how these bounds were determined. While the total systematic uncertainty is small compared to the 6.8 MHz statistical uncertainty (making detailed systematic analysis less urgent for this measurement), future precision measurements targeting the ~kHz level mentioned in the paper would require rigorous characterization of these effects. For example, the MW-beam alignment uncertainty of <0.16 MHz is the largest systematic, yet no description is provided of how the alignment was verified or how this bound was derived. Similarly, the quantum interference contribution of <0.04 MHz references established effects from hydrogen spectroscopy but does not detail the calculation for muonium. "The AC Stark shift due to the coupling to the 2P₃/₂ states is calculated for the average atom to be +0.39 MHz. [...] The 2nd order Doppler shift is +0.03 MHz." While the AC Stark and Doppler shifts are given as specific values, the uncertainties on these shifts are again presented only as upper bounds (<0.02 and <0.01 MHz respectively). For a comprehensive error budget, the uncertainties should ideally be derived from the uncertainties in the underlying parameters (microwave power, beam velocity distribution, etc.) rather than stated as bounds without justification. This is particularly relevant because the paper envisions future measurements at the kHz level where these systematics would become dominant.

Ni,\textsuperscript{3} Z. Salman,\textsuperscript{3} A. Suter,\textsuperscript{3} T. Prokscha,\textsuperscript{
review_item: This paper’s central frequency determination for the \(2S_{1/2},F=0 \rightarrow 2P_{1/2},F=1\) transition is vulnerable to line-pulling from a substantial \(n=3\) population, yet the manuscript does not demonstrate an experimental suppression/cross-check (e.g., quenching-on/off, altered flight time) that would bound the model-dependence, undermining **Validity** and the robustness of the extracted **Conclusions**. "Due to the reduced influence of other nearby transitions, the systematic uncertainties related to line-pulling would become negligible." This sentence motivates choosing the “isolated” \(n=2\) hyperfine transition specifically to avoid line-pulling as a limiting systematic. That framing makes control of any residual line-pulling a make-or-break requirement for the core claim that this transition is a robust LS probe. However, the rest of the manuscript shows that an additional nearby structure (from \(n=3\)) actually plays a dominant role in the fitted line shape. Because the paper’s measurement uncertainty is dominated by the fit (“Fitting 6.8 MHz”), an unbounded model bias from line-pulling would directly propagate into the headline 580.6(6.8) MHz result. In precision microwave spectroscopy, “negligible line-pulling” is typically justified by showing that plausible alternative line-shape models or controlled changes to the contaminant population do not shift the line center beyond the stated uncertainty; this is not shown here. As written, the manuscript asserts negligible line-pulling as a design principle but does not substantiate it for the as-operated configuration. "In our measurement, the comparably large population of 3S states distorts the line-shape and introduces line-pulling, which might seem to defeat the purpose of choosing the isolated \(n = 2\) transition." This sentence concedes that the core design motivation (choosing an isolated transition) is compromised by an unexpectedly important beam component. The issue is not merely an auxiliary observation: the paper relies on a fitted line shape to infer the line center, so any distortion can bias the extracted frequency. The phrase “comparably large population” indicates that \(n=3\) is not a small perturbation; it is potentially co-dominant with \(n=2\) contributions in the observable. In such situations, the standard expectation is that the authors either (i) experimentally suppress the contaminant, or (ii) rigorously propagate model uncertainty from the contaminant amplitude/shape into the final frequency uncertainty. The manuscript mainly discusses future mitigation options, but does not provide an in-run validation that current results are not significantly shifted. This leaves the primary measurement’s systematic integrity insufficiently established. Exact fit-table excerpt comparing fits without vs. with a 3S contribution: ```html x 7.8(8) 22.0(18) - -24.8(74) 6.7 ✓ 3.4(11) 25.0(19) 5.0(9) 2.3(68) 2.0 ``` These two fit variants (without vs. with a 3S contribution) imply a ~27 MHz change in the fitted frequency offset, which is several times larger than the quoted 6.8 MHz statistical fitting uncertainty for the final result. That magnitude demonstrates that the line center is highly sensitive to whether (and how) the \(3S\rightarrow 3P\) component is modeled, i.e., classic line-pulling behavior. If the 3S contribution is not independently constrained, then the fitted offset can absorb modeling mismatches rather than purely reflect the physical transition frequency. A large model sensitivity is not automatically fatal, but it must be paired with either strong external constraints (e.g., known 3S fraction, validated detection efficiency ratio, measured quench dependence) or an explicit model-uncertainty term in the frequency error budget. The manuscript’s uncertainty table does not separately account for uncertainty in the 3S contribution or its detection response. Consequently, the reported agreement with theory could be partly an artifact of the chosen model family rather than a robust experimental inference. "An additional uncertainty in the estimated 3S/2S ratio could arise from the assumption that the detection efficiency of Lyα and Lyβ is similar. In fact, the efficiency depends on a number of factors, which make an accurate determination of its wavelength-dependency challenging (see Ref. [37, 38])." This explicitly identifies a key nuisance parameter—how efficiently the detector sees 3S-derived Lyβ compared to 2S-derived Lyα—as difficult to determine accurately. That uncertainty directly affects the inferred 3S fraction in the fit (through \(B_{3S}\)) and therefore can shift the fitted line center when 3S causes line-pulling. The manuscript acknowledges the challenge but does not quantify how much the line center would move under realistic efficiency variations, nor does it include such a variation as a systematic term in Table I. Because the “with 3S” vs “without 3S” fit already produces a multi–tens-of-MHz difference, even modest uncertainty in the modeled 3S amplitude/shape could plausibly induce non-negligible shifts relative to the 6.8 MHz quoted uncertainty. In established Lamb-shift SOF-style analyses, contaminant-state effects are typically treated with dedicated suppression checks or with conservative systematic bounds when detector response is uncertain. Here, the manuscript recognizes the vulnerability but leaves it largely unbounded for the main result. "These investigations indicate that interference with a neighboring resonance, even if it is very distant, can lead to significant shifts for precision measurements." ([1](#ref1)) This literature statement captures a core reason why “small” or “distant” additional resonances cannot be dismissed in precision line-center work: they can bias extracted frequencies through interference/line-shape effects. The present manuscript reports a large \(n=3\) population and shows strong dependence of the fitted offset on whether that contribution is included, aligning with the broader warning articulated here. Even if the 3S resonance is not extremely close in frequency, its presence can still distort the apparent line shape in a sparse scan and shift the inferred center. The cited work also emphasizes that proper treatment often requires more complete modeling (e.g., density matrix including radiative pathways) and careful assessment of systematic uncertainties. Relative to that standard, the paper’s limited bounding of 3S-induced line pulling weakens confidence that the reported 580.6(6.8) MHz is a stable, model-independent result. This is especially critical because the paper’s own motivation for the isolated transition is precisely to avoid such biases.

Ni,\textsuperscript{3} Z. Salman,\textsuperscript{3} A. Suter,\textsuperscript{3} T. Prokscha,\textsuperscript{
review_item: The manuscript’s extraction of the transition frequency depends heavily on simulation-driven line shapes (including power corrections) built from a two-level optical Bloch approximation and fitted to only seven frequency points, but it does not provide the validation expected for such a model-dependent inference, weakening **Conclusions** and the transparency of **Data and methodology**. "Seven frequency points are measured in the range of 200 MHz to 800 MHz with an average power of 22.3 W." A seven-point scan is extremely sparse given that the fitted function includes multiple overlapping components (three \(n=2\) hyperfine-related contributions and a possible \(n=3\) contribution) and includes at least four fit parameters (background, scaling(s), and a global frequency offset). With sparse sampling, the inferred line center becomes more sensitive to the assumed line-shape family than to the data themselves, particularly when nuisance amplitudes are weakly constrained. In such contexts, it is standard to show robustness checks (e.g., alternative scan grids, additional points near the steepest slope, different power settings) to demonstrate that the extracted center is stable. The manuscript does not report such robustness checks for the \(580\) MHz scan. This matters because their own fit comparison shows substantial sensitivity to inclusion of the \(3S\) component, suggesting that the scan density may be insufficient to break degeneracies between offset and contaminant amplitude/shape. As a result, the reported uncertainty may understate model-driven uncertainty. "The average power inside the TLs is frequency-dependent. To be able to fit a line-shape, all frequency points are corrected to the same “global” power of 22.5 W, obtaining the corrected signal \( S_c \)." Power-dependent corrections are a known source of systematic uncertainty because the excitation probability and AC Stark-related effects depend nonlinearly on field amplitude and detuning. Here, the correction \(C(f)\) is itself derived from simulation, so any mismatch between simulated and actual field distributions or power calibration propagates into the corrected data points before fitting. The paper does not provide enough information (e.g., measured field maps, calibration residuals, or a sensitivity study of the correction procedure) to independently evaluate whether this step can bias the fitted line center. Because only seven points are collected, even a modest frequency-dependent correction error could distort the apparent asymmetry of the scan and shift the fitted offset. In precision SOF work, power-variation effects are typically constrained either by careful field modeling validated against measurements or by experimental strategies (e.g., measuring at multiple powers) that allow empirical cross-checks. The manuscript’s description does not show such validation for the key correction that precedes the fit. "Because of the linear polarization of the field in the TLs, we can approximate our system to two levels and use the relevant optical Bloch equations [32] in the simulation." The measured spectrum arises from hyperfine-structured atoms with multiple \(F, m_F\) sublevels and includes radiative decay pathways; a strict two-level reduction is therefore an approximation whose adequacy depends on the precision goal and on whether other nearby states contribute coherently or incoherently. The paper does not provide an explicit justification that this approximation is sufficient for extracting an unbiased line center in the presence of overlapping transitions and an identified \(n=3\) contaminant. While the uncertainty table includes “Quantum Interference < 0.04”, the manuscript does not describe how that number was computed within a two-level framework or how interference between different decay pathways was treated. Modern analyses of SOF Lamb-shift measurements emphasize that quantum-interference effects and multi-level dynamics can matter at the line-center level, motivating density-matrix modeling when precision is pushed. Given the demonstrated model sensitivity in the fits, the absence of a clear validation of the two-level reduction undermines confidence that the simulation-generated line shapes accurately capture the relevant physics. This is a methodological weakness that directly impacts the reliability of the extracted frequency. "Additionally, modeling the time development of the density matrix from the time at which the atom is created to the time of detection is now possible." ([1](#ref1)) This sentence reflects a common contemporary standard in high-precision microwave spectroscopy: explicitly modeling multi-level dynamics and radiative processes using density-matrix methods rather than relying on minimal two-level approximations. The cited work motivates this not as a cosmetic improvement but as a way to capture systematic effects (including quantum-mechanical interference and detailed field geometry) that can shift line centers. In contrast, the reviewed manuscript adopts a two-level approximation and does not provide a bridge showing that omitted couplings and coherences are negligible at the MHz level for the present configuration. This gap is particularly salient because the manuscript aims at an “isolated” transition precisely to reduce subtle line-shape biases, yet then confronts a multi-component spectrum that strains the two-level simplification. Without a validation step (e.g., reproducing known hydrogen line shapes or demonstrating stability under model extensions), the heavy dependence on simplified simulation limits the credibility of the final extracted frequency. The issue is methodological and affects the strength of the conclusions drawn from the fit. "Due to its smaller nuclear mass, the contributions of the higher-order recoil corrections (160 kHz level) and nucleus self-energy (40 kHz level) are enhanced for muonium compared to hydrogen..." ([4](#ref4)) This theoretical context explains why the experimental program emphasizes pushing systematic uncertainties well below the MHz scale: the physics targets are at \(\mathcal{O}(10^5)\) Hz. The reviewed manuscript frames the isolated transition as a path toward such precision, which in turn places a premium on having a line-shape model whose systematics can be validated and controlled. However, the present analysis already exhibits line-shape sensitivity at the tens-of-MHz level depending on inclusion of extra components, signaling that the modeling framework is not yet in a regime where kHz-level effects can be meaningfully targeted. This does not invalidate the “first measurement” claim, but it does weaken the manuscript’s implied methodological readiness for the precision frontier without additional validation experiments. The gap between the observed model sensitivity and the kHz-scale physics goals suggests that systematic control and model validation should be treated as a central result rather than deferred. In short, the analysis choices should be justified in light of the known scale of the physics effects of interest.

review_item: The manuscript reports extracted values for the Muonium Lamb shift and the \(2S\) hyperfine splitting, but it omits essential derivational and data-reporting details (including the per-frequency data and the combination procedure with prior measurements), limiting independent verification and reproducibility under **Data and methodology**, and weakening confidence in the **Conclusions** about the first determination of the \(2S\) HFS. "From this measurement we extract the \( 2S_{1/2}, F=0 \rightarrow 2P_{1/2}, F=1 \) resonance frequency to be 580.6(68) MHz and determine the M LS to be 1045.5(68) MHz." This reports a derived LS value but does not show, within the preprint, the explicit relation used to convert a hyperfine-resolved transition frequency into the centroid Lamb shift. In the \(I=J=1/2\) case, this conversion requires incorporating hyperfine shifts for the specific \(F\) levels and therefore depends on adopted \(2S\) and \(2P\) hyperfine splittings. The preprint does not clearly state which hyperfine splitting values (measured vs. theoretical) were used for the conversion, nor does it provide the intermediate calculation. While the numerical values are plausible, the lack of explicit derivation prevents an independent reader from checking whether the LS definition, sign conventions, and inputs match common practice. This is particularly important because the manuscript’s narrative emphasizes isolating a specific hyperfine transition to reduce systematics, so the mapping from that transition to the LS should be transparent and verifiable. Without that transparency, the LS result is not fully reproducible from the manuscript. "Using our previous results of the \( 2S_{1/2}, F=1 \rightarrow 2P_{1/2}, F=1 \) resonance frequency [20], we extract for the first time the 2S hyperfine splitting in Muonium to be 559.6(72) MHz." This claim depends on combining the present measurement with a prior measurement, but the preprint does not describe the statistical procedure used for the combination (e.g., whether a joint fit was performed, how correlations are treated, and what theoretical inputs were fixed). Because both measurements are performed by the same collaboration in related apparatus configurations, correlated systematics are plausible and can materially affect the uncertainty on a derived difference like the 2S HFS. The manuscript also does not provide the underlying data points for the earlier resonance scan, so the “first time” HFS determination cannot be independently reconstructed from the preprint alone. In precision spectroscopy, derived splittings are typically accompanied by the explicit formula used and a clear accounting of correlated uncertainties. The absence of such detail makes the reported 559.6(72) MHz difficult to independently validate and therefore weakens the strength of the conclusion. "* The value was estimated by taking the most precise 1S HFS measurement in muonium [12] and dividing it by a factor of \(2^3\). The uncertainty was estimated by calculating the QED \(D_{21}\) difference [35]." This footnote shows that the paper’s “theoretical value HFS (*)” is not a standalone ab initio prediction for the 2S HFS, but an estimate derived from experimental 1S HFS plus a theory-motivated correction. Presenting this as a “Theoretical value” in Table I can overstate how independently the measured 2S HFS is being validated against theory. For a first measurement claim, a clear separation between (i) experimentally inferred quantities and (ii) independent theoretical benchmarks is important to interpret “agreement with theory.” If the benchmark partially inherits the experimental 1S HFS input, the comparison is less stringent than it appears. This matters for conclusions about consistency with bound-state QED, especially if future work aims at kHz-level tests where such distinctions become critical. As written, the manuscript’s presentation risks giving a stronger impression of theory validation than is warranted by an estimated reference value. "To estimate the inﬂuence of the simulation input pa-rameters on the resonance position, we scan them around their uncertainty and repeat the analysis." ([2](#ref2)) This excerpt from the authors’ prior muonium LS work exemplifies a standard reproducibility and robustness practice in simulation-driven spectroscopy: explicitly varying key inputs and repeating the full analysis to quantify induced line-center shifts. In the reviewed manuscript, multiple simulation-derived elements (power correction factors, field maps, and 3S contamination modeling) can influence the fitted offset, but the preprint does not report a comparable input-sensitivity study for the current measurement. The contrast matters because the present result already shows large fit sensitivity to inclusion of extra components, making input-parameter robustness especially important. Without providing either the data or a documented sensitivity study, independent readers cannot judge whether the stated uncertainties appropriately cover simulation/model uncertainty. Thus, the methodology disclosure in the reviewed manuscript falls short of what the field commonly reports for simulation-dominated inferences. This directly limits reproducibility and confidence in the derived LS and 2S HFS. "Here, a direct measurement of the *n* = 2 Lamb shift of atomic hydrogen is presented." ([5](#ref5)) This statement highlights why “direct” Lamb-shift determinations are of broad interest: they are intended to rely on well-defined spectroscopic observables that can be independently checked and reanalyzed. For such measurements, the community expectation is that the mapping from measured transition frequencies to the reported Lamb shift (including hyperfine and other corrections) is fully specified, enabling independent reproduction from the reported inputs. The reviewed manuscript’s LS and 2S HFS values are presented without enough explicit derivational detail and without tabulation of the underlying frequency-point data beyond a single off-resonance point. As a result, the work is harder to independently verify than is typical for high-impact spectroscopy results built around “direct” determinations. This does not negate the measurement, but it weakens the reproducibility and interpretability of the derived quantities. In turn, that limits how strongly the paper can support its conclusions about first determinations and agreement with theory.

review_item: The paper reports a fitted background parameter that is statistically inconsistent with the measured background data point, or alternatively, the reported Chi-squared value is incorrect. "An additional data point is taken with the Scanner TL turned off. ... The data point with TL OFF is not displayed in the figure, but is included in the fit; it lies at 20.4(4) × 10^-4." The authors explicitly state that the background measurement (TL OFF) yields a value of 20.4(4) (in units of 10^-4) and that this point is included in the fit. "S_BKG ... 7.8(8) ... 3.4(11)" (from the table in the text) The table reporting the fit results lists the background parameter $S_{BKG}$ as 7.8(8) for the fit without 3S and 3.4(11) for the fit with 3S. These values differ from the measured background (20.4) by approximately 16 and 17 standard deviations, respectively. "Chi^2 ... 6.7 ... 2.0" (from the table in the text) The reported Chi-squared values are 6.7 and 2.0. If the "TL OFF" data point (20.4) were included in the fit with a model prediction of ~3.4 or ~7.8, the contribution to Chi-squared from this single point would be huge (approx. $[(20.4-3.4)/0.4]^2 > 1000$), which contradicts the reported low Chi-squared. This implies a severe inconsistency in the data analysis or reporting.

review_item: The claim of observing the Muonium 3S state is not supported by the statistical significance of the data. "We also present the first observation of Muonium in the n = 3 excited state." The authors claim to have observed the 3S state for the first time. However, in particle physics and precision spectroscopy, an "observation" typically requires a statistical significance of at least 5 sigma. "Chi^2 ... 6.7 ... 2.0" (from the table in the text) The improvement in Chi-squared when including the 3S component is only 4.7 (from 6.7 to 2.0). For one additional degree of freedom, this corresponds to a significance of roughly 2.1 sigma. Furthermore, the Chi-squared of 6.7 for the fit *without* the 3S component (with 5 degrees of freedom) corresponds to a p-value of approximately 0.24, indicating that the null hypothesis (no 3S state) provides an acceptable fit to the data. Therefore, the data does not statistically require the presence of the 3S state, making the claim of "observation" an overstatement.
annotator_comments: Here in general the problem is that the fit is of bad quality

Ni,\textsuperscript{3} Z. Salman,\textsuperscript{3} A. Suter,\textsuperscript{3} T. Prokscha,\textsuperscript{
review_item: The method of correcting the signal for power variations before fitting is circular and introduces unquantified systematic errors. "To be able to fit a line-shape, all frequency points are corrected to the same 'global' power of 22.5 W, obtaining the corrected signal S_c. The applied correction is given by: S_c(f) = (S(f) - S_{BKG}) \cdot C(f) + S_{BKG}" The authors correct the experimental data points using a factor $C(f)$ derived from the same simulation model used for the final fit. This approach modifies the data to match the model's expectations regarding power dependence, rather than fitting the raw data with a model that includes the power variation. "The correction factors C(f) are extracted by simulating the scaling ratio between the excitation probabilities..." This method embeds the model assumptions into the data itself. If the simulation of the power dependence is imperfect, the "corrected" data will be biased towards the model, potentially masking discrepancies. Furthermore, the uncertainty in the correction factor $C(f)$ does not appear to be propagated into the error bars of the corrected signal, which would lead to an underestimation of the final uncertainties.
