Ni,\textsuperscript{3} Z. Salman,\textsuperscript{3} A. Suter,\textsuperscript{3} T. Prokscha,\textsuperscript{
review_item: The analysis relies on a simulated line shape for the 3S background without quantifying the systematic uncertainty associated with the simulation accuracy. "The individual line-shape P^{(i)} ... is constructed by simulating the excitation probability for each initial state..." The 3S contribution is modeled using a fixed line shape derived from simulations (involving SIMION field maps and optical Bloch equations). The authors fit the *amplitude* of this contribution ($B_{3S}$) but assume the *shape* is perfectly known. "In our measurement, the comparably large population of 3S states distorts the line-shape and introduces line-pulling" Since the 3S resonance significantly overlaps with and pulls the 2S signal, any inaccuracy in the simulated 3S line shape (e.g., due to errors in the field map or atomic trajectories) would directly shift the extracted 2S frequency. The systematic uncertainty budget (Table I) lists "MW field intensity" and "MW-Beam alignment" but does not explicitly include an uncertainty for the *shape* of the 3S background model, which is a critical omission given the magnitude of the line-pulling effect.

Ni,\textsuperscript{3} Z. Salman,\textsuperscript{3} A. Suter,\textsuperscript{3} T. Prokscha,\textsuperscript{
review_item: The precision of the reported measurement is significantly lower than the collaboration's previous work, undermining the claim that this transition is the "best candidate" for future measurements without further demonstration of improved systematics. "From this measurement we extract the 2S_{1/2}, F=0 -> 2P_{1/2}, F=1 resonance frequency to be 580.6(68) MHz" The reported uncertainty is 6.8 MHz. "Our result ... is limited by the statistical uncertainty." The authors admit the measurement is statistically limited. "Combining this result with our previous measurement [20]..." The previous measurement by the same collaboration (Ref [20]) achieved an uncertainty of 2.5 MHz. The current measurement is nearly factor of 3 less precise. While measuring a new transition is valuable, the paper argues that this transition is superior ("best candidate") because it is "isolated". However, the current result does not demonstrate this advantage in practice, as it is dominated by statistics and potentially 3S line-pulling, yielding a result that is less precise than existing data.
annotator_comments: Yes this was mentioned previously as well, the paper should be rephrased a bit on this point, however the result is still valuable

review_item: My doubts are regarding the scientific contents and I will omit the „Experimental method” section. Authors have previously [20] measured 2S_1/2, F=1 -> 2P_1/2, F=1 transition frequency and determined Lamb shift. In this work, they measure 2S_1/2, F=0 -> 2P_1/2, F=1 and determine Lamb Shift with 2.5 times worse accuracy. So, from the theoretical point of view only, it is not a significant achievement. So, I would expect some explanations, what is really new here.
annotator_comments: I think the novelty is added that it is the first measurement of the 2S_1/2, F=0 -> 2P_1/2, F=1 , the fact that is of lower precision doesn’t prevent from being published.

review_item: This paper describes results following the n=2 Lamb shift spectroscopy of muonium reported by the group at Physical Review Letters this year (B. Ohayon et al., “Precision Measurement of the Lamb Shift in Muonium,” Phys. Rev. Lett., 128, 011802 (2022), hereinafter referred to as Oyahon2022.). It is possible that this paper could be interpreted as an independent confirmation of the results reported in Oyahon2022, in which case it is difficult to determine from this manuscript whether the level of novelty and significance required by the journal has been satisfied.

review_item: Another minor concern is that the uncertainty notation of the measurement result is inconsistent with that of Oyahon2022. For example, the Lamb shift is shown as 1047.2(2.3)stat(1.1)syst MHz in Oyahon2022, which is correct. However, this paper notes 1047.2(25) MHz, with the decimal point in the incorrect position. This error is also found in other quantities such as 2S-HFS, so the reviewer recommends that the authors check the manuscript again. From the reviewer’s point of view, the scientific significance of the motivation and the importance of the results stated in this paper are sufficient. So the reviewer would like to see a more detailed and substantive description enhanced.

review_item: I do not completely understand the wording about 'finite size'. In a sense a muon or an electron have finite-size effects, but they are calculable. The Lamb shift is a result of the size of an electron. The problem is the nuclear structure (of nuclei heavier than a proton) or the proton structure. They are not calculable and they include the nuclear finite size and nuclear polarizability. One should rather speak about hadronic structure, hadronic effects, or effects of strong interactions etc.

paper_title: Observation of an exotic narrow doubly charmed tetraquark
paper_excerpt: Observation of an exotic narrow doubly charmed tetraquark LHCb collaboration† Abstract Conventional hadronic matter consists of baryons and mesons made of three quarks and quark-antiquark pairs, respectively. The observation of a new type of hadronic state, a doubly charmed tetraquark containing two charm quarks, an anti-u and an anti-d quark, is reported using data collected by the LHCb experiment at the Large Hadron Collider. This exotic state with a mass of about \(3875\ \mathrm{MeV}/c^2\) manifests itself as a narrow peak in the mass spectrum of \(D^0 D^0 \pi^+\) mesons just below the \(D^{**} D^0\) mass threshold. The near-threshold mass together with a strikingly narrow width reveals the resonance nature of the state. †Authors are listed at the end of this Letter. Quantum chromodynamics (QCD), the theory of the strong force, describes interactions of coloured quarks and gluons and the formation of hadronic matter, the so-called mesons and baryons. While QCD makes precise predictions at high energies, the theory has difficulties describing the interactions of quarks in hadrons from first principles due to the highly non-perturbative regime at the corresponding energy scale. Hence, the field of hadron spectroscopy is driven by experimental discoveries that sometimes are unexpected, which could lead to changes in the research landscape. Along with conventional mesons and baryons, made of a quark-antiquark pair (\( q_1 \overline{q}_2 \)) and three quarks (\( q_1 q_2 q_3 \)), respectively, particles with an alternative quark content, known as exotic states, have been activ
review_item: The paper uses a relativistic P-wave Breit-Wigner function to model the signal, which is acknowledged as a simplification. For a resonance located extremely close to the D\*+D0 threshold (δmBW = −273 ± 61 keV/c²), standard Breit-Wigner parametrizations are known to be inadequate as they do not properly account for threshold effects, coupled-channel dynamics, and the energy-dependent width behavior that is critical for near-threshold states. "Despite its simplicity, the model serves well to quantify the existence of the Tcc+ state and to measure its properties, such as the position and the width of the resonance." The authors themselves acknowledge the simplified nature of their signal model. While they argue it is sufficient for establishing the observation, the extracted parameters (mass and width) may not represent the true physical pole parameters of the resonance. For near-threshold states, the difference between Breit-Wigner parameters and pole parameters can be substantial, affecting the physical interpretation of the state's properties. "A follow-up study [90] investigates the underlying nature of the Tcc+ state, expanding on the modelling of the signal shape and determining its physical properties." The paper explicitly defers to a follow-up study for proper characterization of the signal. This indicates that the authors recognize the limitations of their approach. The follow-up study (published in Nature Communications, Ref. [2]) indeed uses a unitarized Breit-Wigner formalism that properly accounts for threshold effects and coupled-channel dynamics, yielding notably different parameter values (δmU = −359 ± 40 keV/c² vs. δmBW = −273 ± 61 keV/c²). "Because the Breit-Wigner amplitude with a constant decay width does not include the effect of the threshold, the Breit-Wigner amplitude cannot be applied to" near-threshold states according to general amplitude analysis principles [[1]](#citation-list). This statement from the literature confirms that standard Breit-Wigner parametrizations are fundamentally inadequate for near-threshold resonances. The Flatté amplitude or more sophisticated unitarized approaches are required to properly capture the threshold physics, which the authors acknowledge by deferring detailed analysis to a follow-up publication. References: [1] K. Sone, "General amplitude of near-threshold hadron scattering for exotic hadrons," Physical Review D (2025). [https://scoap3-prod-backend.s3.cern.ch/media/harvested_files/10.1103/qp1j-slqg/qp1j-slqg.pdf](https://scoap3-prod-backend.s3.cern.ch/media/harvested_files/10.1103/qp1j-slqg/qp1j-slqg.pdf) [2] M. Karliner and J. L. Rosner, "The doubly charmed strange tetraquark," arXiv:2110.12054 (2022). [https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.12054](https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.12054)

This exotic state with a mass of about \(3875\ \mathrm{MeV}/c^2\) manifests itself as a narrow peak in the mass spectrum of \(D^0 D^0 \pi^+\) mesons just below the \(D^{**} D^0\) mass threshold. The near-threshold mass together with a strikingly narrow width reveals the resonance nature of the state. †Authors are listed at the end of this Letter. Hence, the field of hadron spectroscopy is driven by experimental discoveries that sometimes are unexpected, which could lead to changes in the research landscape. Along with conventional mesons and baryons, made of a quark-antiquark pair (\( q_1 \overline{q}_2 \)) and three quarks (\( q_1 q_2 q_3 \)), respectively, particles with an alternative quark content, known as exotic states, have been activ
review_item: The paper assumes JP = 1+ quantum numbers for the Tcc+ state based on theoretical expectations, but these quantum numbers are not experimentally determined from the data. This assumption directly affects the signal model (P-wave Breit-Wigner function) and the interpretation of the measured parameters, yet the paper does not present an angular analysis or other direct measurement of the spin-parity. "The use of a P–wave resonance is motivated by the expected JP = 1+ quantum numbers for the Tcc+ state." This statement shows that the choice of a P-wave resonance is based on theoretical expectation rather than experimental determination. The spin-parity quantum numbers are crucial for understanding the nature of the state (compact tetraquark vs. hadronic molecule vs. other configurations), yet no model-independent measurement of these properties is provided. "The model uncertainty related to the assumption of JP = 1+ quantum numbers of the state is estimated and listed separately." While the authors appropriately treat the JP assumption as a source of systematic uncertainty that is listed separately, this does not replace a direct measurement. The systematic uncertainties listed for alternative S-wave and D-wave assumptions (δmBW: +11/-14 keV/c², ΓBW: +18/-38 keV) indicate non-negligible sensitivity to this assumption. * Quote from Table 2: "JP quantum numbers: +11/-14 (keV/c²) for δmBW, +18/-38 (keV) for ΓBW" These systematic uncertainties associated with alternative JP assignments represent a significant fraction of the total systematic uncertainty, highlighting that the physical parameters extracted depend notably on the assumed spin-parity quantum numbers. A more complete analysis would include an angular analysis to constrain or determine these quantum numbers directly from data.

Along with conventional mesons and baryons, made of a quark-antiquark pair (\( q_1 \overline{q}_2 \)) and three quarks (\( q_1 q_2 q_3 \)), respectively, particles with an alternative quark content, known as exotic states, have been activ
review_item: While the paper claims to observe a state "compatible with being a Tcc+ tetraquark state," the analysis does not provide sufficient evidence to distinguish between a compact tetraquark configuration and a loosely bound D\*D hadronic molecular state. The proximity of the observed mass to the D\*+D0 threshold (δm ≈ −273 keV/c²) is equally consistent with either interpretation, yet this important theoretical question is not adequately addressed. "In this Letter, the observation of a narrow state in the D0D0π+ mass spectrum near the D\*+D0 mass threshold compatible with being a Tcc+ tetraquark state is reported." The identification as a "tetraquark" is presented cautiously but without discussion of alternative interpretations. Given that "Due to the closeness of their masses to known particle-pair thresholds, many of these states are likely to be hadronic molecules" as stated in the introduction, the assignment requires more careful consideration. The extreme proximity to threshold (< 1 MeV below) is actually more characteristic of molecular states in many theoretical frameworks. "The near-threshold mass together with a strikingly narrow width reveals the resonance nature of the state." While this statement establishes that a resonance is observed, it does not address whether the state is a compact tetraquark or a molecular configuration. The theoretical literature cited in the paper (Refs. [41-46]) discusses both possibilities for QQq̄q̄ systems, and distinguishing between them requires additional observables such as production cross-sections, decay patterns, or size-related measurements. * Quote from Ref. [[2]](#citation-list): "The proximity of this maximum to the intersection of the two M(D\*+) dashed straight lines is a cautionary signal of a possible kinematic enhancement." The follow-up theoretical work by Karliner and Rosner explicitly notes that the observed signal's proximity to the kinematic crossing point raises questions about its interpretation, suggesting the need for caution in assigning a purely resonant tetraquark interpretation without additional evidence. References: [2] M. Karliner and J. L. Rosner, "The doubly charmed strange tetraquark," arXiv:2110.12054 (2022). [https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.12054](https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.12054)

Hence, the field of hadron spectroscopy is driven by experimental discoveries that sometimes are unexpected, which could lead to changes in the research landscape. Along with conventional mesons and baryons, made of a quark-antiquark pair (\( q_1 \overline{q}_2 \)) and three quarks (\( q_1 q_2 q_3 \)), respectively, particles with an alternative quark content, known as exotic states, have been activ
review_item: This paper’s reported discovery-level statistical significance for the T\(_{cc}^+\) peak is not demonstrably valid because it is derived using Wilks’ theorem on an analysis that (i) uses sPlot subtraction with negative weights and (ii) tests a signal-yield parameter that is effectively on the boundary, both of which violate regularity assumptions needed to trust asymptotic \(\chi^2\)-based likelihood-ratio calibrations. “The statistical significance of the observed T\(_{cc}^+ \to\) D\(^0\)D\(^0\pi^+\) signal is estimated using Wilks’ theorem [94] to be 22 standard deviations.” This sentence anchors the central discovery claim on an asymptotic result (Wilks) that requires regularity conditions (e.g., parameters not on boundaries, standard likelihood construction, and well-behaved nuisance structure). In this analysis, the signal yield is intrinsically non-negative in the physical model, so the null hypothesis “no signal” places the yield at the boundary; this is a known setting where the naive Wilks \(\chi^2\) reference distribution does not hold. The problem is not merely pedantic: boundary behavior changes the null distribution of test statistics and therefore the mapping from \(-2\Delta\ln\mathcal{L}\) to “\(\sigma\)” units. Additionally, the paper’s pipeline uses a statistically-subtracted (weighted) dataset, which typically implies that the objective function is not a conventional (unweighted) log-likelihood for i.i.d. events. Without an explicit demonstration (e.g., toy-MC calibration under the full procedure), the quoted “22 standard deviations” cannot be independently trusted as a frequentist false-positive rate. Even if the true significance remains very large, the paper’s *method* for claiming it is not justified on the stated assumptions. “Each candidate is assigned a positive weight for being signal-like or a negative weight for being background-like, with the masses of the two \( D^0 \) candidates as the discriminating variables. All candidates are then retained and the weights are used in the further analysis for statistical subtraction of non-D\(^0\) background.” This describes an analysis stage that produces negative event weights and then uses them downstream, which is not the standard setting under which Wilks’ theorem is usually invoked. A conventional extended unbinned likelihood assumes contributions from events are log-probabilities of an actual sampling distribution (and expected counts are non-negative); negative weights typically correspond to an estimator for a background-subtracted distribution, not a generative likelihood. If the final fit and likelihood-ratio test treat the weighted sample “as if” it were an ordinary unweighted dataset, the resulting likelihood-ratio statistic may not have the nominal asymptotic distribution. Moreover, the variance introduced by the subtraction itself (and any correlations it induces) can distort test-statistic behavior relative to a pure maximum-likelihood fit on unsubtracted data with an explicit background component. The manuscript does not provide a calibration of the likelihood-ratio statistic under this weighted procedure. In high-stakes “observation” claims, the standard practice is to validate the test statistic’s distribution under the null with pseudoexperiments when regularity is questionable. “For the special case of \(\mu′ = 0\), this reduces to \( f(q_0|0) = \tfrac{1}{2}\delta(q_0) + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{q_0}} e^{-q_0/2}.\) That is, one finds a mixture of a delta function at zero and a chi-square distribution for one degree of freedom, with each term having a weight of 1/2.” [[1]](#ref1) This statement makes explicit that, for the common discovery test in particle physics where the signal strength is constrained to be non-negative, the null distribution is *not* the standard Wilks \(\chi^2_1\) but a half-\(\chi^2\) mixture. The paper under review reports a Wilks-based “22 standard deviations” without clarifying whether such boundary corrections were applied or whether the parameter was allowed to go negative for calibration purposes. While the numerical difference at extremely high significance may be small, the paper’s claim is nevertheless not methodologically supported as written. The distinction matters because the mapping from \(q_0\) to \(Z\) is only straightforward (\(Z=\sqrt{q_0}\)) under the correct mixture distribution. More importantly, the paper’s likelihood is further complicated by background subtraction weights, so even this corrected asymptotic form may fail. Best practice in such nonstandard conditions is to validate via pseudoexperiments that include the full weighting/subtraction pipeline. “In these and other settings that involve testing a hypothesis that is on the boundary of the parameter space, contrary to common practice, the nominal \(\chi^2\) distribution for the LRT or the F distribution for the F test should not be used.” [[4]](#ref4) This directly contradicts the paper’s unqualified reliance on Wilks’ theorem for a signal-existence test where the null is naturally at the boundary (signal yield zero). The cited discussion emphasizes that boundary problems persist “even asymptotically” and can yield unpredictable false-positive rates unless the reference distribution is properly calibrated. In the present paper, the discovery claim is the main scientific outcome, so the calibration of its significance is central to validity. The authors could have mitigated the concern by (i) describing the exact test statistic, (ii) stating the boundary handling, and (iii) showing toy-MC calibration under the background-only hypothesis that includes sPlot subtraction. Because none of this is provided in the main paper, the numerical “22 standard deviations” is not reproducibly interpretable as a discovery-level p-value. This is a major methodological gap given field norms for claims of observation. “Strictly speaking, Wilks’ theorem does not apply here since the tested value of \(\mu\) is on the boundary of the allowed region. This difficulty however can be easily alleviated by extending the allowed region of \(\mu\) to negative values and then reducing the p-value by half.” [[2]](#ref2) This passage summarizes a standard fix used in HEP when applying likelihood-ratio tests to a non-negative signal-strength parameter. The paper under review does not state whether it applied this fix (or any equivalent correction) when quoting a Wilks-based 22\(\sigma\) significance. That omission matters because the paper presents the result as a calibrated “standard deviations” statement rather than as an internal test statistic value. The quoted fix also highlights that boundary issues are expected in exactly the type of resonance searches performed here. Given the additional complication of negative sPlot weights, simply appealing to Wilks without an explicit correction and validation is not sufficient. References: [1] [G. Cowan, K. Cranmer, E. Gross, O. Vitells, “Asymptotic formulae for likelihood-based tests of new physics” (arXiv:1007.1727)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1007.1727.pdf) [2] [E. Gross, O. Vitells, “Trial factors for the look elsewhere effect in high energy physics” (arXiv:1005.1891)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1005.1891.pdf) [4] [R. Protassov et al., “Statistics: Handle with Care: Detecting Multiple Model Components with the Likelihood Ratio Test” (arXiv:astro-ph/0201547)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/astro-ph/0201547.pdf)

The near-threshold mass together with a strikingly narrow width reveals the resonance nature of the state. †Authors are listed at the end of this Letter. Quantum chromodynamics (QCD), the theory of the strong force, describes interactions of coloured quarks and gluons and the formation of hadronic matter, the so-called mesons and baryons. While QCD makes precise predictions at high energies, the theory has difficulties describing the interactions of quarks in hadrons from first principles due to the highly non-perturbative regime at the corresponding energy scale. Hence, the field of hadron spectroscopy is driven by experimental discoveries that sometimes are unexpected, which could lead to changes in the research landscape. Along with conventional mesons and baryons, made of a quark-antiquark pair (\( q_1 \overline{q}_2 \)) and three quarks (\( q_1 q_2 q_3 \)), respectively, particles with an alternative quark content, known as exotic states, have been activ
review_item: This paper’s background-subtracted \(D^0D^0\pi^+\) mass spectrum and all downstream measurements are potentially biased because the sPlot procedure is used without demonstrating that the control variable (\(m(D^0D^0\pi^+)\) or its constrained variant) is uncorrelated with the discriminating variables (the two \(D^0\) candidate masses), which is a required condition for sPlot to yield unbiased unfolded distributions. “The procedure employs the sPlot technique [110]… Each candidate is assigned a positive weight for being signal-like or a negative weight for being background-like, with the masses of the two \( D^0 \) candidates as the discriminating variables. All candidates are then retained and the weights are used in the further analysis for statistical subtraction of non-D\(^0\) background.” This specifies that the paper relies on an sPlot-weighted dataset (with negative weights) to produce the spectrum that is later fit for the T\(_{cc}^+\) signal. In sPlot, unbiased reconstruction of a control-variable distribution requires that the control variable be uncorrelated with the discriminating variables for each component (signal and background). Here, the discriminating variables are explicitly the two reconstructed \(D^0\) masses; the control variable is effectively the reconstructed \(D^0D^0\pi^+\) mass (after constraints and kinematic fitting). Because the \(D^0\) masses enter the kinematics and the event selection (mass windows and mass constraints), correlations are plausible and must be checked rather than assumed. The manuscript does not present any quantitative validation (e.g., correlation studies, closure tests, or simulation-based bias checks) that the sPlot independence condition holds in this specific reconstruction. If the condition fails, the “statistically subtracted” spectrum can be distorted in a mass-dependent way, directly impacting both the peak shape and fitted background under the signal. Given that the signal width is at the few-hundred-keV level, even small distortions could be consequential. “To improve the \(m\) mass resolution… the mass of the D\(^0\)D\(^0\pi^+\) combinations is calculated with the mass of each D\(^0\) meson constrained to the known value [35].” This sentence makes the dependence between the discriminating variables (the reconstructed \(D^0\) masses) and the control variable (the constrained \(D^0D^0\pi^+\) mass) especially delicate. A mass constraint is a non-linear transformation that uses measured track momenta and reconstructed masses to adjust the kinematics, and such constraints can induce or amplify correlations between the unconstrained \(D^0\) mass residuals and the final constrained composite mass. Because the sPlot weights are derived from the unconstrained \(D^0\) masses, any residual correlation with the constrained \(D^0D^0\pi^+\) mass can bias the weighted spectrum. In practice, collaborations often demonstrate that such correlations are negligible via simulation closure tests or by validating that the sWeighted control-variable distribution matches expectations when applying the method to known resonances. No such demonstration appears in the main text, leaving a key methodological assumption implicit. This is not a cosmetic omission: it is a prerequisite for treating the shown “statistically subtracted” spectrum as an unbiased estimator. Without it, reproducibility of the peak parameters is uncertain. “An essential assumption for the sPlot technique to apply is that the control variable is uncorrelated with the discriminating variable.” [[3]](#ref3) This is the core condition under which sPlot delivers unbiased unfolded distributions for variables not used in the fit. The paper under review does not state or test this assumption for its particular choice of discriminating variables (two \(D^0\) masses) and control variable (the \(D^0D^0\pi^+\) mass used to observe the peak). Because the selection explicitly imposes \(D^0\) mass windows and uses a kinematic fit, the potential for correlation is not hypothetical. In modern particle-physics analyses, it is standard to include validation plots/correlation coefficients, or to propagate a systematic uncertainty derived from closure tests if correlations are non-negligible. Absent such checks, the sPlot-based background subtraction is not fully justified as an unbiased preprocessing step. That undermines confidence in the correctness of the reported peak shape and thus the extracted mass/width parameters. “If the control variable x exhibits significant correlation with the discriminating variable y, the sPlots obtained with Eq. (15) cannot be compared directly with the pure distributions of the various species. In that case, one must proceed to a Monte-Carlo simulation of the procedure to obtain the expected distributions to which the sPlots should be compared with.” [[3]](#ref3) This provides a concrete remedy recommended by the sPlot authors: when correlations exist, a Monte Carlo study is required to obtain the correct expectation for comparison and to assess bias. The manuscript does not report such a correlation-aware MC study for the variable of central interest (the near-threshold \(D^0D^0\pi^+\) mass spectrum). Importantly, the paper’s later fit assumes that the subtracted spectrum can be interpreted as if it were the “true” prompt \(D^0D^0\pi^+\) distribution. If the control/discriminant correlation is nonzero, then the fitted background under the signal and possibly the signal yield itself can be biased. This concern is amplified by the limited signal statistics (\(117 \pm 16\) events) and the extremely narrow structure, where small systematic distortions can matter. Therefore, the lack of a documented correlation check is a significant reproducibility and validity problem. References: [3] [M. Pivk, F. R. Le Diberder, “sPlot: a statistical tool to unfold data distributions” (arXiv:physics/0402083)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/physics/0402083.pdf)

review_item: This paper’s conclusion that the observed structure’s near-threshold mass and narrow width “reveal” a resonance is not robust because the signal is modeled with a simplified two-body P-wave Breit–Wigner ansatz (with ad hoc decay structure) even though the extracted width is comparable to detector resolution and the system is explicitly near a hadronic threshold where Breit–Wigner parameters can become non-physical or strongly model-dependent. “The near-threshold mass together with a strikingly narrow width reveals the resonance nature of the state.” This is a strong interpretive statement in the abstract: it asserts that the measured mass and width *establish* a resonance interpretation. For near-threshold structures, however, the observed peak shape can be distorted by threshold effects, coupled-channel dynamics, and kinematic cusps, and thus “narrowness” in a reconstructed spectrum does not by itself distinguish resonance poles from non-resonant enhancements. The paper’s own modeling choices (see below) acknowledge simplicity and defer deeper dynamical interpretation to a follow-up study, which is in tension with this categorical abstract-level conclusion. Moreover, the reported width is of the same order as the experimental resolution, meaning the inferred “narrow width” is obtained by deconvolution and is sensitive to the assumed resolution model and lineshape form. A resonance claim should, at minimum, be supported by demonstrating stability of the extracted parameters under physically motivated near-threshold amplitudes. Without that, the abstract overstates what the present analysis establishes. “The signal component is described by the convolution of the detector resolution with a resonant shape, which is modelled by a relativistic P-wave two-body Breit–Wigner function… A two-body decay structure T\(_{cc}^+ \to\) AB is assumed with \(m_A = 2m_{D^0}\) and \(m_B = m_{\pi^+}\)… Despite its simplicity, the model serves well to quantify the existence of the T\(_{cc}^+\) state and to measure its properties…” This explicitly acknowledges that the signal lineshape is treated with a simplified two-body Breit–Wigner model and an artificial “AB” decay structure that is not the physically dominant open-charm channel (which is discussed elsewhere in the paper as \(D^{*+}D^0\)-related near threshold). Near-threshold hadronic systems often require coupled-channel or Flatté/effective-range-like parameterizations to connect peak shapes to pole properties, especially when one channel is opening/closing in the fit region. While a simplified model can be acceptable to *demonstrate an excess*, the paper goes beyond existence and uses the Breit–Wigner parameters to support “resonance nature” language. The quoted sentence concedes the model’s simplicity but does not quantify how that simplicity impacts the physical interpretation of the width and binding relative to threshold. In particular, the ad hoc mapping of a three-body final state to a two-body P-wave Breit–Wigner can bias the extracted \(\delta m_{\mathrm{BW}}\) and \(\Gamma_{\mathrm{BW}}\) in ways not captured by small quoted systematics. This is a major issue because the paper’s headline claims hinge on the near-threshold position and extreme narrowness. “The root mean square of the resolution function is around 400 keV/\(c^2\).” The quoted resolution scale is essentially the same order as the fitted Breit–Wigner width (hundreds of keV). In this regime, the inferred natural width is a deconvolution result and is highly sensitive to the resolution model and any mismodeling of its tails. Even small mismodeling can move substantial probability mass between “resolution” and “intrinsic width,” directly impacting claims like “strikingly narrow width.” The paper reports small systematic uncertainties from resolution-model variations, but those variations are within a family of parametric shapes rather than derived from a fully data-driven calibration specific to this final state. Additionally, near-threshold dynamics can change the functional form of the intrinsic lineshape, making the concept of a single Breit–Wigner width parameter fragile. Given this sensitivity, a robust resonance interpretation typically requires showing that a physically motivated near-threshold amplitude yields a consistent pole or that the data exclude non-resonant explanations. The present paper does not provide that evidence. “Previous measurements have assumed a Breit–Wigner resonance shape. However… this is not well motivated due to the proximity of the D\(^0\)D\(^*0\) threshold.” [[5]](#ref5) This statement (from an LHCb paper on another near-threshold exotic candidate) reflects a broadly accepted methodological point: Breit–Wigner modeling can be poorly motivated near thresholds. The T\(_{cc}^+\) signal here is explicitly “near the D\(^{*+}\)D\(^0\) mass threshold,” so the same caveat applies in principle. The comparison is particularly relevant because it shows LHCb itself has treated near-threshold exotic lineshapes with Flatté-inspired and analytic-structure analyses when interpreting resonance properties. In the current paper, the authors instead adopt an intentionally simplified Breit–Wigner for parameter extraction while simultaneously making strong resonance-nature statements. This mismatch between the modeling rigor typically used for threshold-adjacent states and the strength of conclusions drawn here weakens the paper’s interpretability. At minimum, the manuscript should delimit which claims are purely phenomenological (peak existence) versus which are physical (resonance pole). “The result highlights the importance of a proper lineshape parameterisation for a measurement of the location of the pole.” [[5]](#ref5) This is directly relevant to the present work because the paper interprets the measured near-threshold peak location and narrowness as evidence of a genuine resonance. Yet the Breit–Wigner parameters reported here (\(\delta m_{\mathrm{BW}}\), \(\Gamma_{\mathrm{BW}}\)) are not pole parameters, and near-threshold distortions can make their mapping to pole properties unreliable. The cited sentence emphasizes that a physically correct parameterization is crucial specifically for pole-related statements, which the current paper implicitly gestures toward by invoking “resonance nature” and citing scattering length/effective range in the summary. Although a follow-up study is referenced, the present paper’s core narrative still leans on Breit–Wigner width and sub-threshold mass to make a qualitative physical claim. This creates a risk that readers will overinterpret the Breit–Wigner fit parameters as direct resonance properties, contrary to best practices established in the literature. Therefore, the paper’s conclusions are not as robust as claimed under the presented modeling. References: [5] [LHCb collaboration, “Study of the lineshape of the χc1(3872) state” (arXiv:2005.13419)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2005.13419.pdf)

The near-threshold mass together with a strikingly narrow width reveals the resonance nature of the state. †Authors are listed at the end of this Letter. Quantum chromodynamics (QCD), the theory of the strong force, describes interactions of coloured quarks and gluons and the formation of hadronic matter, the so-called mesons and baryons. While QCD makes precise predictions at high energies, the theory has difficulties describing the interactions of quarks in hadrons from first principles due to the highly non-perturbative regime at the corresponding energy scale. Hence, the field of hadron spectroscopy is driven by experimental discoveries that sometimes are unexpected, which could lead to changes in the research landscape. Along with conventional mesons and baryons, made of a quark-antiquark pair (\( q_1 \overline{q}_2 \)) and three quarks (\( q_1 q_2 q_3 \)), respectively, particles with an alternative quark content, known as exotic states, have been activ
review_item: This paper’s extracted mass and width depend on an assumed \(J^P\) assignment (implemented via orbital-angular-momentum choices in the Breit–Wigner model), but the paper does not measure \(J^P\) in this analysis and treats the resulting model dependence as partially “separate” rather than fully integrated, weakening the robustness of the reported physical interpretation. “The use of a P–wave resonance is motivated by the expected J\(^P = 1^+\) quantum numbers for the T\(_{cc}^+\) state.” This sentence makes clear that the core signal model choice (P-wave Breit–Wigner) is not inferred from the data in the paper but motivated by external theoretical expectations. In spectroscopy, the orbital angular momentum strongly affects the near-threshold lineshape through momentum-dependent widths and barrier factors; thus, the extracted \(\delta m_{\mathrm{BW}}\) and \(\Gamma_{\mathrm{BW}}\) can be model-dependent under different \(L\) assignments. Because the paper does not present an angular analysis or other direct \(J^P\) discriminator, the chosen P-wave assumption is not empirically validated here. The paper later reports a third uncertainty “related to the J\(^P\) quantum numbers assignment,” but that does not substitute for measuring \(J^P\) or demonstrating that alternative physically plausible assignments are excluded by the data. When the main result is an ultra-narrow near-threshold structure, model dependence in the centrifugal-barrier behavior can materially affect inferred binding and width. Therefore, the reliance on an assumed \(J^P\) weakens the robustness of the extracted parameters and downstream interpretation. “Model parameters of the Breit–Wigner function: alternative parametrisations include… the orbital angular momentum between A and B particles, corresponding to S- and D-waves… The parameters of interest are more sensitive to the choice of orbital angular momentum… As the S-wave and D-wave imply that the quantum numbers of the T\(^{cc}\) state differ from J\(^\mathrm{P} = 1^+\), the corresponding systematic uncertainty is considered separately and is not included it in the total systematic uncertainty.” This paragraph explicitly states that varying the orbital angular momentum (which changes the effective \(J^P\) modeling) moves the mass and width parameters and that this dependence is handled “separately.” That separation can be reasonable for bookkeeping, but it also reveals that the reported (mass, width) are conditional on a specific \(J^P\) assumption rather than being a fully model-averaged measurement. More importantly, the logic “S/D waves imply different \(J^P\), therefore treat separately” does not eliminate the fact that the data in this paper do not determine \(J^P\), so the extracted parameters necessarily inherit this ambiguity. In standard practice, if quantum numbers are not measured, one either restricts conclusions to the observation of a structure or performs alternative fits and propagates the full spread into the quoted resonance-parameter uncertainties, especially when the physics interpretation (bound vs unbound, pole vs cusp) depends on the near-threshold behavior. Here, the mass-below-threshold claim and the narrow-width narrative are sensitive to these modeling choices, so handling them as an auxiliary uncertainty while still concluding consistency with \(J^P=1^+\) is weaker than typical spectroscopy standards. This directly affects the reliability of the physical interpretation presented in the conclusion. “The measured mass and width are consistent with the expected values for a T\(_{cc}^+\) isoscalar tetraquark ground state with quantum numbers J\(^P = 1^+\).” This conclusion sentence leans on consistency with a specific \(J^P\) scenario even though the analysis does not measure those quantum numbers and acknowledges that alternative angular-momentum assumptions shift the fitted parameters. In a spectroscopy context, “consistent with” is a weak statement, but in this paper it appears in the same concluding paragraph that asserts “genuine resonance nature,” encouraging a stronger interpretive reading. Without a demonstrated \(J^P\) determination (or at least a fit-quality comparison showing that alternative \(L\) assignments are disfavored by the data), the evidence presented here does not robustly support the \(J^P=1^+\) assignment. Moreover, treating quantum-number dependence as a separate uncertainty does not provide readers a clear sense of how much the core claims (sub-threshold position, extreme narrowness) would move under different assignments. As a result, the interpretation is not as well supported by the reported analysis as the conclusion implies.
annotator_comments: It’s true that the assumption is made, however these inputs are not subject to a measurement in this paper.

While QCD makes precise predictions at high energies, the theory has difficulties describing the interactions of quarks in hadrons from first principles due to the highly non-perturbative regime at the corresponding energy scale. Hence, the field of hadron spectroscopy is driven by experimental discoveries that sometimes are unexpected, which could lead to changes in the research landscape. Along with conventional mesons and baryons, made of a quark-antiquark pair (\( q_1 \overline{q}_2 \)) and three quarks (\( q_1 q_2 q_3 \)), respectively, particles with an alternative quark content, known as exotic states, have been activ
review_item: The paper uses a simple relativistic P-wave Breit-Wigner function to model the $T_{cc}^+$ signal, which is insufficient for a state located extremely close to the $D^{*+} D^0$ threshold. This leads to a significant overestimation of the decay width and a bias in the mass determination. "The signal component is described by the convolution of the detector resolution with a resonant shape, which is modelled by a relativistic P-wave two-body Breit–Wigner function modified by a Blatt–Weisskopf form factor [88,89] with a meson-radius parameter of 3.5 GeV\(^{-1}\). ... Despite its simplicity, the model serves well to quantify the existence of the T\(_{cc}^+\) state and to measure its properties, such as the position and the width of the resonance." The authors acknowledge the simplicity of the model but claim it serves well to measure properties. However, for a state with $\delta m \approx -273$ keV relative to the threshold, the line shape is heavily distorted by the threshold opening and cannot be accurately described by a simple Breit-Wigner. This simplification leads to physically incorrect parameters. "$\Gamma_{BW}$ ... 410 ± 165 keV" (from Table 1) The reported width of 410 keV is likely an artifact of the resolution and the inadequate model. A subsequent detailed study by the same collaboration [1] using a unitarized model found a much smaller width ($\approx 48$ keV), indicating that the value reported in this observation paper is overestimated by an order of magnitude. "While the standard Breit-Wigner parametrization generates a Tcc+ that is too broad for current theoretical calculations to reproduce, a three-body unitarized Breit-Wigner shape reveals instead a decay width..." [2] Theoretical analysis confirms that the standard Breit-Wigner parametrization yields a width that is too broad and inconsistent with more rigorous unitarized approaches, which are necessary for near-threshold states. References: [1] LHCb collaboration, "Study of the doubly charmed tetraquark Tcc+", Nature Communications 13, 3351 (2022). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-30206-w [2] M. Albaladejo, "Subleading contributions to the decay width of the Tcc+ tetraquark", Phys. Rev. D 105, 014007 (2022). https://journals.aps.org/prd/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevD.105.014007

review_item: The analysis assumes a two-body decay structure $T_{cc}^+ \to (2D^0) + \pi^+$ with a threshold at $2m_{D^0} + m_{\pi^+}$, which ignores the dominant intermediate state $D^{*+} D^0$ and its associated threshold behavior in the immediate vicinity of the signal. "A two-body decay structure T\(_{cc}^+ \to\) AB is assumed with \(m_A = 2m_{D^0}\) and \(m_B = m_{\pi^+}\), where \(m_{\pi^+}\) stands for the known mass of the \(\pi^+\) meson." This assumption implies that the phase space opens at $2m_{D^0} + m_{\pi^+}$, which is approximately 6 MeV below the $D^{*+} D^0$ threshold. Since the signal is located at the $D^{*+} D^0$ threshold ($\delta m \approx -273$ keV), the relevant physical threshold is $D^{*+} D^0$. By using the lower 3-body threshold, the model fails to capture the rapid variation of the phase space near the signal peak, leading to distorted line shapes. "The near-threshold mass together with a strikingly narrow width reveals the resonance nature of the state." The "resonance nature" and its parameters depend critically on the threshold behavior. Ignoring the $D^{*+}$ width and the correct threshold location (treating it as a simple 2-body decay to $2D^0 \pi$) misrepresents the underlying physics of the decay, which proceeds via $T_{cc}^+ \to D^{*+} D^0 \to D^0 D^0 \pi^+$.

review_item: The systematic uncertainty assigned to the fit model is grossly underestimated, as it does not account for the large discrepancy between the simple Breit-Wigner model and physically motivated unitarized models. "Systematic uncertainties for the \( \delta m_{\mathrm{BW}} \) and \( \Gamma_{\mathrm{BW}} \) parameters are summarised in Table 2... The largest systematic uncertainty is related to the fit model... Total [Fit model] ... 43 [keV] [for width]" The total systematic uncertainty for the width is quoted as 43 keV. However, the difference between the width obtained from the simple Breit-Wigner (410 keV) and the more accurate unitarized model (~48 keV [1]) is over 300 keV. The assigned systematic uncertainty fails to cover this model dependence, leading to a misleading precision claim. "Several variations in the fit model are considered: changes in the signal model due to the imperfect knowledge of the detector resolution... parametrisation of the background component..." The variations considered seem to be minor perturbations of the simple model rather than fundamentally different (and more correct) physical models. This limited exploration of the model space results in a significant underestimation of the true systematic uncertainty associated with the line-shape parametrization. References: [1] LHCb collaboration, "Study of the doubly charmed tetraquark Tcc+", Nature Communications 13, 3351 (2022). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-30206-w

review_item: [Overall significance] This manuscript describes an observation of a new type hadron containing four quarks, namely cc\bar{u}\bar{d}, which is beyond the conventional quark model telling us only baryons and mesons. The results are original and has great impact to the particle physics field usually studying sub-atom particles. It helps complete the exotic hadron picture, which is popular and of interest in a wide community over the last two decades. Within my knowledge, I think the authors credited previous work properly, except for the tetraquark candidate observed by Belle in 2013 (Phys. Rev. Lett. 110, 252002), which should be added in the reference as well.

review_item: [Strength of the claims] P2, 3rd para, L6: In the fit to the signal, the author use a P-wave resonance, and treat other options as systematic. However, in my opinion, the D^0pi^+ system is very likely to be a virtue D*+ (which is a vector), as indicated also by D^0pi^+ events in the higher mass region. Thus, it seems S-wave resonance is the most natural parameterization method, still within the J^P=1^+ assumption.
annotator_comments: In the mass fits in the nominal model there has to be some assumption. Given that the S wave was taken as a systematic uncertainty in the final result this is also taken into account.

review_item: [Strength of the claims] P2, 3rd para, L15-16: I am not sure root mean square is equivalent to standard deviation here. Usually we model detector resolution with a Gaussian.
annotator_comments: It is said in the text that the model is sum of two Gaussians, the standard deviation of a sum of these is square root of the two of them, maybe that’s what is meant in the text. The detector resolution can be asymmetrical and therefore needed to be modelled with something different than a Gaussian.

paper_title: Observation of charge-parity symmetry breaking in baryon decays
paper_excerpt: Observation of charge-parity symmetry breaking in baryon decays LHCb collaboration† Abstract The Standard Model of particle physics, the theory of particles and interactions at the smallest scale, predicts that matter and antimatter interact differently due to violation of the combined symmetry of charge conjugation (\( C \)) and parity (\( P \)). Charge conjugation transforms particles into their antimatter particles, while the parity transformation inverts spatial coordinates. This prediction applies to both mesons, which consist of a quark and an antiquark, and baryons, which are composed of three quarks. However, despite having been discovered in various meson decays, \( CP \) violation has yet to be observed in baryons, the type of matter that makes up the observable Universe. This article reports a study of the decay of the beauty baryon \( \Lambda_b^0 \) to the \( p K^- \pi^+ \pi^- \) final state and its \( CP \)-conjugated process, using data collected by the LHCb (Large Hadron Collider beauty) experiment at CERN. The results reveal significant asymmetries between the decay rates of the \( \Lambda_b^0 \) baryon and its \( CP \)-conjugated antibaryon, marking the first observation of \( CP \) violation in baryon decays, thus demonstrating the different behaviour of baryons and antibaryons. In the Standard Model, \( CP \) violation arises from the Cabibbo–Kobayashi–Maskawa mechanism, while new forces or particles beyond the Standard Model could provide additional contributions. This discovery opens a new path to search for physics beyond the Standard Model. Submitted
review_item: The manuscript’s headline percent-level CP-asymmetry result depends critically on cancellations between signal and control modes and on simulation-driven fit/efficiency corrections, but it does not report the key closure/robustness tests (e.g., post-correction control-mode null results and fit-bias/charge-dependent-shape stress tests) that would be expected to rule out detector/production effects at the few 0.1% level. "The otherwise excluded \( \Lambda_b^0 \to \Lambda_c^+ (\to p K^- \pi^+) \pi^- \) decay, which shares the same final state as the signal channel, instead comprises the control channel. The \( CP \) asymmetry in the \( \Lambda_b^0 \to \Lambda_c^+ \pi^- \) decay is expected to be negligible in the Standard Model, as it is dominated by the tree-level \( b \to c \bar{u} d \) transition, making it suitable for calibrating and cancelling nuisance asymmetries." This establishes that the core strategy is cancellation of production/detection biases using a control mode assumed to have negligible intrinsic CP violation. For such a strategy, a standard expectation is to show a clear null/closure result after *all* corrections, demonstrating that the full pipeline does not generate a spurious asymmetry when applied to the control channel. The manuscript quotes only the *raw* yield asymmetries of the control mode (not a final corrected asymmetry) and does not present a dedicated closure statement (e.g., corrected control asymmetry consistent with zero) in the main text. Because the reported global signal A_CP ~ 2.5% is not orders of magnitude larger than typical hadron-collider nuisance asymmetries, incomplete validation of the cancellation pipeline meaningfully affects confidence in the validity of the observation. This is particularly important because the nuisance-asymmetry corrections combine multiple external calibrations and pseudoexperiment-based uncertainties, where small mismodellings can coherently bias the result. "In the simultaneous fit, the same probability density function for each component is used for both baryon and antibaryon decays, with the yields allowed to differ between the two samples. The yield asymmetries obtained from the mass fits are affected by nuisance effects arising from asymmetries in the \( b \)-baryon production cross-section, as well as from the detection, reconstruction, and selection of final-state particles. Most of these effects cancel in the difference between the yield asymmetries of the signal and the control channels. Nevertheless, the difference in the \( \Lambda_b^0 \) or final-state kinematics between the signal and control channels leads to an incomplete cancellation of nuisance asymmetries." This passage highlights two separate, nontrivial assumptions that can generate false asymmetries: (i) the mass-model shapes are constrained to be identical for baryon and antibaryon for each component, and (ii) nuisance asymmetries are handled by a mostly cancel argument plus a residual correction. Both assumptions are plausible but are also precisely the kinds of places where collaborations typically report stability/closure checks (e.g., fit-bias studies with injected asymmetries, or allowing charge-dependent shape variations) because the target effect is only a few percent. The manuscript describes systematic variations of the mass fit model, but it does not explicitly connect these variations to a demonstrated absence of charge-dependent mass-shape biases. Likewise, it asserts incomplete cancellation due to kinematic differences, but does not show a final end-to-end check that the subtraction procedure eliminates detector-induced asymmetries in a mode expected to have zero CPV. Without these explicit closure demonstrations, readers cannot independently judge whether the claimed residual nuisance-asymmetry uncertainty fully covers the possible bias mechanisms. "Production asymmetries have the same experimental signature as direct CPV effects and are potentially much larger than the CP asymmetries to be determined." ([[6]](#ref6)) This statement from an LHCb production-asymmetry measurement underscores the general experimental risk at hadron colliders: production (and correlated detector) asymmetries can mimic direct CP violation unless convincingly controlled. The present manuscript’s approach (control channel plus kinematic weighting plus additional corrections) is aligned with this accepted framework, but the burden is then on clearly reported closure tests that demonstrate the residual is negligible in practice. Because the observed CP asymmetry is itself only at the few-percent level, a small unaccounted detector/production effect at the sub-percent level could materially shift the measured significance. In other LHCb measurements, such risks are often mitigated by reporting magnet-polarity splits, control-mode post-correction nulls, or additional independent control channels; analogous explicit demonstrations are not included here. The external quote supports why the absence of these checks is a major methodological gap rather than a cosmetic omission. References: [6] [LHCb Collaboration, Measurement of the D± production asymmetry in 7 TeV pp collisions, Phys. Lett. B 718 (2013) 902–909](https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/200208716.pdf)

paper_title: Observation of charge-parity symmetry breaking in baryon decays
paper_excerpt: Observation of charge-parity symmetry breaking in baryon decays LHCb collaboration† Abstract The Standard Model of particle physics, the theory of particles and interactions at the smallest scale, predicts that matter and antimatter interact differently due to violation of the combined symmetry of charge conjugation (\( C \)) and parity (\( P \)). Charge conjugation transforms particles into their antimatter particles, while the parity transformation inverts spatial coordinates. This prediction applies to both mesons, which consist of a quark and an antiquark, and baryons, which are composed of three quarks. However, despite having been discovered in various meson decays, \( CP \) violation has yet to be observed in baryons, the type of matter that makes up the observable Universe. This article reports a study of the decay of the beauty baryon \( \Lambda_b^0 \) to the \( p K^- \pi^+ \pi^- \) final state and its \( CP \)-conjugated process, using data collected by the LHCb (Large Hadron Collider beauty) experiment at CERN. The results reveal significant asymmetries between the decay rates of the \( \Lambda_b^0 \) baryon and its \( CP \)-conjugated antibaryon, marking the first observation of \( CP \) violation in baryon decays, thus demonstrating the different behaviour of baryons and antibaryons. In the Standard Model, \( CP \) violation arises from the Cabibbo–Kobayashi–Maskawa mechanism, while new forces or particles beyond the Standard Model could provide additional contributions. This discovery opens a new path to search for physics beyond the Standard Model. Submitted
review_item: The paper’s observation claim for the global CP asymmetry relies on a simple z-score (asymmetry divided by total uncertainty) rather than a likelihood-based hypothesis test that profiles systematic uncertainties, which is not sufficiently robust for an observation-level (5σ) statement. "The measured \( CP \) asymmetry, \( \mathcal{A}_{CP} = (2.45 \pm 0.46 \pm 0.10)\% \), with a significance of 5.2 standard deviations, reveals a difference in behaviour between baryonic matter and antimatter." This sentence ties the central scientific claim (first observation of CP violation in baryon decays) directly to a reported 5.2 significance. Because the claimed significance is only modestly above the canonical discovery threshold, the exact statistical procedure used to convert an asymmetry measurement into a p-value matters. In particular, the total uncertainty combines statistical and systematic components that may not be strictly Gaussian or independent (e.g., systematics derived from alternative fit models and pseudoexperiments). A z-score based on a point estimate and an aggregated uncertainty can be a useful summary, but it is not automatically equivalent to a proper hypothesis test under realistic nuisance-parameter models. Without a likelihood-based significance evaluation (or an explicit demonstration that the z-score approximation is well-calibrated here), the robustness of the observation claim is weaker than standard practice in high-stakes HEP measurements. "For the global \( \mathcal{A}_{CP} \), the baseline method to evaluate the significance is the \( z \)-score which is the \( \mathcal{A}_{CP} \) absolute value divided by its total uncertainty." This explicitly defines the significance calculation as a z-score, i.e., an approximate Gaussian test based on a single summary statistic and its uncertainty. The method assumes that the estimator distribution under the null is sufficiently normal and that the total uncertainty appropriately captures all nuisance effects in a way that maps cleanly to tail probabilities. However, the analysis pipeline includes nontrivial ingredients (sPlot weighting, external efficiency asymmetry calibrations, simulation-fixed fit parameters, and alternative-model systematics), which can induce non-Gaussian behavior or correlated uncertainties. In discovery/observation contexts, HEP convention typically prefers a profile likelihood ratio (or pseudoexperiment-calibrated test statistic) precisely to avoid miscalibrated tail probabilities. The manuscript does not provide evidence (e.g., toy-study calibration of the z-score under the full pipeline) that would justify treating the quoted 5.2 as a well-defined global p-value. "We describe likelihood-based statistical tests for use in high energy physics for the discovery of new phenomena and for construction of confidence intervals on model parameters. We focus on the properties of the test procedures that allow one to account for systematic uncertainties." ([[1]](#ref1)) Cowan et al. emphasize likelihood-based testing as the standard route for discovery claims, particularly because it provides a principled mechanism to incorporate systematic uncertainties as nuisance parameters. The present manuscript’s reliance on a z-score does not obviously implement this framework, and therefore it is not clear that the quoted standard deviations correspond to the same calibrated discovery metric used elsewhere in HEP. This matters most when the systematic model is complex or partially simulation-driven, as in this analysis. A likelihood-ratio approach would also naturally address correlations between nuisance components and the measured asymmetry, which a simple quadrature combination may not fully capture. The external reference supports the criticism that the chosen significance procedure is not aligned with common discovery-level statistical practice. References: [1] [G. Cowan, K. Cranmer, E. Gross, O. Vitells, Asymptotic formulae for likelihood-based tests of new physics (arXiv:1007.1727)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1007.1727)
annotator_comments: The comment is correct from statistical point of view, but the likelihood test cannot be done here due to how the parameters of interest are obtained.

paper_title: Observation of charge-parity symmetry breaking in baryon decays
paper_excerpt: Observation of charge-parity symmetry breaking in baryon decays LHCb collaboration† Abstract The Standard Model of particle physics, the theory of particles and interactions at the smallest scale, predicts that matter and antimatter interact differently due to violation of the combined symmetry of charge conjugation (\( C \)) and parity (\( P \)). review_item: The paper’s physical interpretation asserts that CP symmetry should hold in the \(\Lambda_b^0 \to R(p\pi^-)R(K^-\pi^+)\) topology because the tree amplitude does not contribute, yet the measured asymmetry in that region is non-negligible; the manuscript does not reconcile this tension (e.g., via look-elsewhere corrected significance or demonstrating consistency with residual systematics), weakening the reliability of the interpretation. "The \( \Lambda_b^0 \to R(p\pi^-)R(K^-\pi^+) \) decay involves a \( b \to d\bar{d}s \) transition, where the \( d \) quark forms the \( R(p\pi^-) \) baryon together with \( ud \) quarks from the initial \( \Lambda_b^0 \) baryon and the remaining \( \bar{d}s \) quarks forms the \( R(K^-\pi^+) \) meson. In this case, the tree amplitude does not contribute, and thus \( CP \) symmetry is expected to hold." This is a strong, testable theoretical statement embedded in the manuscript’s interpretation section. When such a topology is used as a quasi-null expectation, a measured nonzero asymmetry in that region becomes an important internal consistency check of the analysis. If the measured asymmetry is compatible with zero after accounting for multiple testing and systematic uncertainties, the paper should explicitly show that consistency to avoid confusion. Conversely, if the asymmetry remains notable, the statement CP symmetry is expected to hold may be too simplified for the selected mass window (which can still contain interfering contributions or residual tree pollution). The manuscript does not provide the quantitative reconciliation needed to support its interpretive narrative. "\( \Lambda_b^0 \to R(p\pi^-)R(K^-\pi^+) \)" and "(2.7 0.8 0.1)%" Numerically, this corresponds to a roughly 3 deviation from zero if one uses the quoted uncertainties in the same way the manuscript uses them elsewhere (i.e., dividing by the total uncertainty). Even if the authors later argue that this is not significant after accounting for the look-elsewhere effect across multiple regions, the paper does not report the corrected p-value or corrected significance for this specific region. Because the interpretation claims CP should hold here, leaving this apparent deviation unquantified is problematic: it can either indicate residual experimental bias in that region or reflect inadequacy of the simplified topology-level argument. Either way, the absence of a clear reconciliation weakens confidence in the data interpretation. Reporting the look-elsewhere corrected significance (and/or a dedicated systematic cross-check) for this null-expected region would materially improve the robustness of the conclusions. "When searching for a new resonance somewhere in a possible mass range, the significance of observing a local excess of events must take into account the probability of observing such an excess anywhere in the range. This is the so called \"look elsewhere effect\"." ([[2]](#ref2)) While Gross & Vitells discuss look-elsewhere effects for resonance searches, the underlying principle applies generally: when multiple correlated regions or selections are examined, local significances can be inflated relative to global significance. The manuscript acknowledges a look-elsewhere correction for the local \(\mathcal{A}_{CP}\) measurements, but it does not provide the corrected significance values for each region, including the topology where CP is expected to hold. Given the tension between expectation and the quoted central value, readers need the globally adjusted significance (or an explicit statement that the result becomes fully compatible with zero) to interpret this check correctly. Without this, the narrative risks appearing internally inconsistent: CP should hold is stated, yet a seemingly notable asymmetry is reported in the corresponding region. The external reference supports why the paper should explicitly quantify how multiple testing affects this apparent deviation. References: [2] [E. Gross, O. Vitells, Trial factors for the look elsewhere effect in high energy physics (arXiv:1005.1891)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1005.1891)
annotator_comments: What is being measured in Lb -> RR is not enough for an observation therefore the text is fine.

paper_excerpt: Observation of charge-parity symmetry breaking in baryon decays LHCb collaboration† Abstract The Standard Model of particle physics, the theory of particles and interactions at the smallest scale, predicts that matter and antimatter interact differently due to violation of the combined symmetry of charge conjugation (\( C \)) and parity (\( P \)). Charge conjugation transforms particles into their antimatter particles, while the parity transformation inverts spatial coordinates. This prediction applies to both mesons, which consist of a quark and an antiquark, and baryons, which are composed of three quarks. However, despite having been discovered in various meson decays, \( CP \) violation has yet to be observed in baryons, the type of matter that makes up the observable Universe. This article reports a study of the decay of the beauty baryon \( \Lambda_b^0 \) to the \( p K^- \pi^+ \pi^- \) final state and its \( CP \)-conjugated process, using data collected by the LHCb (Large Hadron Collider beauty) experiment at CERN. The results reveal significant asymmetries between the decay rates of the \( \Lambda_b^0 \) baryon and its \( CP \)-conjugated antibaryon, marking the first observation of \( CP \) violation in baryon decays, thus demonstrating the different behaviour of baryons and antibaryons. In the Standard Model, \( CP \) violation arises from the Cabibbo–Kobayashi–Maskawa mechanism, while new forces or particles beyond the Standard Model could provide additional contributions. This discovery opens a new path to search for physics beyond the Standard Model. Submitted
review_item: The manuscript attributes the observed CP violation to specific intermediate resonances based primarily on coarse phase-space region selections, but it simultaneously acknowledges strong overlap/interference and defers amplitude modeling; this is insufficient to justify the resonance-level causal conclusion. "Each decay topology receives multiple resonant or nonresonant contributions that often overlap and interfere with each other. The intrinsic \( CP \) asymmetry may vary in magnitude and could even change sign between different contributions." This statement correctly highlights that region-integrated asymmetries can be diluted (or even change sign) when multiple interfering amplitudes contribute within the same selected window. That observation undermines attempts to attribute the measured asymmetry to a particular resonance or subset of resonances unless an amplitude analysis disentangles the contributing components. In other words, a mass-window selection that is dominated by certain resonances does not uniquely identify which amplitude(s) carry the CP-violating phase difference. This is especially true in baryonic four-body decays where overlapping baryon and meson excitations are common and can populate the same kinematic region. As a result, the evidence presented in the manuscript is more consistent with localization of CPV in phase space than with pinpointing specific intermediate resonances as the cause. "An investigation of the amplitude structure of this decay is left for future studies." Deferring an amplitude analysis is a reasonable scope decision, but it constrains what can be concluded about mechanisms. Without an amplitude-level decomposition (or a model-independent amplitude extraction), the study cannot robustly connect the observed asymmetry to particular resonant subprocesses rather than to broader hadronic dynamics in that region. The manuscript nonetheless makes strong mechanistic language elsewhere (e.g., specific intermediate resonances play a key role), which is not supported by the presented level of analysis. A more cautious phrasing would be warranted: the results identify regions where CPV is enhanced, but do not yet determine which resonance(s) or interference patterns are responsible. The lack of amplitude analysis therefore weakens the causal interpretation. "A full amplitude analysis is the necessary step towards identifying the underlying mechanisms responsible for the observed ACP distribution." ([[5]](#ref5)) This LHCb workshop summary on multi-body decays explicitly states the community consensus that amplitude analysis is required to identify underlying mechanisms behind localized CP asymmetries. The manuscript’s approach of measuring asymmetries in hand-chosen mass regions is compatible with discovering where CPV is large, but it does not meet the bar described in the quote for identifying mechanisms. In multi-body decays, apparent resonance dominance in a projection does not uniquely determine the contributing amplitudes or their relative phases, and interference can shift both sign and magnitude locally. The external quote therefore directly supports the criticism that mechanistic resonance-level conclusions are premature without amplitude analysis. Aligning the manuscript’s claims with this standard would improve the reliability of its conclusions. References: [5] [J. H. Alvarenga Nogueira et al., Summary of the 2015 LHCb workshop on multi-body decays of D and B mesons (arXiv:1605.03889)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1605.03889)
annotator_comments: The comment is too vague of what is insufficient in the paper since there is some discussion on the resonant part.

This prediction applies to both mesons, which consist of a quark and an antiquark, and baryons, which are composed of three quarks. However, despite having been discovered in various meson decays, \( CP \) violation has yet to be observed in baryons, the type of matter that makes up the observable Universe. This article reports a study of the decay of the beauty baryon \( \Lambda_b^0 \) to the \( p K^- \pi^+ \pi^- \) final state and its \( CP \)-conjugated process, using data collected by the LHCb (Large Hadron Collider beauty) experiment at CERN. The results reveal significant asymmetries between the decay rates of the \( \Lambda_b^0 \) baryon and its \( CP \)-conjugated antibaryon, marking the first observation of \( CP \) violation in baryon decays, thus demonstrating the different behaviour of baryons and antibaryons. review_item: The manuscript’s analysis choices (simulation-fixed fit shapes, external calibration inputs, and pseudoexperiment-based uncertainties) are not documented in enough detail, and are not accompanied by reusable released artifacts, to enable independent reproduction or reanalysis of the key result. "The signal shape is modelled as a combination of a Gaussian and two Crystal Ball functions [49], all sharing the same peak position. The parameters of the signal function are determined from simulated events and are fixed in the fit to data, except for the Gaussian width, the average width of the Crystal Ball functions, and the peak position, which accounts for imperfections in the simulation." This shows that the extracted yields (and thus the asymmetry) depend on simulation-derived shape parameters that are not fully floated in the data fit. For an external reader, reproducing the measurement requires access not only to the data but also to the exact simulation configuration, the derived fixed parameter values, and the fitting implementation. The manuscript does not tabulate these fixed parameters or provide a machine-readable specification of the full likelihood model, making an independent reproduction infeasible even in principle. This is not merely a documentation issue: if fixed parameters shift yields differently for baryon and antibaryon due to subtle reconstruction differences, the asymmetry can be biased. Providing the parameter values and a compact statistical model description (or a public likelihood artifact) would significantly strengthen reproducibility and facilitate scrutiny. "The Durham High Energy Physics Database (HEPData) has been built up over the past four decades as a unique open-access repository for scattering data from experimental particle physics papers. It comprises data points underlying several thousand publications." ([[3]](#ref3)) This reference illustrates an established community mechanism for releasing the numerical content underlying published results in particle physics. For a paper making a historically significant first observation claim, depositing the key measurement tables (e.g., asymmetries, uncertainties, and possibly binned distributions needed for reinterpretation) in such repositories is a common and impactful practice. The manuscript does not indicate any such deposit or provide equivalent machine-readable artifacts for the measurement, which limits downstream re-use and independent cross-checking. While raw LHCb collision data may not be trivially shareable, the derived high-level results and auxiliary tables often are. The absence of an explicit artifact release therefore materially harms reproducibility under common open-science norms in HEP. "CERN today launched its Open Data Portal where data from real collision events, produced by experiments at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) will for the first time be made openly available to all." ([[4]](#ref4)) This CERN policy statement demonstrates that LHC data and accompanying tools can, in some circumstances, be released openly and in a reusable form. While not all analyses can be reproduced from open data today, the existence of such infrastructure raises the bar for transparency around what is (or is not) being shared for a particular result. The manuscript provides no data/code availability statement and no pointers to reusable analysis materials, leaving readers unable to assess what is needed to reproduce the published asymmetry. Even if full reproduction is restricted to the collaboration, providing analysis-level outputs (tables, simplified likelihoods, or open code for the statistical treatment) would enable partial independent validation. The external quote supports the expectation that major LHC results should, where feasible, provide clearer pathways for data and tool sharing. References: [3] [E. Maguire, L. Heinrich, G. Watt, HEPData: a repository for high energy physics data (arXiv:1704.05473)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1704.05473) [4] [CERN, CERN makes public first data of LHC experiments (Open Data Portal announcement)](https://home.cern/news/news/accelerators/cern-makes-public-first-data-lhc-experiments)
annotator_comments: It is true that these things are not documented in the paper, however they are intenally at CERN. It is not a practice to publish these as part of Nature papers so this comment is irrelevant.
